_metadata.description: Deterministic ingredient-level interaction rules keyed by canonical database identity
_metadata.structure_note: One entry per ingredient (or per form-scoped variant). All conditions, drug classes, and dose thresholds for that ingredient are in one object. As of v6.0.0, every sub-rule (condition_rules[], drug_class_rules[], dose_thresholds[], pregnancy_lactation block) carries an explicit profile_gate predicate that consumers MUST evaluate against the user profile before firing the alert.

v60_added_fields.profile_gate: deterministic predicate per sub-rule. See scripts/INTERACTION_RULE_SCHEMA_V6_ADR.md. Required field; consumers MUST evaluate the gate against (user_profile, product_context) before rendering an alert.

completed_migrations.summary: Path C authored safety copy fields + flutter_top_level_key invariant

Diabetes-rule clinical accuracy patch: removed ghost PMID 10479204 (psyllium); dropped beta-cell regeneration overclaim (gymnema); downgraded magnesium evidence (established → probable); upgraded aloe evidence (theoretical → limited) and scoped to oral; softened bitter melon plant-insulin-analog framing; reframed cinnamon evidence (probable → limited); downgraded garlic diabetes severity (caution → monitor); downgraded tribulus evidence (probable → limited, single-RCT note); added PMID 24438170 (fenugreek) and PMID 34467577 (gymnema); refreshed alert_headlines from generic 'Caution with diabetes' to specific phrasing. No schema fields added; backward-compatible.

Pregnancy-rule clinical accuracy patch: softened 5-HTP mechanism (removed organogenesis framing in favor of 'limited human pregnancy data + serotonergic pharmacology'); softened black seed oil mechanism (preclinical animal signal, not proven human risk); reframed butterbur mechanism to make PA-free vs raw distinction explicit (severity stays contraindicated as defensive default for unverified products); refreshed 10 generic 'Not recommended during pregnancy' headlines with reason-specific phrasing (hormone-active, uterine activity, FDA-advised, stimulant laxative, etc.). No schema fields added; backward-compatible.

Cross-condition headline variety pass: replaced 100 generic alert_headlines with reason-specific phrasing across 8 conditions. heart_disease (12): mechanism-specific (potassium/BP-rhythm/stimulant/clinician-review per dev review). bleeding_disorders (26): platelet/omega-3/coumarin/salicylate/heparin-like axes. kidney_disease (9): hyperkalemia, hypermagnesemia, electrolyte loss, creatinine, nephrotoxicity. thyroid_disorder (10): TPO inhibition, hormone modulation, lab interference. seizure_disorder (8): seizure-threshold, GLA, caffeine, cholinergic. liver_disease (14): hepatotoxic, HILI, cholestasis, hepatic clearance. hypertension (14): BP direction (raise/lower), med interactions. autoimmune (6): immunostimulating vs immunomodulatory. high_cholesterol (5): lipid mechanism. diabetes (9 residuals from 5.3.1). ttc (7): preconception care framing. surgery_scheduled (12) intentionally not changed — current headline is purpose-fit. No schema fields added; backward-compatible.

MAJOR: introduced profile_gate field on every sub-rule per scripts/INTERACTION_RULE_SCHEMA_V6_ADR.md. Migration was deterministic (no clinical judgment) and applied via scripts/tools/migrate_to_profile_gate.py. 557 profile_gate fields added across 145 rules: 210 condition_rules, 213 drug_class_rules, 73 dose_thresholds, 61 pregnancy_lactation blocks (84 rules with no_data pregnancy_lactation correctly skipped). Mappings: condition_id={pregnancy,lactation,ttc,surgery_scheduled} → profile_flag gate; other condition_id → condition gate; drug_class_id → drug_class gate; dose_thresholds with scope={condition,drug_class,profile_flag} → combination gate; pure dose thresholds → dose gate. Excludes blocks initialized empty; populated by hand in Step 5 (vitamin A nutrient-form, aloe topical, curcumin culinary, berberine medication-class escalation). Consumers (Flutter) MUST evaluate profile_gate against user profile + product context before rendering alerts; backend emits the gate but does NOT evaluate user profile.

v52_added_fields.informational_note: authored string per sub-rule, 40-120 chars (required when severity in {avoid, contraindicated})

condition_rules.mechanism: 7-Keto DHEA has hormonal activity concerns and is not established as safe during pregnancy.
condition_rules.action: Do not use during pregnancy unless directed by an obstetric specialist.
condition_rules.alert_body: 7-Keto DHEA has hormone-active properties and is not established as safe in pregnancy. If you are pregnant, talk to your obstetrician before using any product containing it.
condition_rules.informational_note: 7-Keto DHEA is hormonally active — relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.action: Do not use in pregnancy.
condition_rules.alert_body: Ephedra carries serious cardiovascular and neurologic risks and is FDA-banned as a supplement ingredient. If you are pregnant, do not use ephedra-containing products.
condition_rules.informational_note: Ephedra is an FDA-banned stimulant with cardiovascular risks relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

drug_class_rules.action: Avoid use with antihypertensives.

drug_class_rules.informational_note: Ephedra raises blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Ephedra is an FDA-banned stimulant with cardiovascular risks relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Synephrine is structurally similar to ephedrine, acts on adrenergic receptors, and significantly increases systolic and diastolic blood pressure, especially when combined with caffeine.
condition_rules.action: Avoid bitter orange / synephrine supplements. Commonly found in weight-loss and pre-workout products as an ephedra substitute.
condition_rules.alert_body: Bitter orange (synephrine) has ephedrine-like effects. If you have high blood pressure, talk to your clinician before using it, especially combined with caffeine.
condition_rules.informational_note: Bitter orange has stimulant effects relevant to people with high blood pressure.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Sympathomimetic effects directly counteract blood pressure-lowering therapy.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid combination with antihypertensive medications.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Bitter orange's stimulant effects can work against blood-pressure-lowering drugs. If you take antihypertensive medication, talk to your prescriber before using it.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Bitter orange is a stimulant — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Banned/recalled or high-risk substance — pregnancy default is contraindicated.

condition_rules.mechanism: Propylene glycol is metabolized to lactic acid via alcohol dehydrogenase and ~40-50% is renally excreted. Renal impairment reduces clearance, leading to accumulation and lactic acidosis.
condition_rules.action: Prefer products without propylene glycol when chronic kidney disease is present.
condition_rules.alert_body: Propylene glycol is cleared through the kidneys and may accumulate when kidney function is reduced. If you have kidney disease, talk to your nephrologist about safe intake of propylene glycol.
condition_rules.informational_note: Propylene glycol depends on kidney clearance — relevant to people with kidney conditions.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Oral aloe (gel/latex/whole leaf extract) has safety concerns and may be unsafe in pregnancy.
condition_rules.action: Avoid oral aloe supplements during pregnancy.
condition_rules.alert_headline: Oral aloe not recommended in pregnancy
condition_rules.alert_body: Oral aloe products (gel, latex, whole-leaf) raise safety concerns in pregnancy. If you are pregnant, talk to your obstetrician before using any oral aloe supplement.
condition_rules.informational_note: Oral aloe has safety concerns relevant to anyone pregnant.

condition_rules.mechanism: Limited human evidence suggests oral aloe vera may modestly reduce fasting glucose and HbA1c in people with type-2 diabetes; topical aloe is not implicated.
condition_rules.action: Applies to oral aloe (gel/latex/whole-leaf) only. Monitor glucose if combining oral aloe with diabetes medications. No alert for topical aloe.
condition_rules.alert_body: Oral aloe vera supplements may lower blood sugar; topical aloe does not. If you have diabetes and use oral aloe, talk to your clinician and monitor your glucose.
condition_rules.informational_note: Oral aloe affects blood sugar — relevant to people with diabetes.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Possible additive glucose-lowering. Oral aloe may also alter drug absorption via laxative effects.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor glucose and GI symptoms.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Aloe vera can lower blood sugar. If you take a diabetes medication, talk to your prescriber before adding aloe vera and monitor your glucose more closely at first.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Aloe vera affects blood sugar — relevant to people on diabetes medications.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: NCCIH indicates oral aloe may be unsafe in pregnancy and breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: This rule is intended for oral/systemic supplement use, not topical-only products.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_headline: Oral aloe not recommended in pregnancy

pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Oral aloe has safety concerns relevant to anyone pregnant.

condition_rules.mechanism: Enhances insulin-stimulated glucose uptake via GLUT4 translocation and reduces oxidative stress-mediated insulin resistance. May potentiate glucose-lowering effects of diabetes medications.
condition_rules.action: Monitor blood glucose when initiating ALA at doses >= 600 mg/day alongside diabetes medications.
condition_rules.alert_body: Alpha-lipoic acid can lower blood sugar. If you have diabetes, talk to your clinician before adding Alpha-lipoic acid and monitor your glucose, especially in the first weeks.
condition_rules.informational_note: Alpha-lipoic acid affects blood sugar — relevant to people with diabetes.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive insulin-sensitizing effect may increase hypoglycemia risk, especially with insulin and sulfonylureas.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor glucose and adjust medication only under clinician supervision.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Alpha-lipoic acid can lower blood sugar. If you take a diabetes medication, talk to your prescriber before adding Alpha-lipoic acid and monitor your glucose more closely at first.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Alpha-lipoic acid affects blood sugar — relevant to people on diabetes medications.

condition_rules.mechanism: Berberine activates AMPK, increases insulin sensitivity, and stimulates GLP-1 secretion. Produces glucose-lowering effects comparable to metformin. Additive hypoglycemia risk is clinically significant.
condition_rules.action: Do not combine with diabetes medications without physician oversight. Monitor blood glucose closely.
condition_rules.alert_headline: May lower blood sugar in diabetes
condition_rules.alert_body: Berberine lowers blood sugar with effects comparable to metformin. If you have diabetes, talk to your prescriber before adding berberine and monitor glucose closely.
condition_rules.informational_note: Berberine lowers blood sugar — relevant to people with diabetes.

condition_rules.mechanism: Berberine activates PCSK9 degradation, upregulates LDL receptors, and has demonstrated significant lipid-lowering effects in multiple RCTs, including reductions in total cholesterol, LDL-C, and triglycerides comparable to some statins. This makes berberine a candidate adjunct for hypercholesterolemia management.
condition_rules.action: Berberine may lower LDL cholesterol meaningfully. If combined with statins, monitor lipid panel and be alert to additive effects. Discuss with prescriber.
condition_rules.alert_body: Berberine can affect lipid metabolism. If you are managing high cholesterol, talk to your clinician before adding berberine — it may change your lipid-therapy plan.
condition_rules.informational_note: Berberine affects lipid pathways — relevant to people managing high cholesterol.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive glucose-lowering comparable to metformin. Also inhibits CYP3A4/CYP2D6, potentially increasing drug levels of co-administered medications.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid unsupervised co-administration. Requires clinician-guided glucose monitoring and potential dose adjustment.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Berberine lowers blood sugar with effects comparable to metformin. If you take a diabetes medication, monitor glucose closely and talk to your prescriber before adding berberine.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Berberine has blood-sugar-lowering effects relevant to people on diabetes medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Berberine inhibits CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein, which are primary metabolic and transport pathways for many statins (simvastatin, atorvastatin, lovastatin). Inhibition of CYP3A4 can increase plasma statin concentrations, potentially raising myopathy and rhabdomyolysis risk. Additive LDL-lowering also occurs.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid combining berberine with high-dose CYP3A4-metabolized statins without prescriber oversight. If combined, start with lower statin doses and monitor for muscle symptoms (myalgia, weakness, dark urine).
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Berberine shares muscle or liver pathways with statins. If you take a statin, talk to your prescriber before adding berberine and watch for new muscle aches.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Berberine affects statin pathways — relevant to people on statin therapy.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Berberine inhibits MAO-A and MAO-B activity in vitro, producing additive monoamine oxidase inhibition when combined with MAOI antidepressants (phenelzine, tranylcypromine, selegiline). This combined MAO inhibition can elevate serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine to toxic levels, increasing risk of serotonin syndrome (agitation, hyperthermia, clonus, autonomic instability) and hypertensive crisis. Additionally, berberine inhibits CYP2D6 and CYP3A4, reducing metabolic clearance of MAOIs.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid combining berberine with MAOIs. If transitioning from MAOI to berberine-containing supplements, observe a minimum 14-day washout. Alert prescriber to use.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Berberine has shown MAO-inhibiting activity in laboratory studies. If you take an MAOI antidepressant, talk to your prescriber before adding berberine.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Berberine has theoretical MAO-inhibiting activity relevant to people on MAOI antidepressants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: In-vitro CYP3A4 inhibition; clinical effect modest at typical doses (≥500 mg/day). Increasingly common in metabolic-health supplements.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take a CYP3A4-substrate prescription drug, talk to your prescriber before adding a berberine supplement.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Berberine can mildly slow the breakdown of certain prescription drugs. Mention berberine use to your prescriber if you take other medications.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Berberine inhibits CYP3A4 at clinical doses — relevant to anyone on prescription drugs metabolized by that enzyme.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Berberine (as in goldenseal) has documented neonatal bilirubin/kernicterus risk concerns.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid berberine-containing supplements during pregnancy and lactation.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_headline: Not recommended in pregnancy or breastfeeding
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Berberine carries neonatal bilirubin and kernicterus concerns. If you are pregnant or breastfeeding, do not use berberine-containing supplements unless specifically directed by your clinician.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Berberine has neonatal safety concerns — relevant to anyone pregnant, planning pregnancy, or breastfeeding.

dose_thresholds.note: Clinical trials typically use 1,000–1,500 mg/day (Yin 2008 PMID 18397984, Dong 2012 meta-analysis). This threshold starts the avoid alert at the upper end of the evidence-based dosing range. Below that range, prescriber discussion is still appropriate, but the glycemic-effect signal is less likely to justify an avoid-level alert on dose alone.

Additive hypoglycemia risk with sulfonylureas/insulin/GLP-1RAs is clinically plausible across the 1,000–1,500 mg/day evidence-based dosing band; this threshold starts the avoid alert at the upper end of that band. Sub-1500 mg/day use in compound formulations still warrants prescriber oversight (caution).

condition_rules.mechanism: Bitter melon contains compounds (charantin, polypeptide-p, vicine) that may influence glucose use and insulin signaling; clinical trials report fasting glucose reductions in some patients.

condition_rules.alert_body: Bitter melon can lower blood sugar. If you have diabetes, talk to your clinician before adding bitter melon and monitor your glucose, especially in the first weeks.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid during pregnancy and lactation.

condition_rules.mechanism: Black cohosh may have uterotonic/hormonal effects and is not considered safe in pregnancy.
condition_rules.action: Avoid use during pregnancy unless explicitly directed by obstetric care team.
condition_rules.alert_body: Black cohosh may have uterotonic and hormone-active effects. If you are pregnant, do not use black cohosh unless specifically directed by your obstetrician.
condition_rules.informational_note: Black cohosh is hormone-active — relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Cimicifuga racemosa (black cohosh) causes idiosyncratic hepatitis via two overlapping mechanisms: (1) oxidative stress generating 4-hydroxynonenal (4HNE) protein adducts in hepatocytes that act as autoantigens, triggering autoimmune troxis necrosis by T-lymphocytes; and (2) mitochondrial toxicity impairing beta-oxidation and membrane potential at clinical concentrations. In patients with pre-existing liver disease, these injury pathways are amplified by baseline hepatic dysfunction and reduced regenerative capacity, increasing risk of severe or fulminant outcomes.
condition_rules.action: Avoid black cohosh in patients with liver disease of any etiology. If a patient develops unexplained liver enzyme elevation while taking black cohosh, discontinue immediately and evaluate for DILI.
condition_rules.alert_body: Black cohosh has been linked to rare idiosyncratic liver injury. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician before using it, and monitor liver enzymes.
condition_rules.informational_note: Black cohosh has liver safety signals relevant to people with liver conditions.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Pregnancy safety is uncertain and potential adverse effects are described in safety guidance.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid in pregnancy; lactation evidence is limited.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Black cohosh may have uterotonic and hormone-active effects. If you are pregnant, do not use black cohosh unless specifically directed by your obstetrician.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Black cohosh is hormone-active — relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Caffeine crosses the placenta and higher daily intake is associated with pregnancy risk concerns.
condition_rules.action: Keep supplemental caffeine low and account for all dietary caffeine sources.
condition_rules.alert_headline: Caffeine adds up — watch total intake
condition_rules.alert_body: Caffeine crosses the placenta and passes into breast milk. If you are pregnant or nursing, monitor total caffeine from coffee, tea, chocolate, and supplements against a 200 mg/day ceiling.
condition_rules.informational_note: Caffeine crosses the placenta and reaches breast milk — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

condition_rules.mechanism: Caffeine causes acute blood pressure elevation (3-15 mmHg systolic) through adenosine receptor antagonism and sympathetic stimulation.
condition_rules.action: Limit caffeine-containing supplements. Monitor BP when starting caffeine supplements.
condition_rules.alert_body: Caffeine can influence blood pressure. If you have high blood pressure, talk to your clinician before adding caffeine and monitor your readings.
condition_rules.informational_note: Caffeine can affect blood pressure — relevant to people with high blood pressure.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Caffeine increases renal clearance of lithium → reduced levels and reduced efficacy. Withdrawal of caffeine raises levels → toxicity risk. Bidirectional risk: keep caffeine intake CONSISTENT, not just low.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take lithium, keep your daily caffeine intake steady. Sudden increases or decreases in caffeine can change your lithium levels.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Caffeine changes how your kidneys clear lithium. Big changes in coffee, tea, or caffeine pill use can swing lithium levels in either direction. Talk to your prescriber if your caffeine habits are c...
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Caffeine affects lithium clearance — relevant to anyone on lithium therapy.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: High caffeine exposure can affect maternal-fetal and infant outcomes.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: This threshold applies to supplemental caffeine only; total daily caffeine from beverages/foods should be considered.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_headline: Caffeine adds up — watch total intake
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Caffeine crosses the placenta and passes into breast milk. If you are pregnant or nursing, monitor total caffeine from coffee, tea, chocolate, and supplements against a 200 mg/day ceiling.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Caffeine crosses the placenta and reaches breast milk — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

condition_rules.action: Use with glucose monitoring if on diabetes treatment.
condition_rules.alert_body: Chromium can lower blood sugar. If you have diabetes, talk to your clinician before adding chromium and monitor your glucose, especially in the first weeks.
condition_rules.informational_note: Chromium affects blood sugar — relevant to people with diabetes.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Chromium picolinate can reduce levothyroxine absorption when taken concurrently. Divalent chromium binds to thyroid hormone molecules in the gastrointestinal tract.
drug_class_rules.action: Separate chromium supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Chromium can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing chromium away from your dose.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Chromium affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: No severe pregnancy-specific evidence at RDA-range doses; talk to your clinician before use, especially at higher doses.

pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: RDA-range standard nutrient — generally compatible with pregnancy and lactation; high-dose forms have separate evidence bases.

condition_rules.mechanism: Cinnamon supplements may modestly affect fasting glucose in some studies, but results are mixed and overall evidence does not clearly support a clinically meaningful effect. Product forms (Ceylon vs cassia) and doses also vary widely.

condition_rules.alert_headline: May affect glucose trends in some people
condition_rules.alert_body: Cinnamon supplements may slightly affect blood sugar in some people, but the evidence is mixed. If you have diabetes, talk to your clinician before relying on cinnamon and monitor your glucose.
condition_rules.informational_note: Cinnamon may modestly affect blood sugar — relevant to people with diabetes.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Coumarin content plus antiplatelet phytochemicals. Multiple case reports of INR elevation in patients on warfarin.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take warfarin or an antiplatelet, avoid dong quai unless cleared by your prescriber.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: If you take anticoagulants, do not combine with this product. Dong quai contains natural coumarins and antiplatelet compounds. Combined with warfarin, this can dangerously raise INR.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Dong quai has documented warfarin interaction — relevant to anyone on blood thinners.

condition_rules.mechanism: Galactomannan fiber delays carbohydrate absorption and 4-hydroxyisoleucine may stimulate insulin secretion. A meta-analysis (Neelakantan 2014) reported a fasting glucose reduction of approximately 0.96 mmol/L and HbA1c reduction of 0.85% across trials.
condition_rules.action: Additive blood sugar lowering with diabetes medications. Separate dosing from oral medications by 2 hours (fiber impairs drug absorption). Monitor glucose.
condition_rules.alert_headline: May lower blood sugar — monitor on diabetes meds
condition_rules.alert_body: Fenugreek can lower blood sugar. If you have diabetes, talk to your clinician before adding fenugreek and monitor your glucose, especially in the first weeks.
condition_rules.informational_note: Fenugreek affects blood sugar — relevant to people with diabetes.

condition_rules.mechanism: Fenugreek is one of the most widely used galactagogues globally. Proposed mechanism involves stimulation of sweat glands (structurally similar to mammary glands) via phytoestrogens, and possibly dopaminergic pathways affecting prolactin. While some women report increased milk volume, a 2022 Australian cross-sectional survey found widespread use with incomplete evidence of efficacy. Fenugreek's volatile compounds (diosgenin, 4-hydroxyisoleucine) are excreted in breast milk and may cause GI upset (colic, diarrhea, unusual maple-syrup body odor) in the nursing infant.
condition_rules.action: Fenugreek is widely used as a galactagogue but evidence for efficacy is inconsistent. If used, monitor nursing infant for GI symptoms (excessive gas, diarrhea, colic). Do not use in infants with maple syrup urine disease family history. Avoid doses >3g/day without lactation consultant guidance.
condition_rules.alert_body: Fenugreek has uterotonic activity and can lower maternal blood sugar. If you are pregnant, talk to your obstetrician; during breastfeeding, use under clinician guidance for milk-supply support.
condition_rules.informational_note: Fenugreek has uterotonic activity — relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive glucose-lowering and fiber-mediated delay of oral medication absorption.
drug_class_rules.action: Separate dosing from oral hypoglycemics by 2 hours. Monitor for hypoglycemia.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Fenugreek can lower blood sugar. If you take a diabetes medication, talk to your prescriber before adding fenugreek and monitor your glucose more closely at first.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Fenugreek affects blood sugar — relevant to people on diabetes medications.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Traditional concern for uterotonic effects. May also lower blood sugar in pregnant women on glucose management.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid during pregnancy.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Fenugreek has uterotonic activity and can lower maternal blood sugar. If you are pregnant, talk to your obstetrician; during breastfeeding, use under clinician guidance for milk-supply support.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Fenugreek has uterotonic activity — relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Tanacetum parthenium parthenolide inhibits platelet aggregation and has demonstrated uterotonic activity. Historical emmenagogue use is well-documented. Cochrane and ACOG recommend avoidance during pregnancy.
condition_rules.action: Do not use during pregnancy.
condition_rules.alert_body: Feverfew inhibits platelets and shows uterine-stimulating activity. If you are pregnant, do not use feverfew — Cochrane and ACOG both advise against it.
condition_rules.informational_note: Feverfew has uterotonic activity relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Parthenolide inhibits platelet aggregation via thromboxane synthase; additive bleeding risk with anticoagulant/antiplatelet therapy.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for increased bleeding tendency.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Feverfew has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding feverfew and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Feverfew can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Parthenolide crosses biological membranes and has uterotonic and anti-platelet effects. No safety data in lactation.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Feverfew is contraindicated in pregnancy per NCCIH and traditional classification as emmenagogue. Avoid during lactation due to insufficient safety data.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Feverfew inhibits platelets and shows uterine-stimulating activity. If you are pregnant, do not use feverfew — Cochrane and ACOG both advise against it.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Feverfew has uterotonic activity relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Viscous soluble fiber delays gastric emptying and slows glucose absorption. Meta-analysis of 6 RCTs showed significant reductions in FBG (-1.08 mmol/L) and 2h postprandial glucose.
condition_rules.action: Separate from oral diabetes medications by at least 2 hours (fiber delays drug absorption). Take with adequate water.
condition_rules.alert_body: Fiber can lower blood sugar. If you have diabetes, talk to your clinician before adding fiber and monitor your glucose, especially in the first weeks.
condition_rules.informational_note: Fiber affects blood sugar — relevant to people with diabetes.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Fiber delays absorption of co-administered oral medications including metformin and sulfonylureas.
drug_class_rules.action: Separate glucomannan from oral diabetes medications by at least 2 hours.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Fiber can lower blood sugar. If you take a diabetes medication, talk to your prescriber before adding fiber and monitor your glucose more closely at first.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Fiber affects blood sugar — relevant to people on diabetes medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: High dietary fiber intake adsorbs levothyroxine in the gut, reducing absorption. Psyllium and other supplemental fibers taken simultaneously with thyroid medication can significantly impair bioavailability.
drug_class_rules.action: Separate fiber supplements from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Fiber can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing fiber away from your dose.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Fiber affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Garlic can reduce platelet aggregation and increase bleeding tendency around procedures.
condition_rules.action: Stop before elective surgery unless clinician directs otherwise.
condition_rules.alert_body: Garlic can reduce platelet activity and raise bleeding risk around procedures. If you have surgery scheduled, talk to your surgeon about pausing garlic supplements.
condition_rules.informational_note: Garlic affects platelet function — relevant to anyone preparing for surgery.

condition_rules.mechanism: Garlic lowers blood pressure via hydrogen sulfide-mediated vasodilation and ACE inhibition (8-10 mmHg systolic). Additive effect with antihypertensives may cause symptomatic hypotension.
condition_rules.action: Inform prescriber if taking garlic supplements alongside BP medications. Monitor for dizziness or orthostatic hypotension.
condition_rules.alert_body: Garlic can influence blood pressure. If you have high blood pressure, talk to your clinician before adding garlic and monitor your readings.
condition_rules.informational_note: Garlic can affect blood pressure — relevant to people with high blood pressure.

condition_rules.mechanism: Allicin and other garlic organosulfur compounds inhibit HMG-CoA reductase and cholesterol synthesis. Meta-analyses of RCTs demonstrate modest but significant reductions in total cholesterol (approximately 17 mg/dL) and LDL-C. The effect is moderate and not sufficient as monotherapy for significant hypercholesterolemia.
condition_rules.action: Garlic supplements may contribute modest cholesterol-lowering. If used alongside lipid-lowering medication, monitor lipid panel. Not a substitute for prescribed therapy.
condition_rules.alert_body: Garlic can affect lipid metabolism. If you are managing high cholesterol, talk to your clinician before adding garlic — it may change your lipid-therapy plan.
condition_rules.informational_note: Garlic affects lipid pathways — relevant to people managing high cholesterol.

condition_rules.mechanism: Garlic supplements may reduce blood sugar to a small extent in people with diabetes; the glucose-only signal is modest, but additive effects are plausible with insulin or sulfonylureas.
condition_rules.action: Monitor for hypoglycemia if combining garlic supplements with insulin or sulfonylureas.
condition_rules.alert_body: Garlic supplements may modestly lower blood glucose and could add to insulin or sulfonylurea effects. If you take diabetes medication, watch for low blood sugar.
condition_rules.informational_note: Garlic can modestly affect blood sugar — relevant to people with diabetes.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Allicin/ajoene give garlic mild antiplatelet activity at supplement doses (≥600 mg/day extract). Standalone risk modest; severity escalates when combined with antiplatelets.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take warfarin or an antiplatelet, monitor for bruising or bleeding when adding a garlic supplement.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: Mild bleeding risk with blood thinners
drug_class_rules.alert_body: High-dose garlic supplements can mildly thin the blood. Combined with warfarin or aspirin-type drugs, this can increase bleeding risk.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Garlic supplements at high dose can affect platelet function — relevant to anyone on blood thinners.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Potential additive platelet inhibition.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for bruising or bleeding.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Garlic has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding garlic.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Garlic can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive blood pressure lowering may cause symptomatic hypotension.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor BP when combining garlic supplements with antihypertensive medications.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Garlic can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding garlic.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Garlic can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Garlic may weakly inhibit CYP3A4 and CYP2C9, potentially increasing plasma levels of some statins metabolized by these enzymes. Additionally, additive LDL-lowering effects may occur. The clinical magnitude of the pharmacokinetic interaction is considered modest but relevant at high garlic extract doses.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor lipid levels when combining garlic supplements with statin therapy. Report any muscle symptoms.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Garlic shares muscle or liver pathways with statins. If you take a statin, talk to your prescriber before adding garlic and watch for new muscle aches.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Garlic affects statin pathways — relevant to people on statin therapy.

dose_thresholds.note: Garlic supplements may have antiplatelet effects. Evidence is dose-dependent; caution is advised with anticoagulants. https://www.nccih.nih.gov/health/garlic

condition_rules.action: Use ginger supplements with caution in bleeding disorders. Monitor for bruising or prolonged bleeding.
condition_rules.alert_body: Ginger has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding ginger.
condition_rules.informational_note: Ginger can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Potential additive bleeding risk with anticoagulants.
drug_class_rules.action: Use with monitoring and clinician input.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Ginger has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding ginger and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Ginger can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for increased bleeding risk when combining ginger with antiplatelet drugs.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Ginger has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding ginger.

condition_rules.mechanism: Ginkgo biloba increases hepatic CYP450 activity, accelerating metabolism of calcium channel blockers (nicardipine studied) and reducing their antihypertensive effect.
condition_rules.action: Inform prescriber. Blood pressure may be less well-controlled. Dose adjustment may be needed.
condition_rules.alert_body: Ginkgo can influence blood pressure. If you have high blood pressure, talk to your clinician before adding ginkgo and monitor your readings.
condition_rules.informational_note: Ginkgo can affect blood pressure — relevant to people with high blood pressure.

condition_rules.mechanism: Ginkgo biloba seeds and insufficiently processed leaf products contain ginkgotoxin (4-O-methylpyridoxine), which inhibits GABA synthesis by depleting pyridoxal-5-phosphate cofactor, producing proconvulsant effects. Standardized 24% flavonoid / 6% terpene leaf extracts with ginkgotoxin removed are lower risk, but purity cannot be guaranteed from all manufacturers.
condition_rules.action: Use ginkgo with caution or avoid in seizure disorders. Use only standardized ginkgo leaf extract from reputable manufacturers. Monitor for increased seizure frequency.
condition_rules.alert_body: Ginkgo may affect seizure threshold in susceptible people. If you have a seizure disorder, talk to your neurologist before adding ginkgo.
condition_rules.informational_note: Ginkgo may affect seizure threshold — relevant to people with a seizure disorder.

condition_rules.mechanism: Ginkgolides (especially ginkgolide B) inhibit platelet-activating factor (PAF), reducing platelet aggregation and extending bleeding time. Multiple case reports of spontaneous hemorrhage including subdural hematoma.
condition_rules.action: Avoid ginkgo with bleeding disorders. If used, monitor for bruising, prolonged bleeding, or unexplained headache.
condition_rules.alert_body: Ginkgo reduces platelet function and has been reported in rare spontaneous bleeding events. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your clinician before use.
condition_rules.informational_note: Ginkgo affects platelet function — relevant to people with bleeding disorders.

condition_rules.mechanism: PAF inhibition by ginkgolides increases perioperative bleeding risk. Discontinue at least 2 weeks before scheduled surgery.
condition_rules.action: Discontinue ginkgo at least 14 days before surgery.
condition_rules.alert_body: Ginkgo increases bleeding risk around procedures. If you have surgery scheduled, discuss pausing ginkgo at least two weeks beforehand with your surgeon.
condition_rules.informational_note: Ginkgo affects platelet function — relevant to anyone preparing for surgery.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Increases metabolism of calcium channel blockers, reducing their plasma levels and antihypertensive effect.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor BP control when combining with calcium channel blockers.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Ginkgo can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding ginkgo.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Ginkgo can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Ginkgo inhibits platelet aggregation (PAF antagonism). Bleeding risk amplified with warfarin or antiplatelet therapy.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take warfarin, aspirin, or another antiplatelet/anticoagulant, avoid ginkgo unless your prescriber clears it.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: If you take anticoagulants, do not combine with this product. Ginkgo can thin the blood and may increase bleeding when combined with warfarin or antiplatelet medication.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Ginkgo has antiplatelet activity — relevant to anyone on blood-thinning medication.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive antiplatelet effect via PAF inhibition combined with COX/ADP pathway inhibition from aspirin/clopidogrel.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for increased bruising or bleeding. Inform prescriber.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Ginkgo has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding ginkgo.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Ginkgo can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Antiplatelet activity raises bleeding-risk concerns in pregnancy and labor.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Use caution in pregnancy due to bleeding-risk profile, especially around delivery.

dose_thresholds.note: Ginkgo's antiplatelet activity is dose-dependent; bleeding risks increase at ≥240 mg/day. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17085776/

condition_rules.action: Avoid routine use during pregnancy unless clinician-directed.

condition_rules.informational_note: Ginseng has limited pregnancy data — relevant to anyone pregnant, planning pregnancy, or breastfeeding.

condition_rules.mechanism: Ginsenosides improve insulin sensitivity and increase pancreatic beta-cell insulin secretion. RCTs show significant postprandial glucose reduction.
condition_rules.action: Monitor glucose levels when combining with diabetes medications.

condition_rules.informational_note: Ginseng affects blood sugar — relevant to people with diabetes.

condition_rules.mechanism: Ginseng may affect platelet aggregation and coagulation parameters. Case reports of reduced warfarin INR documented.
condition_rules.action: Stop ginseng at least 7 days before elective surgery. Inform surgeon.
condition_rules.alert_body: Ginseng can affect bleeding around procedures. If you have surgery scheduled, talk to your surgeon about pausing ginseng about two weeks before the procedure.
condition_rules.informational_note: Ginseng can affect perioperative bleeding — relevant to anyone preparing for surgery.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Mild additive glucose-lowering effect.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for hypoglycemia symptoms.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Ginseng can lower blood sugar. If you take a diabetes medication, talk to your prescriber before adding ginseng and monitor your glucose more closely at first.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Ginseng affects blood sugar — relevant to people on diabetes medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Mixed evidence — some studies show reduced INR (efficacy reduction); others null. Effect appears variable across products and individuals.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take warfarin, talk to your prescriber before using ginseng. INR may need re-checking after starting.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Panax ginseng has mixed evidence for changing how warfarin works. INR monitoring is recommended if you start a ginseng supplement.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Ginseng may interact with warfarin — relevant to anyone on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Multiple case reports of hypertensive episodes when combined with MAOIs (especially phenelzine). Evidence is thinner than M1-M5 but real.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take an MAOI, talk to your prescriber before using Panax or American ginseng.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: May raise blood pressure with MAOIs
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Panax ginseng combined with MAO-inhibitor antidepressants has been linked to high blood pressure in case reports.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Ginseng may interact with MAOIs — relevant to anyone on phenelzine or similar drugs.

dose_thresholds.note: Ginsenosides in high-dose ginseng may antagonize warfarin effects. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22137021/

condition_rules.mechanism: Goldenseal (berberine-containing) safety concern includes newborn bilirubin/kernicterus risk and should not be used in pregnancy.
condition_rules.action: Avoid goldenseal during pregnancy.

condition_rules.informational_note: Goldenseal has neonatal safety concerns relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitor in vitro and in vivo (berberine + hydrastine). Also affects CYP2D6.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take prescription drugs metabolized by CYP3A4 or CYP2D6, avoid goldenseal unless your prescriber clears it.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: Affects how many drugs are processed
drug_class_rules.alert_body: If you take cyp3a4 substrates, do not combine with this product. Goldenseal blocks two enzymes that break down many prescription drugs. This can raise drug levels and side-effect risk.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Goldenseal is a strong CYP3A4 and CYP2D6 inhibitor — relevant to anyone on prescription drugs metabolized by these en...

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Berberine and hydrastine inhibit CYP2D6 in addition to CYP3A4. CYP2D6 metabolizes a large class of psychiatric drugs (many SSRIs, tricyclics), opioids (codeine, tramadol — for activation), and tamoxifen.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take a CYP2D6-substrate prescription drug, avoid goldenseal unless your prescriber clears it. Especially relevant for codeine, tramadol, tamoxifen, paroxetine, and fluoxetine.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: Affects how many drugs are processed
drug_class_rules.alert_body: If you take cyp2d6 substrates, do not combine with this product. Goldenseal blocks the CYP2D6 enzyme that activates codeine and tramadol and metabolizes many antidepressants. This can change the ef...
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Goldenseal inhibits CYP2D6 in addition to CYP3A4 — relevant to anyone on antidepressants, opioids for pain, or tamoxi...

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid use in pregnancy and breastfeeding.

condition_rules.mechanism: Green tea catechins (EGCG) inhibit intestinal OATP transporters, reducing oral bioavailability of nadolol and potentially other beta-blockers, diminishing BP control.
condition_rules.action: Avoid taking green tea extract supplements close in time to beta-blockers. Separate doses by several hours.
condition_rules.alert_body: Green tea extract can influence blood pressure. If you have high blood pressure, talk to your clinician before adding green tea extract and monitor your readings.
condition_rules.informational_note: Green tea extract can affect blood pressure — relevant to people with high blood pressure.

condition_rules.mechanism: EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate), the principal catechin in green tea extract, undergoes hepatic first-pass metabolism. At supplemental doses (≥800 mg EGCG/day), EGCG saturates first-pass elimination and reaches hepatotoxic concentrations, inducing mitochondrial oxidative stress, hepatocellular pattern of liver injury, and transaminase elevations. The United States Pharmacopeia 2020 comprehensive review confirmed dose-dependent hepatocellular toxicity with adverse events reported from 140 mg to ~1,000 mg EGCG/day. Pre-existing liver disease impairs EGCG clearance and lowers the threshold for injury.
condition_rules.action: Avoid concentrated green tea extract supplements (standardized EGCG ≥400 mg/dose) in patients with liver disease. Brewed green tea is not implicated. Recommend liver function monitoring if use cannot be avoided.
condition_rules.alert_body: High-dose green tea extract has been linked to liver injury. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician before using concentrated EGCG supplements.
condition_rules.informational_note: Concentrated green tea extract has liver safety signals relevant to people with liver conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: EGCG inhibits OATP transporters, reducing beta-blocker absorption and potentially compromising BP control.
drug_class_rules.action: Separate green tea extract from beta-blocker dosing by at least 2-3 hours.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Green tea extract can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding green tea extract.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Green tea extract can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

dose_thresholds.note: EGCG hepatotoxicity threshold; at ≥800 mg EGCG/day first-pass saturation produces hepatocellular injury per USP 2020 review. Brewed green tea not implicated.

condition_rules.informational_note: Gymnema affects blood sugar — relevant to people with diabetes.

drug_class_rules.action: Monitor blood glucose closely when combining with diabetes medications.

condition_rules.mechanism: Hawthorn extract has mild antihypertensive properties via endothelium-dependent vasodilation and mild ACE-inhibitory activity.
condition_rules.action: Inform prescriber. Additive BP lowering may occur with antihypertensives.
condition_rules.alert_body: Hawthorn can influence blood pressure. If you have high blood pressure, talk to your clinician before adding hawthorn and monitor your readings.
condition_rules.informational_note: Hawthorn can affect blood pressure — relevant to people with high blood pressure.

condition_rules.mechanism: Hawthorn (Crataegus) has positive inotropic and mild chronotropic effects via phosphodiesterase inhibition and increased calcium sensitivity. It is used as an adjunct in mild heart failure (NYHA Class I-II). The SPICE trial (post-LVEF <35%) did not show mortality benefit. Hawthorn may potentiate or interact with cardiac glycosides via additive effects on cardiac contractility and the potential for P-glycoprotein interactions affecting digoxin pharmacokinetics.
condition_rules.action: Hawthorn may be used as an adjunct in mild heart failure under physician supervision. Inform cardiologist if taking alongside digoxin or other cardiac medications. Monitor for bradycardia or hypotension.
condition_rules.alert_headline: May add to heart medication effects
condition_rules.alert_body: Hawthorn can affect heart rate or rhythm in some people. If you have heart disease, talk to your cardiologist before adding hawthorn.
condition_rules.informational_note: Hawthorn can affect cardiovascular pathways — relevant to people with heart conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive blood pressure lowering from mild ACE-inhibitory and vasodilatory activity.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor BP regularly during concurrent use.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Hawthorn can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding hawthorn.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Hawthorn can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.
