condition_rules.mechanism: Iodine is essential in pregnancy, but excessive intake can adversely affect maternal/fetal thyroid function.
condition_rules.action: Keep total iodine intake within guideline ranges; avoid excess supplementation.
condition_rules.alert_body: Iodine is required during pregnancy, but excess can affect maternal and fetal thyroid function. If you are pregnant or nursing, monitor total iodine intake and talk to your clinician if in doubt.
condition_rules.informational_note: Iodine is dose-sensitive — both deficiency and excess matter for pregnancy and lactation.

condition_rules.mechanism: Excess iodine triggers the Wolff-Chaikoff effect, acutely suppressing thyroid hormone synthesis. Individuals with pre-existing thyroid disease (Hashimoto thyroiditis, Graves disease) are particularly susceptible to iodine-induced hypothyroidism or hyperthyroidism. Kelp/seaweed products contain highly variable iodine loads (50–500% of stated dose) and carry additional risk.
condition_rules.action: Avoid high-dose iodine or kelp/seaweed supplements in thyroid disorders without physician oversight. Routine supplementation above the RDA (150 mcg/day) is not recommended.
condition_rules.alert_body: Iodine can influence thyroid hormone levels. If you have a thyroid condition, talk to your endocrinologist before adding iodine and monitor your labs.
condition_rules.informational_note: Iodine affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people with thyroid conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: High iodine intake can alter thyroid physiology and therapy response.
drug_class_rules.action: Coordinate iodine supplements with thyroid medication management.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Iodine can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing iodine away from your dose.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Iodine affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Lithium is goitrogenic; supplemental iodine combined with lithium amplifies hypothyroidism risk. Both substances independently affect thyroid function. Iodine/kelp is common in 'thyroid support' formulas where users may not connect the dots.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take lithium, talk to your prescriber before using iodine, kelp, or bladderwrack supplements. Routine thyroid monitoring is important.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Lithium can suppress thyroid function. Iodine and kelp supplements compound this risk — read labels on 'thyroid support' formulas.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Iodine sources stack thyroid risk with lithium — relevant to anyone on lithium therapy.

dose_thresholds.note: NIH ODS adult Tolerable Upper Intake Level for iodine is 1100 mcg/day. Above this, thyroid dysfunction (both hypo- and hyper-thyroidism) risk increases via Wolff-Chaikoff effect or Jod-Basedow phenomenon. Parallels the pregnancy-excess threshold. Clinical-team signoff 2026-04-24.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Both deficiency and excess can be harmful; UL and recommended intake should guide use.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Threshold escalates only on excess; baseline reflects required-but-dose-sensitive nutrient status.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Iodine is required during pregnancy, but excess can affect maternal and fetal thyroid function. If you are pregnant or nursing, monitor total iodine intake and talk to your clinician if in doubt.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Iodine is dose-sensitive — both deficiency and excess matter for pregnancy and lactation.

condition_rules.mechanism: The liver is the principal organ of iron storage and regulation. In liver disease, hepatic hepcidin production is impaired, disrupting iron homeostasis and increasing intestinal iron absorption. Iron supplementation in this context risks exacerbating hepatic iron overload, which drives oxidative stress via the Fenton reaction (Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ → Fe³⁺ + OH⁻ + •OH), lipid peroxidation, and progressive liver fibrosis. This mechanism is most critical in hereditary hemochromatosis and alcoholic liver disease, where iron accumulation is already elevated, but applies broadly to chronic liver disease including NAFLD/NASH, hepatitis C, and cirrhosis.
condition_rules.action: Avoid routine iron supplementation in liver disease patients unless iron deficiency anemia is confirmed by labs (serum iron, ferritin, TIBC). Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration. Monitor ferritin and transferrin saturation regularly.
condition_rules.alert_headline: May worsen iron overload in liver disease
condition_rules.alert_body: Iron is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of iron.
condition_rules.informational_note: Iron depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Iron chelates with methyldopa and certain ACE inhibitors (captopril) in the GI tract, reducing drug absorption and bioavailability.
drug_class_rules.action: Separate iron supplement from methyldopa or captopril by at least 2 hours.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Iron can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding iron.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Iron can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Ferrous sulfate and other iron salts form stable insoluble complexes with levothyroxine, reducing thyroid hormone absorption. Concurrent ingestion can significantly impair replacement therapy efficacy.
drug_class_rules.action: Separate iron supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Iron can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing iron away from your dose.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Iron affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: L-arginine increases nitric oxide production, causing vasodilation and BP reduction (5-7 mmHg systolic). Additive effect with antihypertensives risks excessive hypotension.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution alongside antihypertensives. Start low and monitor BP.
condition_rules.alert_body: L-arginine can influence blood pressure. If you have high blood pressure, talk to your clinician before adding L-arginine and monitor your readings.
condition_rules.informational_note: L-arginine can affect blood pressure — relevant to people with high blood pressure.

condition_rules.mechanism: L-arginine is the substrate for nitric oxide synthase (NOS), producing NO which causes vasodilation. While this may benefit endothelial function in heart failure or coronary artery disease, the VINTAGE MI trial (post-MI patients) found unexpected excess deaths in the L-arginine group (6 vs 0 in placebo), leading to early termination. The mechanism may involve arrhythmogenic properties of NO at high concentrations in the post-infarction myocardium.
condition_rules.action: Avoid L-arginine supplementation in the immediate post-myocardial infarction period. Use with caution and only under cardiologist supervision in other heart disease settings.
condition_rules.alert_headline: May affect blood pressure and heart healing
condition_rules.alert_body: L-arginine can affect heart rate or rhythm in some people. If you have heart disease, talk to your cardiologist before adding L-arginine.
condition_rules.informational_note: L-arginine can affect cardiovascular pathways — relevant to people with heart conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive vasodilatory effect may enhance hypotensive action beyond intended levels.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for dizziness or lightheadedness. Discuss with prescriber.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: L-arginine can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding L-arginine.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: L-arginine can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Glycyrrhizin can increase sodium retention and blood pressure. NOTE: DGL (deglycyrrhizinated licorice) has glycyrrhizin removed and does NOT have this interaction. This rule applies only to whole/standard licorice extract containing glycyrrhizin.
condition_rules.action: Avoid in uncontrolled blood pressure unless deglycyrrhizinated form is confirmed.
condition_rules.alert_body: Whole licorice (with glycyrrhizin) can raise blood pressure through sodium retention. If you have high blood pressure, talk to your clinician; DGL licorice does not carry this concern.
condition_rules.informational_note: Licorice with glycyrrhizin raises blood pressure — relevant to people with hypertension.

condition_rules.mechanism: High glycyrrhizin intake inhibits placental 11β-HSD2 and has been associated with shorter gestation and higher preterm risk.
condition_rules.action: Avoid high-dose licorice supplements in pregnancy.
condition_rules.alert_body: Whole licorice's glycyrrhizin can affect fetal cortisol exposure and is associated with shorter gestation. If you are pregnant, talk to your obstetrician; DGL licorice does not carry this concern.
condition_rules.informational_note: Licorice with glycyrrhizin affects fetal cortisol exposure — relevant in pregnancy.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: May blunt blood pressure control from antihypertensive therapy. Does not apply to DGL (deglycyrrhizinated) licorice.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor blood pressure and review supplement use with clinician.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Licorice can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding licorice.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Licorice can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Glycyrrhizin inhibits placental 11-beta-HSD2, allowing excess maternal cortisol to reach the fetus. Finnish cohort studies documented >2-fold preterm delivery risk and neurodevelopmental harm (IQ reduction, ADHD risk) with heavy glycyrrhizin exposure.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid during pregnancy. Even confectionery licorice at sustained intake poses risk.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Whole licorice's glycyrrhizin can raise maternal cortisol reaching the fetus, linked to preterm delivery. If you are pregnant, talk to your obstetrician; DGL licorice does not carry this concern.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Licorice with glycyrrhizin affects fetal cortisol exposure — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

dose_thresholds.note: 2000 mg of typical commercial licorice extract (4-5% glycyrrhizin) ≈ 100 mg glycyrrhizic acid, the threshold above which BP elevation becomes clinically significant. DGL is exempt. https://www.nccih.nih.gov/health/licorice-root

condition_rules.mechanism: Magnesium acts as a natural calcium channel blocker, producing modest BP reductions (2-4 mmHg systolic). Additive effect with pharmaceutical calcium channel blockers may enhance hypotensive action.
condition_rules.action: Generally safe at recommended doses. Inform prescriber if supplementing alongside calcium channel blockers. Monitor for dizziness.
condition_rules.alert_body: Magnesium can influence blood pressure. If you have high blood pressure, talk to your clinician before adding magnesium and monitor your readings.
condition_rules.informational_note: Magnesium can affect blood pressure — relevant to people with high blood pressure.

condition_rules.mechanism: Magnesium plays a critical role in cardiac electrophysiology, stabilizing cardiac membranes and modulating ion channels. Hypomagnesemia is associated with cardiac arrhythmias. However, in patients with heart disease who also have renal impairment (common comorbidity), magnesium supplements can accumulate and cause hypermagnesemia, leading to bradycardia, cardiac conduction block, and potentially cardiac arrest.
condition_rules.action: Use magnesium supplements with caution in heart disease patients, especially those with reduced kidney function. Do not exceed recommended doses without renal function assessment. Monitor for signs of hypermagnesemia (weakness, bradycardia).
condition_rules.alert_body: Magnesium can affect heart rate or rhythm in some people. If you have heart disease, talk to your cardiologist before adding magnesium.
condition_rules.informational_note: Magnesium can affect cardiovascular pathways — relevant to people with heart conditions.

condition_rules.mechanism: Impaired renal magnesium excretion in CKD (especially eGFR <30) leads to hypermagnesemia accumulation. Supplemental magnesium doses safe in healthy adults can cause life-threatening hypermagnesemia in CKD patients (cardiac arrest, respiratory failure).
condition_rules.action: Avoid magnesium supplementation in severe CKD (eGFR <30) without nephrologist guidance. In mild-moderate CKD, use with caution and monitor serum Mg levels.
condition_rules.alert_body: In advanced kidney disease (eGFR below 30), supplemental magnesium can build up to risky levels. If you have CKD, talk to your nephrologist before adding magnesium.
condition_rules.informational_note: Magnesium excretion depends on kidney function — relevant to people with kidney disease.

condition_rules.mechanism: Higher dietary magnesium intake is associated with lower type-2 diabetes risk in observational data, and supplementation may modestly support insulin sensitivity, but trial evidence for an interaction with diabetes medications is not established.
condition_rules.action: Track glucose and tolerance if using high doses.
condition_rules.alert_body: Magnesium may modestly support insulin sensitivity, but high doses can cause GI losses and electrolyte issues. If you use it with diabetes, track glucose and tolerance.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive blood pressure lowering from natural calcium channel blocking activity.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor BP when supplementing alongside antihypertensives.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Magnesium can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding magnesium.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Magnesium can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Divalent magnesium cations can bind levothyroxine and impair gastrointestinal absorption, similar to other divalent mineral-levothyroxine chelation interactions.
drug_class_rules.action: Separate magnesium supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Magnesium can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing magnesium away from your dose.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Magnesium affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: May reduce lithium absorption when co-ingested. Separate doses by 2 hours.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take lithium, separate magnesium supplements from your lithium dose by at least 2 hours.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: High-dose magnesium taken at the same time as lithium may reduce absorption. Take them at least 2 hours apart.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Magnesium can affect lithium absorption — relevant to anyone on lithium therapy.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: No severe pregnancy-specific evidence at RDA-range doses; talk to your clinician before use, especially at higher doses.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: At RDA-range doses, this nutrient has no specific pregnancy or lactation concerns. Higher therapeutic doses may have a different safety profile — talk to your clinician.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: RDA-range standard nutrient — generally compatible with pregnancy and lactation; high-dose forms have separate evidence bases.

dose_thresholds.note: NIH ODS upper limit for supplemental magnesium is 350 mg/day. https://ods.od.nih.gov/factsheets/Magnesium-HealthProfessional/

condition_rules.mechanism: Potassium supplements combined with ACE inhibitors, ARBs, or potassium-sparing diuretics can cause life-threatening hyperkalemia.
condition_rules.action: Do not take potassium supplements without physician approval if on ACE inhibitors, ARBs, or potassium-sparing diuretics.
condition_rules.alert_headline: May raise potassium with BP meds
condition_rules.alert_body: Potassium can influence blood pressure. If you have high blood pressure, talk to your clinician before adding potassium and monitor your readings.
condition_rules.informational_note: Potassium can affect blood pressure — relevant to people with high blood pressure.

condition_rules.mechanism: Impaired renal potassium excretion greatly increases hyperkalemia risk with supplemental potassium.
condition_rules.action: Avoid potassium supplements with kidney disease unless directed by nephrologist.
condition_rules.alert_body: Supplemental potassium can accumulate to dangerous levels in kidney disease. If you have CKD, talk to your nephrologist before using potassium supplements.
condition_rules.informational_note: Potassium excretion depends on kidney function — relevant to people with kidney disease.

condition_rules.mechanism: Heart failure patients are frequently treated with ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and/or aldosterone antagonists (spironolactone, eplerenone), all of which reduce renal potassium excretion. Adding supplemental potassium to these regimens substantially increases the risk of life-threatening hyperkalemia (>6.0 mEq/L), which can cause ventricular arrhythmias and cardiac arrest.
condition_rules.action: Do not take potassium supplements without physician approval and serum potassium monitoring if on ACE inhibitors, ARBs, or potassium-sparing diuretics. This is especially critical in heart failure management.
condition_rules.alert_headline: May raise potassium with heart medications
condition_rules.alert_body: Potassium can affect heart rate or rhythm in some people. If you have heart disease, talk to your cardiologist before adding potassium.
condition_rules.informational_note: Potassium can affect cardiovascular pathways — relevant to people with heart conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: ACE inhibitors and ARBs reduce aldosterone, decreasing renal potassium excretion. Adding exogenous potassium raises serum K+ to dangerous levels.
drug_class_rules.action: Requires serum potassium monitoring. Do not supplement without clinician supervision.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Potassium can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding potassium.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Potassium can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: No severe pregnancy-specific evidence at RDA-range doses; talk to your clinician before use, especially at higher doses.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: At RDA-range doses, this nutrient has no specific pregnancy or lactation concerns. Higher therapeutic doses may have a different safety profile — talk to your clinician.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: RDA-range standard nutrient — generally compatible with pregnancy and lactation; high-dose forms have separate evidence bases.

condition_rules.mechanism: Soluble viscous fiber delays gastric emptying and slows carbohydrate absorption, which can reduce postprandial glucose spikes.
condition_rules.action: Separate dosing from oral diabetes medications by at least 2 hours (fiber delays drug absorption). Monitor glucose.
condition_rules.alert_body: Psyllium can lower post-meal blood sugar and may delay absorption of oral diabetes medications. If you have diabetes, talk to your clinician and monitor glucose, especially in the first weeks.
condition_rules.informational_note: Psyllium affects blood sugar — relevant to people with diabetes.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Fiber delays absorption of co-administered oral medications including metformin and sulfonylureas.
drug_class_rules.action: Separate psyllium from oral diabetes medications by at least 2 hours.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Psyllium can lower blood sugar. If you take a diabetes medication, talk to your prescriber before adding psyllium and monitor your glucose more closely at first.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Psyllium affects blood sugar — relevant to people on diabetes medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Reduces lithium absorption when taken concurrently. Separate dosing by 1-2 hours.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take lithium, separate psyllium and high-fiber supplements from your lithium dose by at least 2 hours.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Psyllium and other high-fiber supplements can reduce how much lithium your body absorbs if taken at the same time. Space them apart by 2 hours.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Psyllium can interfere with lithium absorption — relevant to anyone on lithium.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: NCCIH notes potential increased birth-defect risk and major drug interaction potential.
condition_rules.action: Avoid St. John’s wort during pregnancy unless specialist-supervised.
condition_rules.alert_body: St John's Wort has been flagged for possible birth-defect risk and major drug interactions. If you are pregnant, talk to a specialist before any use.
condition_rules.informational_note: St John's Wort has pregnancy safety concerns relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: St. John's Wort induces CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein, reducing plasma levels of calcium channel blockers (nifedipine, amlodipine, verapamil) and potentially other antihypertensives.
condition_rules.action: Avoid if taking calcium channel blockers or other CYP3A4-metabolized BP medications.
condition_rules.alert_body: St John's Wort can influence blood pressure. If you have high blood pressure, talk to your clinician before adding St John's Wort and monitor your readings.
condition_rules.informational_note: St John's Wort can affect blood pressure — relevant to people with high blood pressure.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Potent CYP3A4/P-gp induction reduces plasma levels of calcium channel blockers and other CYP3A4-metabolized antihypertensives, compromising BP control.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid combination. If SJW is discontinued, BP medications may suddenly become more potent, risking hypotension.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: St John's Wort speeds up drug metabolism via CYP3A4 and can lower levels of blood-pressure drugs. If you take antihypertensive medication, talk to your prescriber before use.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: St John's Wort induces CYP3A4 — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: CYP3A4 induction reduces warfarin S-enantiomer exposure → loss of anticoagulation. Documented thrombotic events including strokes and pulmonary emboli.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not combine St. John's Wort with warfarin or other vitamin-K antagonists. Discuss alternatives with your prescriber.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: If you take anticoagulants, do not combine with this product. St. John's Wort speeds up the breakdown of warfarin, which can stop it from working and lead to dangerous blood clots.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: St. John's Wort is a strong CYP3A4 inducer — relevant to anyone on warfarin or many other prescription drugs.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: CYP3A4 induction (opposite mechanism to grapefruit) → reduced exposure to tacrolimus, cyclosporine → therapy failure and transplant rejection.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not combine St. John's Wort with transplant immunosuppressants.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: Do not combine with transplant drugs
drug_class_rules.alert_body: If you take immunosuppressants, do not combine with this product. St. John's Wort speeds up the breakdown of tacrolimus and cyclosporine. This can cause transplant rejection.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: St. John's Wort is a strong CYP3A4 inducer — opposite mechanism to grapefruit (drug failure, not toxicity).

drug_class_rules.mechanism: CYP3A4 induction reduces plasma levels of simvastatin, atorvastatin, and other CYP3A4-metabolized statins.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid with CYP3A4-metabolized statins or monitor lipid levels closely.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: St John's Wort shares muscle or liver pathways with statins. If you take a statin, talk to your prescriber before adding St John's Wort and watch for new muscle aches.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: St John's Wort affects statin pathways — relevant to people on statin therapy.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: CYP3A4 induction reduces levels of benzodiazepines (alprazolam, midazolam). Also risk of serotonin syndrome if combined with SSRIs/SNRIs.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid unsupervised combination with sedatives or antidepressants.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: St John's Wort has mild sedative effects. If you take a sedative or sleep medication, talk to your prescriber before adding St John's Wort — the combination can increase drowsiness.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: St John's Wort has sedative activity — relevant to people on sedatives or sleep medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: St John's Wort increases serotonin levels via monoamine reuptake inhibition. Combined with SSRIs/SNRIs, this causes additive serotonergic stimulation leading to serotonin syndrome — a potentially fatal toxidrome (hyperthermia, clonus, agitation, seizures). Multiple case reports and pharmacovigilance alerts.
drug_class_rules.action: Do NOT combine St John's Wort with any SSRI or SNRI. If switching, allow 2-week washout. This is a life-threatening interaction.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: Do not combine with SSRIs or SNRIs
drug_class_rules.alert_body: St John's Wort raises serotonin activity. If you take an SSRI or SNRI antidepressant, do not combine — the combination can cause serotonin syndrome.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: St John's Wort raises serotonin — relevant to people on SSRI or SNRI antidepressants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Hypericin has weak MAO-A inhibition; combination duplicates mechanism → serotonin syndrome and hypertensive risk.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not combine St. John's Wort with MAOIs.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: If you take MAO inhibitors, do not combine with this product. St. John's Wort has its own MAO-inhibiting activity. Combined with prescription MAOIs, this can cause serotonin syndrome or dangerous b...
drug_class_rules.informational_note: St. John's Wort has serotonergic and MAO-A activity — relevant to anyone on MAOIs.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: CYP3A4 induction reduces ethinyl estradiol exposure → contraception failure and breakthrough bleeding documented in clinical literature.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not rely on oral contraceptives while using St. John's Wort. Use additional non-hormonal contraception or discontinue St. John's Wort.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: If you take oral contraceptives, do not combine with this product. St. John's Wort lowers the level of birth-control pills in your blood and can lead to unintended pregnancy.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: St. John's Wort reduces oral contraceptive efficacy.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: At ≥900 mg/day standardized extract, SJW has measurable CYP2D6 effect — the direction is mixed across studies (some induction, some weak inhibition reported). This is distinct from SJW's dramatic and consistent CYP3A4 induction (see contraindicated rule with CYP3A4-substrate drugs). Effect at typical lower doses is not clinically significant.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take a CYP2D6-substrate prescription drug at high-dose St. John's Wort (≥900 mg/day), your prescriber may want to monitor for changes in drug effect. The CYP3A4 interaction at any dose is the dominant clinical concern — see the existing contraindication.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: If you take a CYP2D6-substrate prescription drug, your prescriber may want to monitor for changes in drug effect at high-dose St. John's Wort (≥900 mg/day).
drug_class_rules.informational_note: SJW at ≥900 mg/day has mixed-direction CYP2D6 effect — distinct from its dominant CYP3A4 induction.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Potential fetal risk and broad CYP/P-gp interactions.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Use in pregnancy should be avoided outside specialist care.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: St John's Wort has been flagged for possible birth-defect risk and major drug interactions. If you are pregnant, talk to a specialist before any use.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: St John's Wort has pregnancy safety concerns relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Vanadium is insulin-mimetic: activates insulin receptor substrate, inhibits PTP-1B, and reduces hepatic glucose production. Clinical studies showed FBG and HbA1c reductions at 150 mg/day.
condition_rules.action: Additive hypoglycemia risk with insulin and oral hypoglycemics. GI side effects common. Use only under medical supervision.
condition_rules.alert_body: Vanadium can lower blood sugar. If you have diabetes, talk to your clinician before adding vanadium and monitor your glucose, especially in the first weeks.
condition_rules.informational_note: Vanadium affects blood sugar — relevant to people with diabetes.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Insulin-mimetic activity creates additive hypoglycemia risk. Narrow therapeutic window.
drug_class_rules.action: Use only under clinician supervision with glucose monitoring.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Vanadium can lower blood sugar. If you take a diabetes medication, talk to your prescriber before adding vanadium and monitor your glucose more closely at first.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Vanadium affects blood sugar — relevant to people on diabetes medications.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Limited safety data and insulin-mimetic activity present risk concerns.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid during pregnancy and lactation.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_headline: Not recommended in pregnancy or breastfeeding
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Vanadyl sulfate has insulin-mimetic activity with limited human safety data. If you are pregnant or breastfeeding, do not use vanadium supplements unless directed by your clinician.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Vanadium has limited pregnancy data — relevant to anyone pregnant, planning pregnancy, or breastfeeding.

dose_thresholds.note: Supplement-range vanadium is 10–100 mcg; pharmacologic insulin-mimetic effects documented in clinical studies at 150 mg/day. A 50 mg cutoff reliably separates trace multivitamin exposure from the pharmacologic band. Clinical-team signoff 2026-04-24: use >= to include the first research-level dose in the caution tier.

Supplement-range vanadium is 10–100 mcg; pharmacologic insulin-mimetic effects documented in clinical studies at 150 mg/day. A 50 mg cutoff reliably separates trace multivitamin exposure from the pharmacologic band. Clinical-team signoff 2026-04-24: use >= to include the first research-level dose in the caution tier.

form_scope: vitamin A from cod liver oil

condition_rules.mechanism: Excess preformed vitamin A intake in pregnancy is associated with teratogenic risk.
condition_rules.action: Avoid high-dose preformed vitamin A in pregnancy unless clinician-directed.
condition_rules.alert_headline: Keep preformed vitamin A within prenatal range
condition_rules.alert_body: Preformed vitamin A (retinol) is dose-sensitive in pregnancy; beta-carotene is not. If you are pregnant or nursing, monitor total preformed retinol intake from all sources and talk to your clinician.
condition_rules.informational_note: Preformed retinol is dose-sensitive — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

condition_rules.mechanism: Retinol (preformed vitamin A) is stored in hepatic stellate cells (Ito cells) as retinyl esters. Chronic liver disease impairs retinol clearance and stellate cell function, causing accumulation even at doses below standard UL. Hypervitaminosis A induces stellate cell hypertrophy and proliferation, leading to progressive fibrosis, non-cirrhotic portal hypertension, and eventual cirrhosis. CYP2E1 induction (common in alcoholic liver disease) enhances retinol hepatotoxicity via microsomal oxidative activation. Clinically relevant injury has been reported at supplemental doses ≥25,000 IU/day in individuals with underlying liver disease.
condition_rules.action: Limit supplemental preformed vitamin A (retinol/retinyl esters) to ≤5,000 IU/day in patients with liver disease. Beta-carotene is preferred. Monitor LFTs and clinical signs of hypervitaminosis (hepatomegaly, ascites). Avoid multivitamins with high retinol doses.
condition_rules.alert_body: Vitamin A is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of Vitamin A.
condition_rules.informational_note: Vitamin A depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Dose and form dependent risk is primarily tied to preformed retinoids.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Applies to preformed vitamin A forms; carotenoids are not handled by this threshold rule.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_headline: Keep preformed vitamin A within prenatal range
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Preformed vitamin A (retinol) is dose-sensitive in pregnancy; beta-carotene is not. If you are pregnant or nursing, monitor total preformed retinol intake from all sources and talk to your clinician.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Preformed retinol is dose-sensitive — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

condition_rules.mechanism: At pharmacologic doses (>=1g/day), niacin increases hepatic glucose output and decreases insulin sensitivity. FPG increases of 4-5% and HbA1c increases up to 0.3% documented.
condition_rules.action: Can worsen glycemic control. Monitor glucose after initiating niacin or increasing dose. Low-dose niacin in multivitamins is not a concern.
condition_rules.alert_headline: High-dose niacin may raise blood sugar
condition_rules.alert_body: Niacin can lower blood sugar. If you have diabetes, talk to your clinician before adding Niacin and monitor your glucose, especially in the first weeks.
condition_rules.informational_note: Niacin affects blood sugar — relevant to people with diabetes.

condition_rules.mechanism: Niacin at doses exceeding 2 g/day causes dose-dependent hepatotoxicity via impairment of mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation and calcium homeostasis disruption. Sustained-release formulations are associated with higher hepatotoxicity risk than immediate-release at equivalent doses. Case reports include acute fulminant hepatic failure requiring ICU admission. In patients with pre-existing liver disease, baseline impaired hepatic metabolism reduces the threshold for injury, and niacin-induced vasodilatation may also exacerbate portal hypertension.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in liver disease; doses above 500 mg/day should require physician supervision and baseline LFTs. Avoid sustained-release niacin formulations in patients with liver disease. Discontinue if ALT/AST elevations exceed 3x ULN.
condition_rules.alert_body: Niacin is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of Niacin.
condition_rules.informational_note: Niacin depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

condition_rules.mechanism: Pharmacologic-dose niacin (≥1g/day) raises HDL-C and lowers triglycerides and LDL-C, but large RCTs (AIM-HIGH, HPS2-THRIVE) failed to show cardiovascular benefit when added to statin therapy, and HPS2-THRIVE found excess serious adverse events with niacin. Extended-release niacin combined with laropiprant was withdrawn in Europe. At doses ≥1g/day, niacin also impairs glucose tolerance.
condition_rules.action: High-dose niacin for cholesterol management has fallen out of favor due to lack of incremental cardiovascular benefit on top of statin therapy and significant side effects. Consult prescriber before initiating pharmacologic niacin for lipid management.
condition_rules.alert_body: Niacin can affect lipid metabolism. If you are managing high cholesterol, talk to your clinician before adding Niacin — it may change your lipid-therapy plan.
condition_rules.informational_note: Niacin affects lipid pathways — relevant to people managing high cholesterol.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Niacin can raise blood glucose, potentially requiring adjustment of diabetes medication doses.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor glucose closely. Diabetes medication dose adjustment may be needed.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Niacin can lower blood sugar. If you take a diabetes medication, talk to your prescriber before adding Niacin and monitor your glucose more closely at first.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Niacin affects blood sugar — relevant to people on diabetes medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: High-dose niacin combined with statins increases the risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis beyond that of statin monotherapy. This risk is particularly elevated with gemfibrozil-statin combinations, but niacin-statin combinations also carry increased myopathy risk, estimated at approximately 0.12% incidence in combination therapy trials. The mechanism involves pharmacodynamic additive muscle toxicity.
drug_class_rules.action: If high-dose niacin (≥1g/day) is co-prescribed with statins, monitor for myopathy symptoms (muscle pain, weakness, dark urine). Obtain baseline and periodic CK levels. Avoid high-intensity statin doses in this combination.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Niacin shares muscle or liver pathways with statins. If you take a statin, talk to your prescriber before adding Niacin and watch for new muscle aches.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Niacin affects statin pathways — relevant to people on statin therapy.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Vitamin C is essential, but excessive supplemental intake above UL is not recommended in pregnancy.
condition_rules.action: Keep vitamin C intake within guideline ranges; avoid high-dose supplementation without clinical need.
condition_rules.alert_headline: Keep vitamin C within prenatal range
condition_rules.alert_body: Vitamin C is required in pregnancy, but intakes above the upper limit can raise tolerability concerns. If you are pregnant or nursing, monitor total intake and talk to your clinician.
condition_rules.informational_note: Vitamin C is dose-sensitive at high intake — higher doses warrant clinician review.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Dose-dependent tolerability; excessive daily intake can increase adverse-effect risk.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Threshold escalates only when intake exceeds adult UL.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_headline: Keep vitamin C within prenatal range
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Vitamin C is required in pregnancy, but intakes above the upper limit can raise tolerability concerns. If you are pregnant or nursing, monitor total intake and talk to your clinician.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Vitamin C is dose-sensitive at high intake — higher doses warrant clinician review.

condition_rules.mechanism: Vitamin E is essential, but excessive supplemental intake above established UL is not recommended in pregnancy.
condition_rules.action: Keep total daily vitamin E intake within guideline ranges and avoid megadosing.
condition_rules.alert_headline: Keep vitamin E within prenatal range
condition_rules.alert_body: Vitamin E is dose-sensitive in pregnancy; high intakes can raise adverse-effect risk over time. If you are pregnant or breastfeeding, monitor total intake and talk to your clinician.
condition_rules.informational_note: Vitamin E is dose-sensitive at high intake — higher doses warrant clinician review.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Antiplatelet activity at high doses (≥400 IU/day) via vitamin K-epoxide reductase competition.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take warfarin or an antiplatelet, talk to your prescriber before using vitamin E ≥400 IU/day.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Vitamin E above 400 IU/day can mildly thin the blood and raise bleeding risk when combined with warfarin or aspirin-type drugs.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Vitamin E at high dose has antiplatelet effect — relevant to anyone on blood thinners.

dose_thresholds.note: Vitamin E doses >300 mg/day potentiate anticoagulants. https://ods.od.nih.gov/factsheets/VitaminE-HealthProfessional/

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Dose-dependent risk profile; high intake can increase adverse-effect risk.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Threshold escalates only when intake exceeds adult UL.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_headline: Keep vitamin E within prenatal range
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Vitamin E is dose-sensitive in pregnancy; high intakes can raise adverse-effect risk over time. If you are pregnant or breastfeeding, monitor total intake and talk to your clinician.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Vitamin E is dose-sensitive at high intake — higher doses warrant clinician review.

condition_rules.mechanism: Vitamin K intake changes coagulation balance around perioperative anticoagulation management.
condition_rules.action: Coordinate supplement use with surgeon and stop unless explicitly instructed to continue.
condition_rules.alert_body: Vitamin K affects coagulation and may complicate perioperative anticoagulation plans. If you have surgery scheduled, talk to your surgeon about supplement timing.
condition_rules.informational_note: Vitamin K affects clotting — relevant to anyone preparing for surgery.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Vitamin K opposes warfarin's anticoagulant action via the vitamin-K-dependent clotting cascade. INR destabilization risk; clinical principle is intake consistency, not absolute abstinence.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take warfarin or another vitamin-K antagonist, do not start, stop, or change vitamin K intake without checking with your prescriber. Keep daily intake consistent.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Vitamin K can reduce the effect of warfarin and similar blood thinners. Talk to your prescriber before adding a vitamin K supplement and keep your daily intake steady.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Vitamin K affects how warfarin works — relevant to anyone on warfarin or coumarin anticoagulants.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Longer half-life menaquinone forms may have prolonged interaction potential with anticoagulants.
drug_class_rules.action: Escalate anticoagulant interaction warning for MK-7 forms.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: The MK-7 form of vitamin K has a longer half-life than other forms. If you take warfarin, talk to your prescriber before adding MK-7.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: MK-7 affects warfarin levels — relevant to people on warfarin or other anticoagulants.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Yohimbe has stimulant and hemodynamic effects with concerning maternal-fetal risk profile.
condition_rules.action: Do not use during pregnancy.
condition_rules.alert_body: Yohimbe's stimulant and hemodynamic effects raise maternal-fetal risk. If you are pregnant, do not use yohimbe-containing products.
condition_rules.informational_note: Yohimbe has stimulant effects relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Adrenergic stimulation may increase blood pressure and cardiovascular risk.
condition_rules.action: Do not use in hypertension or cardiovascular risk settings.
condition_rules.alert_headline: Not recommended with high blood pressure
condition_rules.alert_body: Yohimbe's stimulant activity can raise blood pressure and heart rate. If you have high blood pressure, talk to your clinician and do not use it.
condition_rules.informational_note: Yohimbe is a stimulant — relevant to people with high blood pressure.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Yohimbe can counter blood pressure-lowering effects.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid co-use with antihypertensive medications.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Yohimbe's stimulant activity can work against blood-pressure-lowering drugs. If you take antihypertensive medication, talk to your prescriber before using it.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Yohimbe is a stimulant — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Alpha-2 antagonist increases norepinephrine release; combined with MAO inhibition → severe hypertension. Common in pre-workout and male-enhancement stacks where users may not connect the dots.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not combine yohimbe with MAOIs. Many pre-workout and male-enhancement supplements contain yohimbe — read labels carefully.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: If you take MAO inhibitors, do not combine with this product. Yohimbe combined with MAO-inhibitor antidepressants can cause severe high blood pressure. Many pre-workout and 'male enhancement' suppl...
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Yohimbe is a sympathomimetic — relevant to anyone on MAOIs.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Safety data are limited and stimulant effects raise risk concerns.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid in pregnancy and lactation.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Yohimbe's stimulant and hemodynamic effects raise maternal-fetal risk. If you are pregnant, do not use yohimbe-containing products.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Yohimbe has stimulant effects relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Concentrated ginger extracts may affect platelet function.
condition_rules.action: Review pre-op supplement use.
condition_rules.alert_body: Ginger extract can affect bleeding around procedures. If you have surgery scheduled, talk to your surgeon about pausing ginger extract about two weeks before the procedure.
condition_rules.informational_note: Ginger extract can affect perioperative bleeding — relevant to anyone preparing for surgery.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Potential additive bleeding tendency.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor with clinician guidance.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Ginger extract has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding ginger extract and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Ginger extract can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Caulophyllum thalictroides contains caulosaponin and methylcytisine, which stimulate uterine contractions. Case reports document neonatal stroke, heart failure, and multi-organ injury following maternal use near term.
condition_rules.action: Do not use during pregnancy.
condition_rules.alert_body: Blue cohosh stimulates uterine contractions and has been linked to severe newborn harm. If you are pregnant, do not use any blue cohosh product.
condition_rules.informational_note: Blue cohosh stimulates contractions — relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Uterotonic glycosides (caulosaponin) and nicotinic alkaloids (methylcytisine) pose direct fetal risk including cardiac toxicity and CNS injury.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Multiple case reports of serious neonatal adverse events. ACOG and NCCIH advise against use in pregnancy.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Blue cohosh stimulates uterine contractions and has been linked to severe newborn harm. If you are pregnant, do not use any blue cohosh product.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Blue cohosh stimulates contractions — relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Artemisia vulgaris contains thujone and other volatile terpenoids with emmenagogue and abortifacient activity. Animal studies demonstrate uterotonic effects.
condition_rules.action: Do not use during pregnancy.
condition_rules.alert_body: Mugwort contains compounds with uterine-stimulating and historical abortifacient activity. If you are pregnant, do not use mugwort-containing products.
condition_rules.informational_note: Mugwort has uterotonic activity relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Thujone-containing Artemisia species have documented emmenagogue activity. Traditional abortifacient use is well-described historically.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid all Artemisia vulgaris preparations during pregnancy and lactation.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Mugwort contains compounds with uterine-stimulating and historical abortifacient activity. If you are pregnant, do not use mugwort-containing products.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Mugwort has uterotonic activity relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Ruta graveolens contains furanoquinoline alkaloids (dictamnine, skimmianine) and furanocoumarins with established abortifacient activity. Animal studies confirm embryotoxic and teratogenic effects at moderate doses.
condition_rules.action: Do not use during pregnancy.
condition_rules.alert_body: Rue has established abortifacient activity and teratogenic effects in animal studies. If you are pregnant, do not use rue-containing products.
condition_rules.informational_note: Rue has abortifacient activity — relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Ruta alkaloids cause uterine smooth muscle contraction and have embryotoxic effects demonstrated in animal models.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Rue is a well-documented traditional abortifacient. Avoid all preparations during pregnancy and lactation.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Rue has established abortifacient activity and teratogenic effects in animal studies. If you are pregnant, do not use rue-containing products.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Rue has abortifacient activity — relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Kavalactones (from Piper methysticum) inhibit CYP1A2, CYP2C9, CYP2C19, CYP2D6, and CYP3A4, disrupting hepatic drug metabolism. Mechanistically, kavalactones activate the pregnane X receptor (PXR) and undergo bioactivation via CYP3A4 to reactive metabolites that form protein adducts in hepatocytes, inducing mitochondrial dysfunction and hepatocellular necrosis. Over 100 spontaneous reports of severe hepatotoxicity have been documented in Western nations, including cases progressing to fulminant hepatic failure requiring liver transplantation. Alcohol co-ingestion potentiates toxicity. Patients with pre-existing liver disease face dramatically amplified risk from both reduced clearance and baseline hepatocellular vulnerability.
condition_rules.action: Contraindicated in patients with any chronic liver disease. Do not use kava supplements alongside alcohol or other hepatotoxic agents. FDA and European regulatory agencies have issued warnings against kava use in liver disease.
condition_rules.alert_body: Kava has been linked to serious liver injury. If you have any chronic liver condition, do not use kava; also avoid with alcohol or other hepatotoxic agents.
condition_rules.informational_note: Kava has liver safety signals relevant to people with liver conditions.

condition_rules.mechanism: Kavalactones have demonstrated anticonvulsant properties in animal models via GABA-A receptor modulation. However, abrupt discontinuation after regular use may produce withdrawal seizures. Additionally, unpredictable CNS effects make kava use in epilepsy patients risky without neurologist oversight.
condition_rules.action: Avoid kava in seizure disorders without neurologist supervision. Do not abruptly discontinue if already in use.
condition_rules.alert_body: Kava may affect seizure threshold in susceptible people. If you have a seizure disorder, talk to your neurologist before adding kava.
condition_rules.informational_note: Kava may affect seizure threshold — relevant to people with a seizure disorder.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Kavalactones enhance ligand binding at GABA-A receptors, block voltage-gated sodium channels, inhibit calcium ion channels, and reduce norepinephrine reuptake — multiple CNS depressant mechanisms overlapping with benzodiazepines and other sedatives. Potent additive CNS depression, ataxia, respiratory depression risk with concurrent sedatives.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not combine kava with sedatives, benzodiazepines, alcohol, or opioids. Risk of potent additive CNS depression. Contraindicated with prescription sedative-hypnotics.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Kavalactones add to the effects of sedatives, benzodiazepines, alcohol, and opioids. If you take sedating medications, do not combine with kava.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Kava adds to sedation — relevant to people on sedatives, benzodiazepines, or opioids.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Kava has CNS-active constituents and documented hepatotoxic potential. No safe dose in pregnancy or lactation has been established.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid kava supplements entirely during pregnancy and lactation.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_headline: Not recommended in pregnancy or breastfeeding
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Kava has CNS-active compounds and no established safe dose in pregnancy or breastfeeding. If you are pregnant or nursing, do not use kava supplements.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Kava has CNS and hepatic safety concerns — relevant to anyone pregnant, planning pregnancy, or breastfeeding.

condition_rules.mechanism: Valerian root (Valeriana officinalis) is associated with rare idiosyncratic hepatotoxicity based on published case reports. The mechanism is not fully established; isovaleric acid and valerenic acid undergo hepatic oxidation via CYP2C9, and valerian also contains small amounts of hepatotoxic alkaloids. The overall incidence of liver injury is low, but patients with pre-existing liver disease have reduced hepatic reserve and may be at higher risk from idiosyncratic reactions.
condition_rules.action: Monitor liver function (ALT, AST) if valerian is used long-term in patients with liver disease. Discontinue immediately if transaminase elevations are detected.
condition_rules.alert_body: Valerian is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of valerian.
condition_rules.informational_note: Valerian depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Valerenic acid from valerian root increases GABA modulation at GABA-A receptors, sharing a pharmacological mechanism with benzodiazepines. Additive CNS depression, sedation, and psychomotor impairment may occur when combined with sedative drugs.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid combining valerian with prescription sedatives or sleep medications without physician oversight. Do not drive or operate machinery when combining these agents.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Valerian has mild sedative effects. If you take a sedative or sleep medication, talk to your prescriber before adding valerian — the combination can increase drowsiness.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Valerian has sedative activity — relevant to people on sedatives or sleep medications.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Insufficient safety data for use during pregnancy and lactation; valerian's valepotriates have shown cytotoxic potential in vitro.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid valerian during pregnancy and breastfeeding due to insufficient safety data.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_headline: Not recommended in pregnancy or breastfeeding
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Valerian's safety in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not established, and its valepotriates have shown cytotoxic activity in laboratory studies. If you are pregnant or nursing, do not use valerian.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Valerian lacks pregnancy safety data — relevant to anyone pregnant, planning pregnancy, or breastfeeding.

condition_rules.mechanism: Saw palmetto (Serenoa repens) has been associated with rare cholestatic hepatitis and, in at least one case, simultaneous acute hepatitis and pancreatitis with rechallenge confirmation. The mechanism is likely idiosyncratic and related to lipophilic fatty acid esters in the extract undergoing hepatic esterase hydrolysis. Standard rat studies at 2–5x human doses showed no liver toxicity markers, suggesting the risk is low but not zero. Pre-existing liver disease may reduce the threshold for idiosyncratic reactions.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in liver disease. Monitor baseline and periodic LFTs (ALT, ALP, bilirubin) if saw palmetto is initiated in patients with liver disease. Discontinue at first sign of cholestasis (jaundice, elevated ALP, pruritus).
condition_rules.alert_body: Saw palmetto is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of saw palmetto.
condition_rules.informational_note: Saw palmetto depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

condition_rules.mechanism: A published case report documents severe intraoperative hemorrhage in a patient taking saw palmetto (Serenoa repens), with prolonged bleeding time that normalized after discontinuation. The proposed mechanism involves COX pathway inhibition by the lipidosterolic extract, which may reduce thromboxane-mediated platelet activation. However, a randomized in vivo study of standard-dose saw palmetto (manufacturer's recommended dose, 2 weeks) found no measurable effect on PFA-100 platelet function assay in healthy adults.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in patients with bleeding disorders. The evidence is based on a case report; risk may be dose- and context-dependent. Discuss with a healthcare provider before use.
condition_rules.alert_body: Saw palmetto has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding saw palmetto.
condition_rules.informational_note: Saw palmetto can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

condition_rules.mechanism: A case report of severe intraoperative hemorrhage associated with saw palmetto use prompted awareness of potential perioperative bleeding risk. Bleeding time was prolonged and normalized after discontinuation. One RCT (TURP setting) showed pretreatment with Serenoa repens significantly reduced perioperative blood loss, suggesting complex vascular/anti-inflammatory rather than purely antiplatelet effects.
condition_rules.action: Inform your surgeon of saw palmetto use before planned surgery. Based on the case report, consider discontinuing 1-2 weeks before elective procedures as a precaution, though the evidence level is limited.
condition_rules.alert_body: Saw palmetto can affect bleeding around procedures. If you have surgery scheduled, talk to your surgeon about pausing saw palmetto about two weeks before the procedure.
condition_rules.informational_note: Saw palmetto can affect perioperative bleeding — relevant to anyone preparing for surgery.

condition_rules.mechanism: Saw palmetto (Serenoa repens) inhibits 5-alpha-reductase (both type 1 and type 2), reducing conversion of testosterone to the more potent dihydrotestosterone (DHT). In males, this mechanism is the basis for its use in benign prostatic hyperplasia and androgenic alopecia. However, 5-alpha-reductase inhibition in men who are TTC can impair semen quality, reduce seminal fluid volume, and potentially reduce sperm motility. Additionally, saw palmetto may have anti-androgenic effects that interfere with male reproductive hormone signaling needed for spermatogenesis.
condition_rules.action: Men who are actively trying to conceive should discontinue saw palmetto supplementation. 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors (including saw palmetto) may adversely affect semen parameters and male fertility. Discuss with reproductive urologist if fertility is a concern.
condition_rules.alert_body: Saw palmetto has antiandrogenic activity and no established fetal safety. If you are trying to conceive, discuss dosing with your clinician and plan to stop saw palmetto at confirmed pregnancy.
condition_rules.informational_note: Saw palmetto is typically discontinued at confirmed pregnancy — relevant to people trying to conceive.

condition_rules.mechanism: Anti-androgenic and hormonal concern combined with absent pregnancy safety data.
condition_rules.action: Avoid in pregnancy unless specialist-directed.
condition_rules.alert_body: Saw palmetto has hormonal and anti-androgen concerns and pregnancy safety data are limited. If you are pregnant, talk to your clinician before using it.
condition_rules.informational_note: Saw palmetto lacks pregnancy safety data and may affect hormone signaling.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Theoretical concern based on the case report of bleeding and proposed COX-mediated antiplatelet activity. No documented case reports of saw palmetto altering INR or warfarin activity. Risk is considered low at standard doses.
drug_class_rules.action: Inform your prescriber if taking saw palmetto while on anticoagulant therapy. Monitor for unusual bleeding or bruising.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Saw palmetto has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding saw palmetto and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Saw palmetto can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Theoretical additive antiplatelet effect through COX pathway inhibition. The case report of prolonged bleeding time supports caution, though in vivo platelet function studies at standard doses showed no significant effect.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for signs of increased bleeding when combining saw palmetto with antiplatelet medications.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Saw palmetto has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding saw palmetto.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Saw palmetto can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Theoretical additive COX inhibition. Both saw palmetto lipidosterolic extract and NSAIDs affect prostaglandin synthesis. Clinical significance is uncertain at standard saw palmetto doses.
drug_class_rules.action: No specific dose adjustment required. Inform your healthcare provider if combining these agents.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: May add to NSAID bleeding risk
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Saw palmetto has mild antiplatelet activity that can add to NSAID bleeding risk. If you take NSAIDs regularly, talk to your prescriber before adding saw palmetto.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Saw palmetto can affect platelet function — relevant to people on NSAIDs.

dose_thresholds.note: Liver injury rarely reported; high-dose precaution. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16800417/

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Saw palmetto has antiandrogenic activity; safety data in pregnancy and lactation are absent.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Not recommended during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_headline: Not recommended in pregnancy or breastfeeding
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Saw palmetto has antiandrogenic activity with no pregnancy or lactation safety data. If you are pregnant or breastfeeding, do not use saw palmetto supplements.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Saw palmetto is antiandrogenic — relevant to anyone pregnant, planning pregnancy, or breastfeeding.

condition_rules.mechanism: Dietary turmeric and culinary curcumin use is generally safe and even hepatoprotective at low doses. However, high-dose concentrated curcumin supplements — particularly formulations co-administered with piperine (black pepper extract), which can increase curcumin bioavailability up to 20-fold — have been implicated in drug-induced liver injury (DILI) case reports with cholestatic and hepatocellular patterns. The proposed mechanism involves CYP1A2 and CYP3A4 inhibition by curcumin, leading to reactive metabolite accumulation. Patients with existing liver disease may have impaired detoxification capacity, lowering the safety threshold.
condition_rules.action: Culinary turmeric is acceptable. Avoid high-dose curcumin supplement formulations (>500 mg curcumin/day) especially those combined with piperine in patients with liver disease. Monitor LFTs if high-dose curcumin is initiated.
condition_rules.alert_body: Turmeric is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of turmeric.
condition_rules.informational_note: Turmeric depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

condition_rules.mechanism: Curcumin, the active principle of turmeric, inhibits platelet aggregation induced by multiple agonists including arachidonic acid (AA), platelet-activating factor (PAF), collagen, ADP, and epinephrine. The primary mechanisms are (1) inhibition of thromboxane A2 (TXA2) synthesis via COX-1 suppression, (2) inhibition of Ca2+ mobilization in platelets, and (3) suppression of 12-lipoxygenase and 5-lipoxygenase. At concentrated supplement doses, antiplatelet effects are clinically relevant.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in patients with bleeding disorders. High-dose curcumin supplements (>500 mg/day curcumin extract) may meaningfully increase bleeding risk. Avoid in patients with active bleeding.
condition_rules.alert_body: Turmeric has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding turmeric.
condition_rules.informational_note: Turmeric can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Antiplatelet activity at clinical doses (≥500 mg curcuminoids). Multiple case reports of INR elevation with warfarin. Culinary turmeric doses do not interact meaningfully.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take warfarin or an antiplatelet, talk to your prescriber before using high-dose turmeric or curcumin extracts (≥500 mg curcuminoids/day).
drug_class_rules.alert_body: High-dose turmeric or curcumin extracts can thin the blood and raise INR in people on warfarin. Cooking-amount turmeric is fine.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Standardized curcumin extracts have antiplatelet activity at clinical doses — relevant to anyone on blood thinners.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Curcumin independently inhibits platelet aggregation via TXA2 suppression, COX-1 inhibition, and Ca2+ signaling interference. Combined with antiplatelet drugs (aspirin, clopidogrel), this produces additive platelet inhibition.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for signs of increased bleeding when combining turmeric supplements with antiplatelet medications. High-dose curcumin extracts carry more risk than dietary turmeric.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Turmeric has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding turmeric.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Turmeric can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Curcumin inhibits both COX-1 and 5-lipoxygenase, pathways shared with NSAIDs. Combined use may produce additive GI mucosal effects and platelet inhibition, modestly increasing GI bleeding risk.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for GI symptoms when combining turmeric/curcumin supplements with NSAIDs. Take NSAIDs with food.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: May add to NSAID bleeding risk
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Turmeric has mild antiplatelet activity that can add to NSAID bleeding risk. If you take NSAIDs regularly, talk to your prescriber before adding turmeric.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Turmeric can affect platelet function — relevant to people on NSAIDs.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: NSAID-like prostaglandin inhibition is theoretical for curcumin specifically; clinical lithium-elevation evidence absent. Severity reflects conservative safety posture given lithium's narrow therapeutic index, not an established interaction.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take lithium, mention high-dose turmeric or curcumin to your prescriber. Monitoring is precautionary — clinical evidence is limited.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: High-dose turmeric or curcumin may theoretically affect lithium levels via NSAID-like mechanisms, though clinical evidence is limited. Precautionary advice only.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Conservative posture for lithium given narrow therapeutic index — clinical evidence for curcumin specifically is absent.

condition_rules.mechanism: Silymarin (the active flavonolignan complex from Silybum marianum) is hepatoprotective and widely used in liver disease patients — it reduces oxidative stress, inhibits inflammatory signaling, and has antifibrotic properties. However, silymarin and its constituents (especially silybin A, isosilybin A/B, silychristin) are mechanism-based inhibitors of CYP3A4 and also inhibit P-glycoprotein. In liver disease patients who are on hepatically-cleared medications (antivirals, immunosuppressants, anticoagulants), this CYP3A4 inhibition can elevate drug plasma levels to toxic ranges. The herb itself does not cause hepatotoxicity at standard doses.
condition_rules.action: Milk thistle is generally beneficial in liver disease but requires monitoring for drug interactions in patients on CYP3A4-metabolized medications. Review the full medication list before initiating. Preferred formulation: standardized silymarin 70–80%, 140–420 mg/day.
condition_rules.alert_body: Milk thistle is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of milk thistle.
condition_rules.informational_note: Milk thistle depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

condition_rules.mechanism: EPA and DHA competitively inhibit arachidonic acid metabolism, suppressing thromboxane A2 (TXA2) synthesis and reducing platelet aggregation. At doses >3 g/day of combined EPA+DHA, clinically measurable prolongation of bleeding time has been observed. Additionally, omega-3 PUFAs may decrease circulating factor VII levels, further impairing the coagulation cascade.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in patients with bleeding disorders. Doses above 3 g/day of EPA+DHA may significantly increase bleeding risk. Consult a healthcare provider before use.
condition_rules.alert_body: Omega-3 has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding Omega-3.
condition_rules.informational_note: Omega-3 can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

condition_rules.mechanism: Antiplatelet effects of EPA and DHA via TXA2 suppression may theoretically increase perioperative bleeding risk. Large randomized trials (OPERA, n=1516) found no significant increase in surgical bleeding, but case series with high-dose use (6 g/day) combined with other anticoagulants have reported serious hemorrhagic events including subdural hematoma.
condition_rules.action: Inform your surgeon of fish oil use before planned procedures. High-dose fish oil (>3 g/day) combined with other anticoagulants or antiplatelets warrants discussion with the surgical team. Current evidence does not mandate routine discontinuation for standard doses.
condition_rules.alert_body: Omega-3 can affect bleeding around procedures. If you have surgery scheduled, talk to your surgeon about pausing Omega-3 about two weeks before the procedure.
condition_rules.informational_note: Omega-3 can affect perioperative bleeding — relevant to anyone preparing for surgery.

condition_rules.mechanism: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) have established cardioprotective effects, reducing major cardiovascular events (RR 0.95), MI (RR 0.90), and cardiovascular death (RR 0.94) in meta-analysis of 15 RCTs. However, high-dose prescription omega-3 supplementation (≥3g EPA+DHA/day) is associated with a significantly increased risk of atrial fibrillation (RR 1.25, 95% CI 1.10–1.41). This dose-dependent AFib risk is most prominent in populations with existing cardiovascular disease.
condition_rules.action: Low to moderate doses (≤2g/day) are generally cardioprotective. High doses (≥3g/day) should only be used under prescriber supervision with ECG monitoring for AFib in patients with heart disease or existing AFib risk factors.
condition_rules.alert_body: Omega-3 can affect heart rate or rhythm in some people. If you have heart disease, talk to your cardiologist before adding Omega-3.
condition_rules.informational_note: Omega-3 can affect cardiovascular pathways — relevant to people with heart conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Omega-3 fatty acids may enhance warfarin's anticoagulant effect through two mechanisms: (1) additive antiplatelet activity via TXA2 pathway suppression, and (2) possible reduction in vitamin K-dependent coagulation factor VII levels. Multiple case reports document INR elevation (from therapeutic range to >4) when fish oil dose was doubled or added to stable warfarin therapy. One case documented INR of 8.06 after initiating fish oil with warfarin.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor INR more frequently if starting, stopping, or changing the dose of fish oil while on warfarin. Inform your prescriber of fish oil supplementation. At doses >2 g/day, consider closer INR surveillance.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Omega-3 has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding Omega-3 and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Omega-3 can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive inhibition of platelet aggregation. Both omega-3s and antiplatelet drugs (aspirin, clopidogrel) independently suppress TXA2-mediated platelet activation. A case report of subdural hematoma after a minor fall involved high-dose omega-3 (6 g/day) combined with both aspirin and warfarin.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid high-dose fish oil (>3 g/day) when combined with antiplatelet medications unless under medical supervision. Monitor for signs of unusual bleeding or bruising.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Omega-3 has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding Omega-3.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Omega-3 can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Both omega-3 fatty acids and NSAIDs affect prostaglandin synthesis through COX pathway modulation. The additive reduction in thromboxane production and potential combined effect on GI mucosal prostaglandins may modestly increase GI bleeding risk when used together.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for GI symptoms (stomach pain, dark stools) when combining fish oil with NSAIDs. Take NSAIDs with food.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: May add to NSAID bleeding risk
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Omega-3 has mild antiplatelet activity that can add to NSAID bleeding risk. If you take NSAIDs regularly, talk to your prescriber before adding Omega-3.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Omega-3 can affect platelet function — relevant to people on NSAIDs.
