drug_class_rules.mechanism: Antiplatelet/antithrombotic at high dose. Most dietary doses (1-2 g/day EPA+DHA) clinically insignificant; ≥3 g/day raises bleeding risk in patients on warfarin or antiplatelets.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take warfarin or an antiplatelet, talk to your prescriber before using fish oil ≥3 g/day. Doses below 3 g/day are generally well tolerated.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Fish oil at 3 g/day or higher can thin the blood. Combined with warfarin or antiplatelet medication, this raises bleeding risk.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Omega-3 has dose-dependent antiplatelet activity — most relevant at total EPA+DHA ≥3 g/day.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive inhibition of platelet aggregation through independent TXA2 suppression. Combined use of high-dose fish oil with aspirin and warfarin has been associated with subdural hematoma after minor trauma.

condition_rules.mechanism: Curcumin preferentially inhibits platelet-activating factor (PAF)- and arachidonic acid (AA)-induced platelet aggregation with IC50 values of 20-25 µM. Mechanisms include: (1) inhibition of TXA2 synthesis (IC50 ~70 µM for TXA2 formation), (2) blockade of intracellular Ca2+ mobilization via the ionophore pathway, (3) inhibition of 12-lipoxygenase and 5-lipoxygenase, and (4) reduction of serotonin levels in platelets. Curcuminoids (the standardized extract) are approximately 2-fold more potent than curcumin alone in inhibiting platelet aggregation.
condition_rules.action: Avoid curcumin extracts in active bleeding disorders. Use with caution in patients with underlying coagulopathy or thrombocytopenia. Dietary turmeric at culinary doses is generally not a concern.
condition_rules.alert_body: Curcumin has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding curcumin.
condition_rules.informational_note: Curcumin can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

condition_rules.mechanism: Culinary turmeric is different from gram-level curcumin extracts; high-dose supplements may affect platelet and GI biology and have limited pregnancy data.
condition_rules.action: Keep to food-level intake; avoid routine high-dose extracts.
condition_rules.alert_headline: High doses may be a concern
condition_rules.alert_body: Food-level turmeric is different from concentrated curcumin extracts. If you are pregnant, routine high-dose curcumin may not be the right choice because safety data are limited.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Curcumin has antiplatelet activity at clinical doses (≥500 mg/day). Multiple case reports of INR elevation with warfarin.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take warfarin or an antiplatelet, talk to your prescriber before using curcumin extracts ≥500 mg/day.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: High-dose curcumin extracts can thin the blood and raise INR in people on warfarin.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Curcumin has antiplatelet activity at clinical doses — relevant to anyone on blood thinners.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Curcumin independently inhibits platelet aggregation via TXA2 suppression, COX-1, 5-LOX, and 12-LOX inhibition, and Ca2+ signaling blockade. Additive inhibition with antiplatelet drugs (aspirin, clopidogrel, ticagrelor) is mechanistically predicted and supported by in vitro data.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for signs of increased bleeding when combining curcumin supplements with antiplatelet medications. Avoid high-dose curcumin extracts (>1 g curcumin/day) without medical supervision in patients on dual antiplatelet therapy.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Curcumin has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding curcumin.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Curcumin can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for GI symptoms (stomach pain, dark stools) when combining curcumin supplements with NSAIDs.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: May add to NSAID bleeding risk
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Curcumin has mild antiplatelet activity that can add to NSAID bleeding risk. If you take NSAIDs regularly, talk to your prescriber before adding curcumin.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Curcumin can affect platelet function — relevant to people on NSAIDs.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Theoretical NSAID-like mechanism; no documented clinical lithium-elevation cases. Conservative posture given lithium's narrow therapeutic index.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take lithium, mention curcumin extracts to your prescriber.

condition_rules.mechanism: Boswellic acids (BAs), particularly acetyl-11-keto-beta-boswellic acid (AKBA), inhibit COX-1 product formation in intact human platelets (IC50 ~6 µM) through direct reversible binding to the COX-1 enzyme. BAs are also potent specific inhibitors of 5-lipoxygenase (5-LO; IC50 1.5 µM for AKBA), reducing leukotriene B4 synthesis. This dual COX-1/5-LOX inhibition profile suggests meaningful antiplatelet potential at supplemental doses. Unlike NSAIDs, BAs do not inhibit the peroxidation of arachidonic acid, making them non-redox inhibitors.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in patients with bleeding disorders. High-dose Boswellia extract standardized for AKBA may inhibit platelet function via COX-1. Consult a healthcare provider before use.
condition_rules.alert_body: Boswellia has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding boswellia.
condition_rules.informational_note: Boswellia can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Theoretical concern based on COX-1 inhibition in platelets and 5-LOX inhibition that could additively suppress hemostatic mechanisms when combined with anticoagulants. No clinical case reports of warfarin-boswellia interaction have been identified. The AKBA-COX-1 interaction is mechanistically relevant but clinical INR impact is unconfirmed.
drug_class_rules.action: Inform your prescriber if using Boswellia supplements while on anticoagulant therapy. Monitor for unusual bruising or bleeding.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Boswellia has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding boswellia and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Boswellia can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: AKBA directly and reversibly inhibits COX-1 in human platelets, the same enzyme targeted by aspirin (irreversibly) and NSAIDs. Combined use with antiplatelet agents produces mechanistically additive platelet inhibition through COX-1 suppression.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for signs of increased bleeding when combining Boswellia supplements with antiplatelet medications. Exercise caution with high-AKBA standardized extracts.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Boswellia has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding boswellia.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Boswellia can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Boswellic acids inhibit COX-1 (AKBA) and 5-LOX, pathways also targeted by NSAIDs. Combined use produces additive arachidonic acid pathway suppression and may modestly increase GI bleeding risk through complementary mechanisms.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for GI symptoms when combining Boswellia with NSAIDs. The combination is mechanistically rational for anti-inflammatory effect but carries additive GI mucosal risk.

condition_rules.mechanism: Evening primrose oil (EPO) contains gamma-linolenic acid (GLA), a precursor to dihomo-gamma-linolenic acid (DGLA) and prostaglandin E1 (PGE1). GLA shifts eicosanoid metabolism away from arachidonic acid-derived TXA2, with PGE1 inhibiting platelet aggregation. Animal studies show EPO significantly extends prothrombin time, activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT), and thrombin time, and decreases platelet count. EPO also demonstrated significant antiplatelet activity in a celecoxib-cardiovascular risk model.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in patients with bleeding disorders or coagulopathy. High-dose EPO (>3 g/day) may affect coagulation parameters. Consult a healthcare provider before use.
condition_rules.alert_body: Evening primrose oil has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding evening primrose oil.
condition_rules.informational_note: Evening primrose oil can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

condition_rules.mechanism: Evening primrose oil (EPO) contains gamma-linolenic acid (GLA). Concern regarding seizure threshold lowering originated from early case reports in the 1980s. A 2007 review concluded the association was likely spurious and that GLA and linoleic acid may actually have anticonvulsant properties. Risk is low but seizure disorder patients warrant monitoring and physician discussion.
condition_rules.action: Evening primrose oil is not absolutely contraindicated in seizure disorders based on current evidence, but patients with epilepsy should discuss use with their neurologist. Monitor for any changes in seizure control.
condition_rules.alert_body: Evening primrose oil may affect seizure threshold in susceptible people. If you have a seizure disorder, talk to your neurologist before adding evening primrose oil.
condition_rules.informational_note: Evening primrose oil may affect seizure threshold — relevant to people with a seizure disorder.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: EPO's GLA-derived PGE1 inhibits platelet aggregation and EPO has demonstrated anti-coagulant effects on multiple coagulation parameters (PT, aPTT, thrombin time) in animal studies. Combined with warfarin, additive anticoagulation could result. Listed among herbs with potential warfarin potentiation in clinical pharmacology references.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor coagulation status if taking EPO with warfarin or other anticoagulants. Inform prescriber of EPO supplementation.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Evening primrose oil has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding evening primrose oil and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Evening primrose oil can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: GLA-derived eicosanoids (PGE1) inhibit platelet aggregation. Combined with antiplatelet drugs, additive platelet inhibition may occur. The clinical magnitude at standard EPO doses (1-3 g/day) is uncertain but mechanistically plausible.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for signs of increased bleeding when combining EPO with antiplatelet medications.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Evening primrose oil has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding evening primrose oil.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Evening primrose oil can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Both EPO (via PGE1 and arachidonic acid competition) and NSAIDs affect prostaglandin pathways. In rat models, EPO was shown to protect against celecoxib-induced cardiovascular thrombogenic risk, suggesting complementary prostaglandin modulation. Combined use may produce complex prostaglandin effects.
drug_class_rules.action: No specific dose adjustment required. Inform your healthcare provider if combining EPO with NSAIDs, especially at high doses.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: May add to NSAID bleeding risk
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Evening primrose oil has mild antiplatelet activity that can add to NSAID bleeding risk. If you take NSAIDs regularly, talk to your prescriber before adding evening primrose oil.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Evening primrose oil can affect platelet function — relevant to people on NSAIDs.

dose_thresholds.note: Authored mechanism anchors the coagulation inflection point at >3 g/day EPO oil dose (not GLA alone). Standard 500–1000 mg softgels are below that threshold. Clinical-team signoff 2026-04-24: threshold applies to EPO oil weight.

condition_rules.mechanism: Vitamin D does not directly affect platelet aggregation at physiological or supplemental doses. However, high-dose vitamin D supplementation may interact with warfarin sensitivity in patients with concurrent vitamin K deficiency by affecting the vitamin K-dependent carboxylation of coagulation factors (II, VII, IX, X). The vitamin D endocrine system and vitamin K share overlapping roles in gamma-carboxylation of hepatic Gla-proteins.
condition_rules.action: Vitamin D at standard supplemental doses (up to 4000 IU/day) is not a clinically significant bleeding risk on its own. Monitor if taking high-dose vitamin D (>10,000 IU/day) with anticoagulants.
condition_rules.alert_headline: Modest bleeding signal at high doses
condition_rules.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding Vitamin D.
condition_rules.informational_note: Vitamin D can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

condition_rules.mechanism: Vitamin D exerts broad immunomodulatory effects, generally shifting immune responses toward tolerance via Treg cell upregulation and Th17 suppression. Low vitamin D status is associated with increased autoimmune risk. Supplementation may be beneficial in Hashimoto thyroiditis and multiple sclerosis, but high doses in active autoimmune disease require monitoring.
condition_rules.action: Vitamin D supplementation at standard doses (1000–4000 IU/day) is generally safe in autoimmune conditions. High-dose vitamin D requires physician-monitored 25-OH vitamin D levels to avoid toxicity.
condition_rules.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild immune-modulating activity. If you have an autoimmune condition, talk to your specialist before adding Vitamin D.
condition_rules.informational_note: Vitamin D affects immune function — relevant to people with autoimmune conditions.

condition_rules.mechanism: Vitamin D deficiency is associated with increased cardiovascular risk, and moderate supplementation is generally considered beneficial. However, pharmacologic doses (>4000 IU/day chronically) can cause hypercalcemia, which promotes vascular calcification and may worsen coronary artery disease. The benefit-risk ratio is dose-dependent.
condition_rules.action: Target serum 25(OH)D levels of 30–60 ng/mL. Avoid high-dose supplementation (>4000 IU/day) without clinical monitoring of calcium and 25(OH)D levels.
condition_rules.alert_headline: Discuss high-dose vitamin D with clinician
condition_rules.alert_body: Vitamin D can affect heart rate or rhythm in some people. If you have heart disease, talk to your cardiologist before adding Vitamin D.
condition_rules.informational_note: Vitamin D can affect cardiovascular pathways — relevant to people with heart conditions.

condition_rules.mechanism: Vitamin D receptors (VDR) are expressed in ovarian granulosa cells, endometrium, and placenta. A 2017 review (Voulgaris et al., Hormones) summarized strong associations between vitamin D status and PCOS metabolic parameters, endometriosis, and IVF outcomes, with deficiency linked to lower clinical pregnancy rates. Meta-analyses of IVF studies suggest women with 25(OH)D >20 ng/mL have significantly better outcomes, though causality is not fully established.
condition_rules.action: Check 25(OH)D levels prior to or during TTC. Correct deficiency (<20 ng/mL) with supplementation (1000–2000 IU/day) under clinician guidance. Achieving sufficiency (30–60 ng/mL) is recommended before and during early pregnancy. Standard prenatal vitamins provide ~400–1000 IU/day.
condition_rules.alert_headline: Discuss vitamin D in preconception care
condition_rules.alert_body: Vitamin D used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of Vitamin D with your clinician.
condition_rules.informational_note: Vitamin D is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Vitamin D deficiency should be corrected, but routine high-dose use beyond standard supplementation should be tracked with labs.
condition_rules.action: Monitor 25(OH)D and avoid unmonitored chronic high dosing.
condition_rules.alert_body: Vitamin D deficiency should be corrected, but chronic high-dose use beyond standard supplementation should be tracked with labs. If you are pregnant, monitor 25(OH)D with your clinician.

condition_rules.mechanism: Repleting deficiency can improve insulin sensitivity in some patients.
condition_rules.action: Monitor glucose and adjust diabetes therapy if needed.
condition_rules.alert_body: Repleting vitamin D deficiency can improve insulin sensitivity in some people. If you take it for diabetes support, monitor glucose and adjust therapy as needed.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: High-dose vitamin D (>10,000 IU/day) has been associated with altered warfarin sensitivity in some patients. The proposed mechanism involves shared metabolic pathways with vitamin K: vitamin D may modulate hepatic expression of vitamin K-dependent coagulation factors (II, VII, IX, X), and vitamin D deficiency itself affects coagulation factor synthesis. Vitamin D and K have documented synergistic effects on Gla-protein carboxylation, and disruption of this balance at supraphysiological vitamin D doses may affect INR stability in warfarin patients.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor INR stability when significantly changing vitamin D supplementation dose, particularly at high doses (>10,000 IU/day). Routine supplementation doses (1000-4000 IU/day) typically do not require INR monitoring changes.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding Vitamin D and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Vitamin D can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: No established direct interaction between vitamin D supplementation and antiplatelet drug activity. Monitoring recommended at high doses due to broader hemostatic pathway involvement.
drug_class_rules.action: No specific action required at standard supplemental doses. Monitor for unusual bleeding if taking high-dose vitamin D with antiplatelet medications.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding Vitamin D.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Vitamin D can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: No established pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic interaction between vitamin D and NSAIDs on platelet or coagulation pathways. Theoretical concern at high doses given shared anti-inflammatory regulatory roles.
drug_class_rules.action: No specific action required at standard supplemental doses.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: May add to NSAID bleeding risk
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild antiplatelet activity that can add to NSAID bleeding risk. If you take NSAIDs regularly, talk to your prescriber before adding Vitamin D.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Vitamin D can affect platelet function — relevant to people on NSAIDs.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Vitamin D has additive immunomodulatory effects that may complement immunosuppressant therapy. Standard-dose supplementation is generally considered safe alongside immunosuppressants and may reduce corticosteroid-induced bone loss.
drug_class_rules.action: Standard vitamin D supplementation is appropriate alongside immunosuppressants. Monitor 25-OH vitamin D levels annually. High-dose supplementation (>4000 IU/day) requires physician oversight.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Vitamin D has mild immune-modulating activity. If you take immunosuppressive medication, talk to your specialist before adding Vitamin D.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Vitamin D affects immune function — relevant to people on immunosuppressants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Thiazide diuretics reduce urinary calcium excretion; high vitamin D doses enhance calcium absorption, increasing the risk of hypercalcemia.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid high-dose vitamin D (>4000 IU/day) when taking a thiazide diuretic. Monitor serum calcium under medical supervision.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: Vitamin D plus thiazides may raise calcium
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Thiazide diuretics reduce calcium excretion. High-dose vitamin D (>4000 IU/day) can raise blood calcium. If you take a thiazide, talk to your doctor about monitoring calcium.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: High vitamin D doses combined with thiazides may cause hypercalcemia.

dose_thresholds.note: Above the Endocrine Society supervised-use ceiling (10,000 IU/day), authored action recommends monitoring when combined with anticoagulation. Endocrine Society CPG 2011 permits ≤6,000–10,000 IU/day therapeutically under clinical supervision; not an absolute avoid.

IOM UL for adults is 4,000 IU/day. Above this, authored action requires physician-monitored 25-OH vitamin D levels to avoid toxicity; deficiency treatment in this range may still be appropriate under clinician guidance (Endocrine Society 2011).
IOM UL for adults is 4,000 IU/day. Authored action: standard supplementation is appropriate alongside immunosuppressants; >4,000 IU/day requires physician oversight, not an absolute contraindication.
Thiazide diuretics reduce renal calcium excretion; high-dose vitamin D can increase hypercalcemia risk. https://ods.od.nih.gov/factsheets/VitaminD-HealthProfessional/

pregnancy_lactation.notes: No severe pregnancy-specific evidence at RDA-range doses; talk to your clinician before use, especially at higher doses.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: At RDA-range doses, this nutrient has no specific pregnancy or lactation concerns. Higher therapeutic doses may have a different safety profile — talk to your clinician.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: RDA-range standard nutrient — generally compatible with pregnancy and lactation; high-dose forms have separate evidence bases.

condition_rules.mechanism: Glucosamine does not directly inhibit platelet aggregation or the coagulation cascade via known pharmacological mechanisms. The bleeding risk in patients with bleeding disorders is primarily indirect — through potentiation of anticoagulant medications (warfarin), as documented in multiple case reports and FDA/WHO pharmacovigilance databases.
condition_rules.action: Glucosamine at standard doses (1500 mg/day) is not a direct bleeding risk for most patients with bleeding disorders. Use caution if also taking anticoagulants.
condition_rules.alert_headline: Modest bleeding signal in case reports
condition_rules.alert_body: Glucosamine has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding glucosamine.
condition_rules.informational_note: Glucosamine can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Multiple case reports and pharmacovigilance data document glucosamine-associated INR elevation in patients on stable warfarin therapy. A systematic pharmacovigilance review (Knudsen & Sokol, 2008) identified 20 FDA MedWatch reports and 21 WHO reports of altered coagulation (increased INR, bleeding) with glucosamine and warfarin, including one case of intraventricular bleed and subdural hematoma. A dose-response relationship was observed: one patient's INR rose from 2.3 to 3.9 after dose escalation from 1000 to 3000 mg/day, then to 4.7 on further dose reduction delay, normalizing after glucosamine discontinuation. The mechanism is unknown — glucosamine is not a known inhibitor of CYP2C9 or CYP3A4.
drug_class_rules.action: Inform your prescriber if taking glucosamine while on warfarin. Check INR within 2-4 weeks of starting or stopping glucosamine. Dose escalation of glucosamine may destabilize previously stable anticoagulation control.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Glucosamine has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding glucosamine and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Glucosamine can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: No documented direct interaction between glucosamine and antiplatelet drugs. Theoretical concern given unknown mechanism of warfarin potentiation.
drug_class_rules.action: No specific dose adjustment required. Inform prescriber of glucosamine use if on antiplatelet therapy.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Glucosamine has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding glucosamine.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Glucosamine can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Glucosamine is frequently co-administered with chondroitin for osteoarthritis, often as an alternative to NSAIDs. No known direct pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic interaction with NSAIDs on coagulation.
drug_class_rules.action: No specific action required. Glucosamine may allow dose reduction of NSAIDs in osteoarthritis management.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: May add to NSAID bleeding risk
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Glucosamine has mild antiplatelet activity that can add to NSAID bleeding risk. If you take NSAIDs regularly, talk to your prescriber before adding glucosamine.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Glucosamine can affect platelet function — relevant to people on NSAIDs.

dose_thresholds.note: 1500 mg/day is the standard osteoarthritis therapeutic dose and the point at which INR elevation cases (Knudsen & Sokol 2008 pharmacovigilance review, 20 FDA MedWatch + 21 WHO reports) are clinically documented. Sub-1500 mg use in compound formulas still warrants prescriber awareness but not an avoid-level alert. Clinical-team signoff 2026-04-24.

pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Chamomile (Matricaria chamomilla) contains natural coumarins including herniarin and umbelliferone, which share structural similarity with warfarin. Additionally, chamomile contains flavonoids (apigenin, quercetin) that may inhibit platelet aggregation. A systematic review of herb-warfarin interactions identified chamomile tea as one of the herbs associated with bleeding events and INR elevation. A case of multiple thromboses and hemorrhage in a patient consuming chamomile tea (also taking warfarin) was reported.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in patients with bleeding disorders. Concentrated chamomile extracts and large volumes of chamomile tea represent more risk than occasional use. Avoid in active bleeding.
condition_rules.alert_body: Chamomile has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding chamomile.
condition_rules.informational_note: Chamomile can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Chamomile contains natural coumarins (herniarin, umbelliferone) that may have additive anticoagulant effect with warfarin. A systematic review of 149 herb-warfarin interaction reports specifically identifies chamomile tea as causing warfarin potentiation with bleeding events. The 2000 AJHP review (Heck et al.) lists chamomile among herbs that may potentially increase bleeding or potentiate warfarin effects.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid large amounts of chamomile tea (>4 cups/day) or concentrated chamomile extracts while on warfarin. Inform your prescriber. Monitor INR if chamomile consumption is substantial or newly started.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Chamomile has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding chamomile and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Chamomile can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Chamomile flavonoids (apigenin, quercetin) may inhibit platelet aggregation through various mechanisms. Clinical significance at typical chamomile tea consumption levels is low.
drug_class_rules.action: Moderate chamomile tea consumption is unlikely to cause clinically significant interaction with antiplatelet drugs. Monitor for unusual bleeding with concentrated extracts.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Chamomile has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding chamomile.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Chamomile can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Chamomile has mild anti-inflammatory properties and may modestly affect prostaglandin synthesis. No established clinically significant interaction with NSAIDs on bleeding parameters.
drug_class_rules.action: No specific action required.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: May add to NSAID bleeding risk
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Chamomile has mild antiplatelet activity that can add to NSAID bleeding risk. If you take NSAIDs regularly, talk to your prescriber before adding chamomile.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Chamomile can affect platelet function — relevant to people on NSAIDs.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Apigenin, the primary bioactive flavonoid in chamomile (Matricaria chamomilla), binds to central benzodiazepine receptors and reduces locomotor activity in animal models. While apigenin shows weaker sedative activity than classical benzodiazepines, additive CNS depression is possible with concurrent sedative use.
drug_class_rules.action: Use caution when combining chamomile supplements (not chamomile tea) with prescription sedatives. Monitor for excessive drowsiness.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Chamomile has mild sedative effects. If you take a sedative or sleep medication, talk to your prescriber before adding chamomile — the combination can increase drowsiness.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Chamomile has sedative activity — relevant to people on sedatives or sleep medications.

pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Cranberry (Vaccinium macrocarpon) contains flavonoids and proanthocyanidins that may inhibit CYP2C9, the primary enzyme responsible for S-warfarin metabolism. While in vitro data suggest CYP2C9 inhibitory potential, two prospective randomized controlled trials using metabolic surrogates found no significant CYP2C9 inhibition at typical cranberry juice consumption levels. Case reports of INR elevation exist but confounding factors were present. Direct antiplatelet effects from cranberry flavonoids are theoretically possible but not clinically established.
condition_rules.action: Cranberry at typical dietary amounts is unlikely to cause clinically significant bleeding. Use caution with concentrated cranberry supplements in patients with bleeding disorders, especially if also taking warfarin.
condition_rules.alert_body: Cranberry has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding cranberry.
condition_rules.informational_note: Cranberry can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Inhibits CYP2C9 → may increase warfarin S-enantiomer exposure → ↑ INR. UK MHRA issued formal advisory in 2003; clinical evidence since has been mixed but the signal is real at high cranberry doses.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take warfarin, monitor INR when using cranberry extract or drinking ≥1 L cranberry juice/day.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: High-dose cranberry extracts or large amounts of cranberry juice can raise INR in people on warfarin. Mention cranberry use to your prescriber.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Cranberry may increase warfarin levels at high doses — UK MHRA issued an advisory.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Cranberry flavonoids may have mild antiplatelet properties in vitro. No established clinical interaction with antiplatelet drugs. The primary interaction concern for cranberry is with warfarin via CYP2C9.
drug_class_rules.action: No specific action required for antiplatelet combinations at dietary cranberry amounts.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Cranberry has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding cranberry.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Cranberry can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: No established direct pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic interaction between cranberry and NSAIDs on coagulation or platelet function.
drug_class_rules.action: No specific action required.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: May add to NSAID bleeding risk
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Cranberry has mild antiplatelet activity that can add to NSAID bleeding risk. If you take NSAIDs regularly, talk to your prescriber before adding cranberry.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Cranberry can affect platelet function — relevant to people on NSAIDs.

condition_rules.mechanism: Flaxseed is the richest plant source of alpha-linolenic acid (ALA, C18:3 n-3). ALA undergoes partial conversion to EPA in humans. Flaxseed oil supplementation significantly increases platelet EPA content (platelet EPA:AA ratio increases), which is associated with reduced TXA2 production and decreased platelet aggregation potential. In a human RCT, flaxseed oil supplementation (40 g/day for 23 days) reduced collagen-induced platelet aggregation at 0.75 and 2 µg collagen concentrations. However, at lower doses (10-40 g milled flaxseed/day in a 30-day RCT), no change in platelet aggregation or urinary thromboxane was observed, suggesting dose-dependence.
condition_rules.action: Flaxseed at typical dietary amounts (1-2 tablespoons/day) is generally safe. High-dose flaxseed oil supplementation may reduce platelet aggregation. Use caution in patients with active bleeding disorders. Consult a healthcare provider for high-dose use.
condition_rules.alert_body: Flaxseed has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding flaxseed.
condition_rules.informational_note: Flaxseed can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Flaxseed's ALA-derived EPA may modestly affect platelet TXA2 production. The conversion rate from ALA to EPA in humans is limited (~5-10%), so anticoagulant potentiation is less likely than with direct EPA/DHA (fish oil) supplementation. No case reports of flaxseed altering warfarin INR have been identified.
drug_class_rules.action: Flaxseed supplementation is unlikely to require INR monitoring changes at typical doses. Inform prescriber of high-dose flaxseed oil use (>30 g ALA/day equivalent) during warfarin therapy.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Flaxseed has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding flaxseed and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Flaxseed can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: High-dose flaxseed oil supplementation reduces collagen-induced platelet aggregation in humans by increasing platelet EPA:AA ratio and reducing TXA2 synthesis potential. Additive platelet inhibition with antiplatelet drugs is mechanistically predicted at high doses.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for signs of increased bleeding at high flaxseed oil doses (>30 mL/day) when combined with antiplatelet medications.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Flaxseed has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding flaxseed.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Flaxseed can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Flaxseed's omega-3 fatty acids and NSAIDs both modulate prostaglandin synthesis through different mechanisms (ALA→EPA competing with AA; NSAIDs inhibiting COX). Combined use may additively reduce platelet TXA2 production.
drug_class_rules.action: No specific action required at typical dietary flaxseed amounts. Monitor for GI symptoms with high-dose flaxseed oil combined with NSAIDs.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: May add to NSAID bleeding risk
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Flaxseed has mild antiplatelet activity that can add to NSAID bleeding risk. If you take NSAIDs regularly, talk to your prescriber before adding flaxseed.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Flaxseed can affect platelet function — relevant to people on NSAIDs.

pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Uncaria tomentosa (cat's claw) bark and leaf extracts demonstrate antiplatelet activity and thrombin inhibition in vitro. Ethanolic fractions inhibit thrombin with IC50 values of 5.86 µg/mL (amidolytic assay) and 12.48 µg/mL (proteolytic assay). The main active components responsible for thrombin inhibition are procyanidins B2 and C1, which interact with thrombin both within and outside the active site. Slight antiplatelet activity and prolonged thrombin time have also been demonstrated. Traditional use of cat's claw includes anticoagulant applications and hemorrhage therapy.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in patients with bleeding disorders. Cat's claw extracts have demonstrated antiplatelet and thrombin-inhibiting activity in vitro. Consult a healthcare provider before use.
condition_rules.alert_body: Cat's claw has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding cat's claw.
condition_rules.informational_note: Cat's claw can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

condition_rules.mechanism: Cat's claw (Uncaria tomentosa) contains immunomodulatory alkaloids and oxindole alkaloids that enhance immune cell function including macrophage and lymphocyte activation. Immune stimulation may exacerbate autoimmune disease.
condition_rules.action: Avoid in autoimmune conditions. Not suitable for patients on immunosuppressive therapy.
condition_rules.alert_body: Cat's claw stimulates immune activity. If you have an autoimmune condition, talk to your clinician before use — it may worsen immune activation.
condition_rules.informational_note: Cat's claw is immune-stimulating — relevant to people with autoimmune conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Cat's claw procyanidins (B2, C1) directly inhibit thrombin, a key enzyme in the coagulation cascade that converts fibrinogen to fibrin. Combined use with anticoagulants (warfarin, heparins, direct oral anticoagulants) may produce additive anticoagulant effects. The herb is documented in the Heck et al. 2000 AJHP review as a herb with potential warfarin interaction.
drug_class_rules.action: Inform your prescriber if taking cat's claw while on anticoagulant therapy. Monitor for signs of excessive anticoagulation (unusual bruising, prolonged bleeding). Avoid in patients requiring tight INR control.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Cat's claw has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding cat's claw and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Cat's claw can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Cat's claw extracts demonstrate antiplatelet activity in vitro. Procyanidins and other polyphenolic components inhibit platelet activation pathways. Combined with antiplatelet drugs, additive platelet inhibition is mechanistically predicted.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for signs of increased bleeding when combining cat's claw with antiplatelet medications.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Cat's claw has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding cat's claw.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Cat's claw can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Cat's claw inhibits TNF-alpha and NF-kB signaling, and inhibits COX-2, pathways also modulated by NSAIDs. Additive anti-inflammatory and potential additive GI mucosal effects may occur.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for GI symptoms when combining cat's claw with NSAIDs.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: May add to NSAID bleeding risk
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Cat's claw has mild antiplatelet activity that can add to NSAID bleeding risk. If you take NSAIDs regularly, talk to your prescriber before adding cat's claw.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Cat's claw can affect platelet function — relevant to people on NSAIDs.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Cat's claw immunomodulatory alkaloids may stimulate immune pathways suppressed by immunosuppressant medications, creating pharmacological antagonism.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not combine with immunosuppressants. Patients on transplant or autoimmune protocols should avoid cat's claw.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Cat's claw stimulates immune pathways that immunosuppressants are designed to calm. If you take transplant or autoimmune medications, do not combine.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Cat's claw is immune-stimulating — relevant to people on immunosuppressant medications.

condition_rules.mechanism: Devil's claw (Harpagophytum procumbens) is documented in clinical pharmacology literature as having potential interactions with warfarin and possible anticoagulant activity. The proposed mechanisms involve iridoid glycoside harpagoside-mediated inhibition of arachidonic acid metabolism and modulation of COX/LOX pathways, which may contribute to antiplatelet effects. Devil's claw is explicitly listed as having documented reports of potential interactions with warfarin in the Heck et al. 2000 systematic review of alternative therapy-warfarin interactions (20 products reviewed).
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in patients with bleeding disorders. Devil's claw may affect platelet function and coagulation. Consult a healthcare provider before use.
condition_rules.alert_body: Devil's claw has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding devil's claw.
condition_rules.informational_note: Devil's claw can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Devil's claw is listed among herbal products with documented reports of potential warfarin interaction in the AJHP systematic review (Heck et al., 2000), which covers 20 alternative therapy agents with established or theoretical warfarin interactions. The mechanism may involve COX/LOX pathway modulation by harpagoside, affecting platelet function and potentially coagulation factor activity.
drug_class_rules.action: Inform your prescriber if taking devil's claw while on warfarin or other anticoagulants. Monitor INR if starting or stopping devil's claw supplementation.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Devil's claw has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding devil's claw and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Devil's claw can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Theoretical antiplatelet effect via COX pathway modulation by iridoid glycosides (harpagoside). No published clinical data specifically demonstrating antiplatelet drug interaction.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for signs of increased bleeding when combining devil's claw with antiplatelet medications.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Devil's claw has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding devil's claw.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Devil's claw can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Devil's claw is used as an anti-inflammatory agent with proposed COX and LOX inhibitory activity. Combined use with NSAIDs may produce additive anti-inflammatory effects and potential additive GI mucosal risk.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for GI symptoms when combining devil's claw with NSAIDs.

drug_class_rules.alert_body: Devil's claw has mild antiplatelet activity that can add to NSAID bleeding risk. If you take NSAIDs regularly, talk to your prescriber before adding devil's claw.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Calcium forms insoluble chelate complexes with levothyroxine in the gastrointestinal tract, reducing oral absorption by 20–25%. Effect is dose-dependent and clinically significant at standard supplementation doses (600–1000 mg/day).
drug_class_rules.action: Separate calcium supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours to prevent absorption interference.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Calcium can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing calcium away from your dose.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Calcium affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: No severe pregnancy-specific evidence at RDA-range doses; talk to your clinician before use, especially at higher doses.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: At RDA-range doses, this nutrient has no specific pregnancy or lactation concerns. Higher therapeutic doses may have a different safety profile — talk to your clinician.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: RDA-range standard nutrient — generally compatible with pregnancy and lactation; high-dose forms have separate evidence bases.

condition_rules.mechanism: Calcium supplements (not dietary calcium) are associated with a modestly increased risk of myocardial infarction. Bolland et al. WHI reanalysis found RR 1.24 (95% CI 1.07–1.45) for MI with supplemental calcium. Proposed mechanism: acute hypercalcemia following bolus supplementation may promote coronary arterial calcification and thrombosis. Risk may be attenuated by co-administration with vitamin D.
condition_rules.action: In individuals with existing heart disease, prefer dietary calcium over supplements. If supplementation is necessary, use the lowest effective dose and split doses to blunt calcium spikes. Discuss with cardiologist.
condition_rules.alert_headline: High-dose calcium may raise heart risk
condition_rules.alert_body: Calcium can affect heart rate or rhythm in some people. If you have heart disease, talk to your cardiologist before adding calcium.
condition_rules.informational_note: Calcium can affect cardiovascular pathways — relevant to people with heart conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Zinc chelation reduces levothyroxine absorption. Zinc is also required for thyroid hormone synthesis; deficiency can cause hypothyroidism, creating a complex bidirectional relationship.
drug_class_rules.action: Separate zinc supplementation from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Zinc can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing zinc away from your dose.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Zinc affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: At RDA-range doses, this nutrient has no specific pregnancy or lactation concerns. Higher therapeutic doses may have a different safety profile — talk to your clinician.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: RDA-range standard nutrient — generally compatible with pregnancy and lactation; high-dose forms have separate evidence bases.

condition_rules.mechanism: Soy isoflavones including genistein inhibit thyroid peroxidase (TPO), the enzyme required for thyroid hormone synthesis. Peripubertal and high-dose exposure associated with subclinical hypothyroidism via disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid axis.
condition_rules.action: Individuals with thyroid disorders should discuss soy isoflavone supplementation with their healthcare provider. Monitor thyroid function tests if using high-dose soy isoflavone supplements.
condition_rules.alert_body: Genistein can influence thyroid hormone levels. If you have a thyroid condition, talk to your endocrinologist before adding genistein and monitor your labs.
condition_rules.informational_note: Genistein affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people with thyroid conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Soy isoflavones can inhibit levothyroxine absorption when taken simultaneously and may independently suppress thyroid hormone synthesis via TPO inhibition, requiring dose adjustments.
drug_class_rules.action: Separate soy isoflavone supplements from levothyroxine by at least 4 hours. Monitor TSH levels.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Genistein can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing genistein away from your dose.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Genistein affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Selenium is essential for iodothyronine deiodinase (type I/II/III) function required to convert T4 to active T3. Selenium supplementation may benefit Hashimoto thyroiditis by reducing thyroid peroxidase antibody titers. However, excess selenium (>400 mcg/day) can paradoxically impair thyroid function and is toxic.
condition_rules.action: Selenium supplementation in thyroid disorders should be guided by serum selenium levels. Doses above 200 mcg/day should only be used under medical supervision.
condition_rules.alert_body: Selenium can influence thyroid hormone levels. If you have a thyroid condition, talk to your endocrinologist before adding selenium and monitor your labs.
condition_rules.informational_note: Selenium affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people with thyroid conditions.

dose_thresholds.note: NIH ODS UL for adults is 400 mcg/day; above this, selenium becomes toxic and paradoxically impairs thyroid function per authored mechanism.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: No severe pregnancy-specific evidence at RDA-range doses; talk to your clinician before use, especially at higher doses.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: At RDA-range doses, this nutrient has no specific pregnancy or lactation concerns. Higher therapeutic doses may have a different safety profile — talk to your clinician.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: RDA-range standard nutrient — generally compatible with pregnancy and lactation; high-dose forms have separate evidence bases.

condition_rules.mechanism: Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) root extract increases serum T3 and T4 levels and decreases TSH, with pro-thyroid effects demonstrated in both animal hypothyroidism models and human endocrine reviews. This can alter thyroid function test results and may be inappropriate in hyperthyroid states.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in thyroid disorders. Monitor thyroid function tests (TSH, T3, T4) during use. Contraindicated in hyperthyroidism without physician oversight.
condition_rules.alert_body: Ashwagandha can influence thyroid hormone levels. If you have a thyroid condition, talk to your endocrinologist before adding ashwagandha and monitor your labs.
condition_rules.informational_note: Ashwagandha affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people with thyroid conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Ashwagandha has demonstrated GABAergic activity via withanolides and sitoindosides. Concomitant use with CNS sedatives may result in additive CNS depression, enhanced drowsiness, and impaired psychomotor performance.
drug_class_rules.action: Advise patients taking CNS depressants or sedatives to use ashwagandha with caution and to monitor for excessive sedation.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Ashwagandha has mild sedative effects. If you take a sedative or sleep medication, talk to your prescriber before adding ashwagandha — the combination can increase drowsiness.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Ashwagandha has sedative activity — relevant to people on sedatives or sleep medications.

condition_rules.mechanism: High-dose biotin (>5 mg/day, common in hair/nail supplements) interferes with biotin-streptavidin immunoassay platforms used for TSH, free T4, free T3, and thyroglobulin measurement. Causes false-low TSH and false-high free thyroid hormone results, mimicking hyperthyroidism. Does not alter actual thyroid function.
condition_rules.action: Stop biotin supplements at least 2 days before thyroid function testing to prevent assay interference. Inform your healthcare provider about biotin use if thyroid tests are planned.
condition_rules.alert_headline: May interfere with thyroid lab tests
condition_rules.alert_body: Biotin can influence thyroid hormone levels. If you have a thyroid condition, talk to your endocrinologist before adding Biotin and monitor your labs.
condition_rules.informational_note: Biotin affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people with thyroid conditions.

dose_thresholds.note: Above 5 mg/day, biotin-streptavidin immunoassay interference causes false-low TSH and false-high free T3/T4 (mimicking hyperthyroidism). Does not alter actual thyroid function — stop biotin ≥2 days before thyroid labs.

condition_rules.mechanism: Echinacea stimulates both innate and adaptive immune function, upregulating macrophage activity, natural killer cells, and pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6, IFN-gamma). This immune activation directly opposes the therapeutic goal of immunosuppression in autoimmune disease management.
condition_rules.action: Avoid echinacea in autoimmune conditions (e.g., lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis, inflammatory bowel disease). Risk of autoimmune flare.
condition_rules.alert_body: Echinacea stimulates immune activity. If you have lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, MS, or IBD, talk to your clinician before use — it may trigger flares.
condition_rules.informational_note: Echinacea is immune-stimulating — relevant to people with autoimmune conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Echinacea's immune-stimulating actions directly antagonize the mechanism of immunosuppressive drugs. Cynarin, an Echinacea component, blocks CD28-CD80 T-cell co-stimulation, but the net immunostimulatory effect of the whole herb opposes pharmacological immunosuppression, potentially precipitating rejection or autoimmune flare.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid concurrent use. Echinacea is contraindicated in patients on immunosuppressive therapy for autoimmune disease or organ transplant.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Echinacea's immune-stimulating effects work against immunosuppressive drugs. If you take transplant or autoimmune medications, do not combine.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Echinacea is immune-stimulating — relevant to people on immunosuppressant medications.

pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Elderberry (Sambucus nigra) extracts stimulate cytokine production including TNF-alpha, IL-1, IL-6, and IL-8. This immune stimulation may exacerbate autoimmune conditions by amplifying the pathological immune response.
condition_rules.action: Avoid elderberry supplements in autoimmune conditions. Consult a physician before use if on immunosuppressive therapy.
condition_rules.alert_body: Elderberry boosts inflammatory cytokine production. If you have an autoimmune condition, talk to your clinician before use — it may worsen activity.
condition_rules.informational_note: Elderberry is immune-stimulating — relevant to people with autoimmune conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Elderberry's pro-inflammatory cytokine stimulation (TNF-alpha, IL-1, IL-6) may functionally oppose immunosuppressive drug therapy, particularly in transplant patients or those with active autoimmune disease.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid concurrent use with immunosuppressants. Patients on transplant or autoimmune immunosuppression should not take elderberry.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Elderberry's cytokine-stimulating effects work against immunosuppressive therapy. If you take transplant or autoimmune medications, do not combine.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Elderberry is immune-stimulating — relevant to people on immunosuppressant medications.

condition_rules.mechanism: Astragalus membranaceus polysaccharides (APS) activate T cells and B cells, enhance NK cell activity, and stimulate cytokine production. This immunostimulation may amplify pathological autoimmune responses and counteract immunosuppressive therapy.
condition_rules.action: Avoid in autoimmune conditions and in patients on immunosuppressive therapy.
condition_rules.alert_body: Astragalus enhances T-cell, B-cell, and NK cell activity. If you have an autoimmune condition, talk to your clinician before use — it may worsen immune activity.
condition_rules.informational_note: Astragalus is immune-stimulating — relevant to people with autoimmune conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Astragalus polysaccharides stimulate T-cell and B-cell activation pathways that immunosuppressants are designed to suppress. This creates pharmacological antagonism with immunosuppressive agents.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not use astragalus with immunosuppressive medications. This combination may undermine transplant protection or autoimmune disease control.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Astragalus stimulates the exact immune pathways immunosuppressants calm. If you take transplant or autoimmune medications, do not combine.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Astragalus is immune-stimulating — relevant to people on immunosuppressant medications.

condition_rules.mechanism: Reishi (Ganoderma lucidum) beta-glucans stimulate immune responses including macrophage activation, NK cell enhancement, and increased immunoglobulin production. In autoimmune conditions, this immunostimulation may amplify the pathological immune response.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in autoimmune conditions. Discuss with a physician before use if on immunosuppressive therapy.
condition_rules.alert_body: Reishi has mild immune-modulating activity. If you have an autoimmune condition, talk to your specialist before adding reishi.
condition_rules.informational_note: Reishi affects immune function — relevant to people with autoimmune conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Reishi beta-glucan immune stimulation may oppose immunosuppressant pharmacological effects. Effect is likely milder than Echinacea or Astragalus but warrants monitoring.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid concurrent use with immunosuppressants without physician oversight, particularly in transplant patients.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Reishi has mild immune-modulating activity. If you take immunosuppressive medication, talk to your specialist before adding reishi.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Reishi affects immune function — relevant to people on immunosuppressants.

condition_rules.mechanism: Melatonin's effects on seizure threshold are debated. While some data suggests anticonvulsant properties via melatonin receptor MT1/MT2 modulation, case reports of seizure exacerbation in predisposed individuals exist. Overall risk is low but warrants physician discussion in active seizure disorders.
condition_rules.action: Use melatonin with caution in seizure disorders. Start at lowest effective dose and monitor for any changes in seizure frequency.
condition_rules.alert_body: Melatonin may affect seizure threshold in susceptible people. If you have a seizure disorder, talk to your neurologist before adding melatonin.
condition_rules.informational_note: Melatonin may affect seizure threshold — relevant to people with a seizure disorder.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Melatonin has sedative and hypnotic properties mediated via MT1/MT2 receptors in the suprachiasmatic nucleus. Concurrent use with CNS sedatives produces additive drowsiness, impaired coordination, and psychomotor slowing.
drug_class_rules.action: Advise caution when combining melatonin with CNS depressants. Avoid driving or operating machinery. Consider dose reduction of sedatives if concurrent use is necessary.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Melatonin has mild sedative effects. If you take a sedative or sleep medication, talk to your prescriber before adding melatonin — the combination can increase drowsiness.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Melatonin has sedative activity — relevant to people on sedatives or sleep medications.

pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Borage seed oil contains high concentrations of gamma-linolenic acid (GLA, ~24%), similar to evening primrose oil. Borage also contains trace pyrrolizidine alkaloids in some preparations, which pose additional neurotoxic risk. Historical concern regarding GLA and seizure threshold lowering applies similarly to borage as to EPO.
condition_rules.action: Avoid high-dose borage oil in seizure disorders. If used, ensure products are certified pyrrolizidine alkaloid-free. Discuss with prescribing neurologist.
condition_rules.alert_body: Borage seed oil may affect seizure threshold in susceptible people. If you have a seizure disorder, talk to your neurologist before adding borage seed oil.
condition_rules.informational_note: Borage seed oil may affect seizure threshold — relevant to people with a seizure disorder.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Chrysin and other flavonoids from Passiflora species act as partial agonists at benzodiazepine (GABA-A) receptors, producing anxiolytic and sedative effects. Concurrent use with CNS sedatives may produce additive CNS depression.
drug_class_rules.action: Use caution when combining passionflower with prescription sedatives. Avoid driving or operating machinery. Reduce alcohol intake during concurrent use.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Passionflower has mild sedative effects. If you take a sedative or sleep medication, talk to your prescriber before adding passionflower — the combination can increase drowsiness.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Passionflower has sedative activity — relevant to people on sedatives or sleep medications.

pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: CoQ10 (ubiquinone/ubiquinol) is involved in mitochondrial ATP synthesis in cardiac myocytes. Supplementation has shown modest benefit in heart failure (improved ejection fraction and symptom class in Q-SYMBIO trial). However, CoQ10 may interfere with anticoagulation therapy (warfarin), an important consideration in cardiac patients.
condition_rules.action: CoQ10 may be beneficial in heart failure under clinician guidance. Monitor INR closely if on warfarin, as CoQ10 has been associated with increased bleeding risk (OR 3.69 in longitudinal study). Discuss with cardiologist before initiating.
condition_rules.alert_body: CoQ10 can affect heart rate or rhythm in some people. If you have heart disease, talk to your cardiologist before adding CoQ10.
condition_rules.informational_note: CoQ10 can affect cardiovascular pathways — relevant to people with heart conditions.

condition_rules.mechanism: Statins inhibit HMG-CoA reductase, blocking not only cholesterol synthesis but also the mevalonate pathway used to synthesize CoQ10. Plasma and muscle CoQ10 levels are measurably reduced by statin therapy. CoQ10 depletion is proposed as a contributing mechanism to statin-induced myopathy.
condition_rules.action: CoQ10 supplementation (100–300 mg/day) is commonly used to address statin-induced muscle symptoms. It is considered safe to combine. Discuss with prescriber whether supplementation is warranted based on symptoms.
condition_rules.alert_body: CoQ10 can affect lipid metabolism. If you are managing high cholesterol, talk to your clinician before adding CoQ10 — it may change your lipid-therapy plan.
condition_rules.informational_note: CoQ10 affects lipid pathways — relevant to people managing high cholesterol.

condition_rules.mechanism: CoQ10 is an essential mitochondrial antioxidant. Oocyte quality declines with age partly due to reduced mitochondrial CoQ10 availability and impaired ATP production. Ben-Meir et al. (2015, Aging Cell) demonstrated that CoQ10 supplementation reversed age-related oocyte deficits in mice, prevented premature ovarian failure in Pdss2-deficient animals, and improved meiotic spindle integrity. A 2024 meta-analysis of 20 RCTs (Shang et al.) found CoQ10 improved retrieved oocyte count, high-quality embryo rates, and clinical pregnancy rates in women with ovarian aging, with optimal effect at 30 mg/day for 3 months before ovarian stimulation in women under 35 with diminished ovarian reserve.
condition_rules.action: CoQ10 supplementation (100–600 mg/day, commonly 200–400 mg/day) may support oocyte quality during the preconception period, especially in women over 35 or those with diminished ovarian reserve. Safe to use during TTC. Discontinue when pregnancy is confirmed to return to standard prenatal supplement regimen.
condition_rules.alert_body: CoQ10 used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of CoQ10 with your clinician.
condition_rules.informational_note: CoQ10 is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Structural similarity to vitamin K; mild reduction in warfarin effect at high dose (≥100 mg/day). Effect is small but documented.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take warfarin, mention CoQ10 supplements to your prescriber. INR monitoring may need to be adjusted.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: CoQ10 is structurally similar to vitamin K and can slightly reduce how well warfarin works at higher doses.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: CoQ10 may affect INR at high doses — relevant to anyone on warfarin.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Statins deplete endogenous CoQ10 by blocking the mevalonate pathway. CoQ10 supplementation is commonly used to mitigate statin-associated myopathy. No adverse pharmacokinetic interaction between CoQ10 and statins has been demonstrated.
drug_class_rules.action: CoQ10 100–300 mg/day may be used alongside statins, particularly if myopathy symptoms are present. Safe to co-administer.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: CoQ10 shares muscle or liver pathways with statins. If you take a statin, talk to your prescriber before adding CoQ10 and watch for new muscle aches.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: CoQ10 affects statin pathways — relevant to people on statin therapy.

pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Red yeast rice (RYR) contains monacolin K, which is chemically identical to lovastatin, a prescription statin drug. FDA has determined that RYR products containing significant amounts of monacolin K are unapproved new drugs and cannot be legally marketed as supplements. Monacolin K content varies widely (0–10 mg per dose) and is not standardized, making dosing unpredictable. EFSA 2025 concluded no safe daily intake level for monacolins can be established.
condition_rules.action: Do not use red yeast rice supplements for cholesterol management. Individuals seeking statin therapy should discuss prescription statin options with their physician.
condition_rules.alert_body: Red yeast rice contains monacolin K, the same molecule as the prescription statin lovastatin. If you have high cholesterol, talk to your prescriber about approved statin options.
condition_rules.informational_note: Red yeast rice contains a natural statin — relevant to people managing cholesterol.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Combining red yeast rice (which contains monacolin K, a natural statin) with prescription statin therapy constitutes uncontrolled statin dosing. The total statin load becomes unpredictable, significantly increasing the risk of statin myopathy, rhabdomyolysis, and hepatotoxicity. There is no safe way to calibrate the RYR contribution to total statin effect.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not combine red yeast rice with any prescription statin. Inform your prescriber if you have been taking red yeast rice. Risk of rhabdomyolysis and liver injury.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Red yeast rice contains the same molecule as lovastatin. If you take a statin, do not combine — total statin exposure becomes unpredictable and muscle or liver injury risk rises.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Red yeast rice contains a natural statin — relevant to people on statin therapy.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Banned/recalled or high-risk substance — avoid in pregnancy regardless of underlying mechanism.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: This substance is banned, recalled, or high-risk and should not be used during pregnancy or breastfeeding under any circumstances.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Banned/recalled or high-risk substance — pregnancy default is contraindicated.

condition_rules.mechanism: Bergamot polyphenols (neoriocitrin, naringin, neohesperidin) inhibit HMG-CoA reductase activity and reduce lipid accumulation in adipocytes. Human clinical trials have reported meaningful reductions in LDL-C and total cholesterol. The mechanism of action overlaps with statins.
condition_rules.action: May provide additive cholesterol-lowering benefit when used alongside dietary modifications. If combined with statin therapy, monitor lipid levels and be alert to additive LDL-lowering. Dose adjustment of statin may be needed if cholesterol falls excessively low.
condition_rules.alert_body: Citrus bergamot can affect lipid metabolism. If you are managing high cholesterol, talk to your clinician before adding citrus bergamot — it may change your lipid-therapy plan.
condition_rules.informational_note: Citrus bergamot affects lipid pathways — relevant to people managing high cholesterol.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Furanocoumarins inhibit intestinal CYP3A4 → ↑ statin AUC 5-15× → rhabdomyolysis risk. Affects simvastatin and lovastatin most; atorvastatin smaller effect; pravastatin and rosuvastatin minimal.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take simvastatin or lovastatin, avoid grapefruit and bergamot products. Pravastatin or rosuvastatin are safer alternatives if grapefruit cannot be eliminated.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: If you take statins, do not combine with this product. Grapefruit and bergamot block an enzyme that breaks down certain statins, dramatically raising drug levels and the risk of muscle damage.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Grapefruit/bergamot interact with simvastatin and lovastatin — relevant to anyone on those statins.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Same CYP3A4 inhibition mechanism. Increased exposure to felodipine and nifedipine raises hypotension and edema risk.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take a calcium channel blocker, avoid grapefruit and bergamot products. Talk to your prescriber.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: May lower blood pressure too much
drug_class_rules.alert_body: If you take calcium channel blockers, do not combine with this product. Grapefruit and bergamot can boost levels of calcium channel blockers, leading to dangerously low blood pressure or swelling.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Grapefruit/bergamot affect calcium channel blockers — relevant to anyone on felodipine, nifedipine, or related drugs.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Same CYP3A4 mechanism. Tacrolimus and cyclosporine have narrow therapeutic indices; toxic levels cause nephrotoxicity.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take tacrolimus or cyclosporine, do not consume grapefruit or bergamot products. This is a strict contraindication.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: Do not combine with transplant drugs
drug_class_rules.alert_body: If you take immunosuppressants, do not combine with this product. Grapefruit and bergamot can push tacrolimus and cyclosporine to toxic levels, damaging the kidneys. Strict avoidance is required.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Grapefruit/bergamot are contraindicated with transplant immunosuppressants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: CYP3A4 inhibition combined with amiodarone's intrinsic QT-prolongation amplifies torsades de pointes risk. Documented cases.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take amiodarone, avoid grapefruit and bergamot products.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: If you take antiarrhythmics, do not combine with this product. Grapefruit and bergamot raise amiodarone levels and increase the risk of dangerous heart rhythms.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Grapefruit/bergamot stack QT risk with amiodarone.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: CYP3A4 + P-gp inhibition increases DOAC (apixaban, rivaroxaban) exposure → bleeding risk.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take apixaban or rivaroxaban, talk to your prescriber before using grapefruit or bergamot products.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Grapefruit and bergamot can raise levels of newer blood thinners like apixaban and rivaroxaban, increasing bleeding risk.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Grapefruit/bergamot affect DOACs through CYP3A4 and P-gp.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: CYP3A4 inhibition may modestly increase ethinyl estradiol exposure. Effect is documented but small.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take oral contraceptives, mention regular grapefruit or bergamot intake to your prescriber.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Grapefruit and bergamot can mildly increase estrogen levels from birth-control pills. Effect is small but documented.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Grapefruit/bergamot mildly raise oral contraceptive levels.

pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
