condition_rules.mechanism: High-dose pyridoxine (vitamin B6) has been investigated as a pharmacologic agent to suppress postpartum lactation. At doses of 450–600 mg/day for 5–7 days, two trials found ~95% inhibition of lactation, though four other studies failed to demonstrate efficacy by prolactin measurement or clinical assessment. The proposed mechanism is dopaminergic: pyridoxine is a cofactor in dopamine synthesis; elevated dopamine tonically inhibits prolactin release from the anterior pituitary. A 1979 NEJM case report warned of dangers of vitamin B6 in nursing mothers at high doses. Normal multivitamin doses (1.9 mg/day recommended for lactation) are not a concern.
condition_rules.action: Standard prenatal vitamin doses of B6 are safe during lactation. High-dose B6 supplementation (>50 mg/day) during established breastfeeding may suppress milk production. Avoid high-dose B6 if breastfeeding is intended. Consult lactation specialist before use.
condition_rules.alert_headline: High-dose B6 may affect milk supply
condition_rules.alert_body: Pharmacologic B6 doses may reduce milk supply via prolactin suppression. If you are breastfeeding, talk to your clinician before using concentrated B6; prenatal-vitamin levels of B6 are appropriate.
condition_rules.informational_note: High-dose B6 can affect lactation — prenatal-dose B6 is appropriate for anyone breastfeeding.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Normal doses of B6 are essential in pregnancy. High pharmacologic doses during lactation may suppress milk production via dopaminergic inhibition of prolactin.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: This rule applies to supplemental/pharmacologic doses. Prenatal vitamin levels of B6 are appropriate and encouraged.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_headline: High-dose B6 may affect milk supply
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Pharmacologic B6 doses may reduce milk supply via prolactin suppression. If you are breastfeeding, talk to your clinician before using concentrated B6; prenatal-vitamin levels of B6 are appropriate.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: High-dose B6 can affect lactation — prenatal-dose B6 is appropriate for anyone breastfeeding.

condition_rules.mechanism: Salvia officinalis (sage) is traditionally used to suppress lactation (antilactogenic effect). The mechanism involves thujone, camphor, and other volatile terpenes which may have estrogenic and/or dopaminergic effects that reduce prolactin and milk production. Sage tea is used as a traditional remedy to wean nursing infants or reduce painful engorgement. Conversely, nursing mothers who consume sage supplements may experience unwanted milk reduction. Thujone in high concentrations can also be neurotoxic.
condition_rules.action: Avoid sage supplements during established breastfeeding if continuing lactation is desired, as they may reduce milk supply. Culinary amounts of sage in food are generally safe. High-dose sage extracts should be avoided.
condition_rules.alert_headline: Concentrated sage may affect milk supply
condition_rules.alert_body: Sage at supplement or concentrated doses may reduce milk supply. If you are breastfeeding, talk to your clinician before using concentrated forms; culinary use at typical amounts is fine.
condition_rules.informational_note: Concentrated sage can affect lactation — culinary amounts are appropriate for anyone breastfeeding.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Sage contains thujone and other terpenes with potential uterotonic effects (avoid in pregnancy). During lactation, may suppress milk production via antilactogenic mechanisms.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid supplemental/concentrated sage in pregnancy and during active breastfeeding. Culinary use is generally considered safe at typical food amounts.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_headline: Concentrated sage may affect milk supply
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Sage at supplement or concentrated doses may reduce milk supply. If you are breastfeeding, talk to your clinician before using concentrated forms; culinary use at typical amounts is fine.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Concentrated sage can affect lactation — culinary amounts are appropriate for anyone breastfeeding.

condition_rules.mechanism: DHEA (dehydroepiandrosterone) supplementation at 75 mg/day for ≥12 weeks has been studied in women with diminished ovarian reserve (DOR) undergoing IVF. The DITTO RCT protocol targeted women with AFC ≤10 and AMH ≤5 pmol/L. A controlled case-series (Barad & Gleicher 2006) showed significant improvements in fertilized oocytes, normal Day-3 embryo count, and embryos transferred after DHEA treatment. Proposed mechanism: DHEA converts to androgens and estrogens in follicular cells, improving follicular microenvironment and reducing follicle apoptosis. DHEA should not be used without hormonal workup confirming DOR, as it may disrupt normal hormonal balance in women without ovarian reserve issues.
condition_rules.action: DHEA at 75 mg/day may be considered for women with confirmed diminished ovarian reserve, ideally under reproductive endocrinologist guidance. Monitor DHEA-S and androgen levels. Not appropriate for general TTC use without confirmed DOR diagnosis.
condition_rules.alert_body: DHEA used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of DHEA with your clinician.
condition_rules.informational_note: DHEA is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: DHEA is a steroid precursor with potential fetal endocrine effects.
condition_rules.action: Avoid in pregnancy unless a reproductive specialist has a specific indication.
condition_rules.alert_body: DHEA is a steroid precursor that can affect fetal hormone signaling. If you are pregnant, talk to a reproductive specialist before using it.
condition_rules.informational_note: DHEA has fetal endocrine concerns and limited pregnancy safety data.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: DHEA has significant androgenic/hormonal activity and is contraindicated once pregnancy is achieved and during lactation.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Discontinue DHEA immediately upon confirmed pregnancy.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: DHEA has androgenic and hormonal activity that is not recommended once pregnancy is achieved. If pregnancy is confirmed, talk to your obstetrician about discontinuing DHEA.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: DHEA is hormonally active — relevant to anyone pregnant, planning pregnancy, or breastfeeding.

condition_rules.mechanism: Periconceptional folic acid/folate supplementation reduces neural tube defect (NTD) risk by 69% (RR 0.31, 95% CI 0.17–0.58) in Cochrane meta-analysis of 5 RCTs (6708 births). The protective effect operates during neural tube closure (days 21–28 post-conception), before many women know they are pregnant. Supplementation should therefore begin at least 1 month (ideally 3 months) before conception. For women with MTHFR polymorphisms, methylfolate (5-MTHF) is preferred as it bypasses the enzymatic conversion step.
condition_rules.action: Begin folate/folic acid supplementation at least 1 month before attempting conception. Standard dose: 400–800 mcg/day for low-risk women; 4 mg/day (4000 mcg) for women with prior NTD-affected pregnancy. Prefer methylfolate (5-MTHF) if MTHFR variant is known. Continue through first trimester.
condition_rules.alert_body: Folate used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of Folate with your clinician.
condition_rules.informational_note: Folate is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Folate is essential throughout pregnancy and lactation for maternal and fetal/infant cell synthesis. The TTC rule transitions to ongoing pregnancy/lactation supplementation guidance.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Folate supplementation started periconceptionally should continue throughout pregnancy as part of prenatal vitamin regimen.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Folate started before pregnancy should continue through pregnancy and breastfeeding as part of prenatal support. If you are pregnant or nursing, discuss dosing with your obstetrician.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Folate is part of standard prenatal support — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

condition_rules.mechanism: Myo-inositol (the primary isomer in supplements) is a second messenger in insulin signaling and plays a critical role in ovarian follicle maturation. In PCOS, insulin resistance disrupts the inositol phosphoglycan mediator system. Supplementation at 2–4 g/day myo-inositol (alone or in 40:1 ratio with D-chiro-inositol) improves ovulation rates, menstrual regularity, and fertility outcomes. An RCT (Agrawal et al. 2019) found significantly higher live birth rates with metformin + myo-inositol (55%) vs. metformin alone (26.67%, p=0.002) in PCOS women undergoing ovulation induction.
condition_rules.action: Myo-inositol (2–4 g/day) is a well-supported supplement for women with PCOS who are TTC. It improves ovulation and insulin sensitivity. Safe for TTC use. Also safe to continue in early pregnancy under clinician guidance. Not specifically indicated for non-PCOS TTC without ovulation issues.
condition_rules.alert_body: Inositol used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of inositol with your clinician.
condition_rules.informational_note: Inositol is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Inositol can improve insulin signaling and glycemic metrics in some settings.
condition_rules.action: Monitor for additive glucose lowering.
condition_rules.alert_body: Inositol can improve insulin signaling and may lower glucose in some settings. If you take diabetes medicines, watch for additive blood sugar lowering.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Myo-inositol is a naturally occurring compound and generally considered safe in pregnancy. Some evidence supports its use to reduce gestational diabetes risk in PCOS pregnancies. Standard monitoring is appropriate.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Transition to clinician-supervised use once pregnancy is confirmed if continuing supplementation.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Myo-inositol is generally considered safe in pregnancy, and use may continue under clinician supervision. If you are pregnant or nursing, discuss dosing with your obstetrician.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Inositol is continued under clinician supervision — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

condition_rules.mechanism: Centella asiatica triterpenoids (asiaticoside, madecassoside) have been associated with idiosyncratic hepatotoxicity in case reports. Three cases of jaundice with markedly elevated ALT (324–1694 U/L) and ALP were reported after 20–60 days of use; one patient had recurrence on rechallenge. Immune-mediated mechanisms (autoantibodies, granulomatous histology) are hypothesized. Risk is heightened in patients with pre-existing liver disease.
condition_rules.action: Avoid gotu kola in patients with active hepatic disease or elevated liver enzymes. If used, monitor LFTs at baseline and after 4–6 weeks. Discontinue immediately if transaminases rise.
condition_rules.alert_body: Gotu kola is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of gotu kola.
condition_rules.informational_note: Gotu kola depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

condition_rules.mechanism: Centella asiatica triterpenoids modulate collagen synthesis and vascular wall integrity. Preclinical data and the mechanism of wound-healing promotion via vascular remodeling raise a theoretical concern for altered hemostasis in patients with bleeding disorders or on anticoagulants. Direct antiplatelet evidence in humans is limited.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in patients with coagulation disorders or on anticoagulant/antiplatelet therapy. Monitor for unusual bruising or bleeding.
condition_rules.alert_headline: Modest bleeding signal at high doses
condition_rules.alert_body: Gotu kola has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding gotu kola.
condition_rules.informational_note: Gotu kola can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Centella asiatica has clinically demonstrated anxiolytic activity in human trials (significantly reduced anxiety and stress scores at 500 mg twice daily). This CNS-active profile creates a pharmacodynamic additive risk with sedative or anxiolytic medications (benzodiazepines, barbiturates, sleep aids).
drug_class_rules.action: Advise caution when combining with prescription sedatives, benzodiazepines, or CNS depressants. Monitor for excessive sedation. Dose adjustment of the sedative may be warranted.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Gotu kola has mild sedative effects. If you take a sedative or sleep medication, talk to your prescriber before adding gotu kola — the combination can increase drowsiness.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Gotu kola has sedative activity — relevant to people on sedatives or sleep medications.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Rhodiola rosea exerts immunomodulatory effects including differential modulation of T-regulatory cell differentiation, IFN-gamma production, and NK-cell activity in vitro and in vivo. Immune stimulation may worsen autoimmune disease activity or counteract immunosuppressive therapy.
condition_rules.action: Avoid in patients with active autoimmune disease (lupus, MS, rheumatoid arthritis) or those on immunosuppressive therapy. Consult prescribing physician before use.
condition_rules.alert_body: Rhodiola has mild immune-modulating activity. If you have an autoimmune condition, talk to your specialist before adding rhodiola.
condition_rules.informational_note: Rhodiola affects immune function — relevant to people with autoimmune conditions.

condition_rules.mechanism: Rhodiola water extract has been shown to lower systolic blood pressure in spontaneously hypertensive rats via beta-endorphin release and opioid mu-receptor activation. Human evidence is limited but the mechanism supports a BP-lowering effect that could be additive with antihypertensive medications.
condition_rules.action: Monitor blood pressure when initiating rhodiola in hypertensive patients on antihypertensive medications. May have additive effect.
condition_rules.alert_body: Rhodiola can influence blood pressure. If you have high blood pressure, talk to your clinician before adding rhodiola and monitor your readings.
condition_rules.informational_note: Rhodiola can affect blood pressure — relevant to people with high blood pressure.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Rhodiola rosea inhibits CYP2C9 activity by ~21% in healthy volunteers (RCT, n=13), reducing metabolism of CYP2C9 substrates such as warfarin and phenytoin. Additionally, its adaptogenic/stimulant profile may interact unpredictably with CNS sedatives. Bidirectional modulation (stimulant at low dose, sedating at high dose) is reported in the literature.
drug_class_rules.action: Caution with concurrent sedative use. Monitor for altered drug levels if co-administered with CYP2C9-metabolized medications. Avoid high-dose combination.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Rhodiola has mild sedative effects. If you take a sedative or sleep medication, talk to your prescriber before adding rhodiola — the combination can increase drowsiness.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Rhodiola has sedative activity — relevant to people on sedatives or sleep medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Rhodiola's immunostimulatory activity (T-cell modulation, IFN-gamma, NK cell effects) may oppose the intended effect of immunosuppressants (cyclosporine, tacrolimus, mycophenolate, corticosteroids). Additionally, CYP2C9 inhibition may increase exposure of certain immunosuppressants metabolized by that pathway.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not use concurrently with immunosuppressant therapy without specialist guidance. Risk of transplant rejection or autoimmune flare if immunosuppression is counteracted.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Rhodiola has mild immune-modulating activity. If you take immunosuppressive medication, talk to your specialist before adding rhodiola.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Rhodiola affects immune function — relevant to people on immunosuppressants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive antihypertensive effect via beta-endorphin/opioid pathway demonstrated in animal models. May enhance BP-lowering effect of antihypertensive drugs.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor blood pressure when combining with antihypertensive therapy. Dose adjustment may be needed.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Rhodiola can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding rhodiola.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Rhodiola can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: A single small RCT (n=98 diabetic women, 1000 mg/day vs placebo over 3 months) reported preliminary glucose-lowering effects with Tribulus terrestris. Evidence is single-trial and not yet replicated; additive risk with insulin or sulfonylureas remains plausible.
condition_rules.action: Monitor blood glucose when combining with insulin or oral hypoglycemic agents. Caution in patients prone to hypoglycemia.
condition_rules.alert_headline: May affect glucose trends in some people
condition_rules.alert_body: Preliminary evidence suggests tribulus may modestly lower blood sugar. If you have diabetes, talk to your clinician before adding tribulus and monitor your glucose.
condition_rules.informational_note: Tribulus may affect blood sugar — relevant to people with diabetes.

condition_rules.mechanism: Tribulus terrestris has diuretic and uricosuric properties and demonstrated antihypertensive effect in preclinical models (in combination with Zingiber officinale). Additive BP lowering with antihypertensive drugs is a theoretical risk.
condition_rules.action: Monitor blood pressure when using tribulus alongside antihypertensive therapy. Report unusual dizziness or postural hypotension.
condition_rules.alert_body: Tribulus can influence blood pressure. If you have high blood pressure, talk to your clinician before adding tribulus and monitor your readings.
condition_rules.informational_note: Tribulus can affect blood pressure — relevant to people with high blood pressure.

condition_rules.mechanism: A case report documented Tribulus terrestris-induced hepatotoxicity alongside nephrotoxicity and neurotoxicity in a healthy male after 2 days of use (ALT elevated, resolved on discontinuation). Pre-existing liver disease may increase vulnerability to hepatotoxic insult.
condition_rules.action: Avoid in patients with active hepatic disease. If used, monitor liver function tests. Discontinue at the first sign of hepatic adverse effects.
condition_rules.alert_body: Tribulus is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of tribulus.
condition_rules.informational_note: Tribulus depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

condition_rules.mechanism: Tribulus terrestris-induced severe nephrotoxicity was documented in a clinical case report (markedly elevated creatinine, resolved on discontinuation). Patients with pre-existing renal impairment are at increased risk. Traditional use as an antiurolithiatic agent paradoxically carries hepato-renal toxicity risk at higher doses.
condition_rules.action: Avoid in patients with chronic kidney disease or impaired renal function. If used in otherwise healthy individuals, limit duration and monitor serum creatinine.
condition_rules.alert_body: Tribulus is cleared through the kidneys and may accumulate when kidney function is reduced. If you have kidney disease, talk to your nephrologist about safe intake of tribulus.
condition_rules.informational_note: Tribulus depends on kidney clearance — relevant to people with kidney conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive glucose-lowering effect demonstrated in RCT. Risk of hypoglycemia when combined with insulin, sulfonylureas, or other hypoglycemic agents.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor blood glucose closely. Advise patient to watch for hypoglycemia symptoms (shakiness, sweating, confusion).
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Tribulus can lower blood sugar. If you take a diabetes medication, talk to your prescriber before adding tribulus and monitor your glucose more closely at first.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Tribulus affects blood sugar — relevant to people on diabetes medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Diuretic properties and demonstrated antihypertensive effect in preclinical models may additively lower blood pressure.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor blood pressure. Report excessive dizziness or postural hypotension to healthcare provider.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Tribulus can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding tribulus.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Tribulus can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Schisandra chinensis lignans (schisandrin, gomisin A/B/C/N, schisantherin A, schisandrol B) are potent inhibitors of CYP3A4, CYP2C8, CYP2C9, and CYP2C19 in human liver microsomes. Schisandrol B and schisantherin A strongly inhibit CYP3A4 (reversible and mechanism-based inhibition). In a clinical PBPK study, Schisandra sphenanthera co-administration increased tacrolimus AUC by 2.4-fold and midazolam AUC by 2.1-fold. Patients with liver disease on CYP3A4-metabolized drugs (calcineurin inhibitors, statins, antiretrovirals) face clinically significant interaction risk.
condition_rules.action: Avoid in patients with liver disease who are taking CYP3A4-metabolized drugs with narrow therapeutic indices (tacrolimus, cyclosporine, warfarin, many statins, antiretrovirals). If use is unavoidable, TDM of co-administered drugs is mandatory.
condition_rules.alert_body: Schisandra is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of schisandra.
condition_rules.informational_note: Schisandra depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: CYP3A4 inhibition by schisandra lignans clinically increased tacrolimus AUC 2.4-fold and midazolam AUC 2.1-fold. Bosutinib AUC is predicted to increase ~3-fold by PBPK modelling. Immunosuppressants with narrow therapeutic indices (tacrolimus, cyclosporine, sirolimus) are predominantly CYP3A4 substrates — schisandra co-administration risks toxic drug accumulation and organ damage.
drug_class_rules.action: Contraindicated with calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, cyclosporine) and mTOR inhibitors without therapeutic drug monitoring. If clinically necessary, initiate TDM immediately and titrate immunosuppressant dose downward.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Schisandra has mild immune-modulating activity. If you take immunosuppressive medication, talk to your specialist before adding schisandra.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Schisandra affects immune function — relevant to people on immunosuppressants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Schisandra chinensis has CNS-active adaptogenic properties with reported sedative-like effects in preclinical models. Additive CNS depression with sedatives, benzodiazepines, or sleep medications is a theoretical pharmacodynamic risk. CYP3A4 inhibition may also increase plasma levels of CYP3A4-metabolized benzodiazepines (e.g., midazolam, triazolam) — a combined pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic interaction.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for excessive sedation when combining with benzodiazepines or other sedatives. Note that CYP3A4 inhibition by schisandra can elevate levels of CYP3A4-metabolized sedatives.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Schisandra has mild sedative effects. If you take a sedative or sleep medication, talk to your prescriber before adding schisandra — the combination can increase drowsiness.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Schisandra has sedative activity — relevant to people on sedatives or sleep medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Schizandrol-rich extracts inhibit CYP3A4. Some clinical interactions documented with tacrolimus and sirolimus. Common in adaptogen blends used by transplant-recovery population.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take a CYP3A4-substrate prescription drug, talk to your prescriber before using schisandra. Especially relevant for transplant patients on tacrolimus or sirolimus.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Schisandra can change levels of certain prescription drugs by inhibiting a key liver enzyme. Especially important for transplant patients.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Schisandra inhibits CYP3A4 — relevant to anyone on prescription drugs metabolized by that enzyme.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Wild yam (Dioscorea villosa) contains diosgenin, a steroidal sapogenin with documented estrogenic and phytoestrogenic activity in animal and in vitro models. Diosgenin has been shown to act as a phytoestrogen via endothelium-dependent pathways. Despite the historical use of diosgenin as a synthetic hormone precursor, the in vivo conversion to progesterone does not occur in humans — however, direct estrogenic receptor activity is documented and raises concern for disruption of hormonal milieu during pregnancy.
condition_rules.action: Do not use wild yam supplements during pregnancy. The estrogenic activity of diosgenin is not established as safe in pregnancy and poses theoretical risk of hormonal disruption.
condition_rules.alert_body: Wild yam contains diosgenin, a phytoestrogen with hormone-active effects. If you are pregnant, do not use wild yam unless directed by your obstetrician.
condition_rules.informational_note: Wild yam is hormone-active — relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: The estrogenic and phytoestrogenic properties of diosgenin in wild yam carry theoretical risk during lactation. Safety data in lactating humans is absent. Phytoestrogens can be transferred to breast milk and may affect infant hormone levels.
condition_rules.action: Do not use wild yam supplements during breastfeeding due to insufficient safety data and estrogenic activity profile.
condition_rules.alert_body: Wild yam's estrogenic activity has no safety data in lactation and can transfer via breast milk. If you are breastfeeding, talk to your clinician before use.
condition_rules.informational_note: Wild yam is hormone-active — relevant to anyone breastfeeding.

condition_rules.mechanism: Exogenous phytoestrogenic compounds during the preconception period may disrupt the natural hormonal cycle regulation required for ovulation, implantation, and early embryo development. Diosgenin's estrogenic receptor activity is documented in animal models; human TTC data are absent.
condition_rules.action: Caution is warranted when trying to conceive. Consult a reproductive endocrinologist or OB-GYN before using wild yam supplements periconceptionally.
condition_rules.alert_body: Wild yam used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of wild yam with your clinician.
condition_rules.informational_note: Wild yam is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Diosgenin (steroidal sapogenin) in wild yam has documented phytoestrogenic activity. Human conversion to progesterone does not occur in vivo. Safety during pregnancy and lactation is not established. Estrogenic activity may disrupt hormonal balance.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Wild yam is sometimes marketed as a 'natural progesterone' but this claim is not supported by human pharmacokinetics. Avoid during pregnancy and lactation.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Wild yam contains diosgenin, a phytoestrogen with hormone-active effects. If you are pregnant, do not use wild yam unless directed by your obstetrician.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Wild yam is hormone-active — relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Yerba mate (Ilex paraguariensis) contains caffeine and theobromine, both xanthine stimulants with acute sympathomimetic effects on the cardiovascular system. Caffeine causes transient blood pressure elevation via catecholamine release and adenosine receptor antagonism. While one acute crossover RCT (n=13) found no significant BP effect at a single infusion dose, chronic or high-dose use may contribute to sustained hypertensive load, particularly in caffeine-sensitive individuals.
condition_rules.action: Patients with hypertension should limit or avoid yerba mate due to stimulant caffeine content. Monitor blood pressure. Avoid high-dose or frequent consumption.
condition_rules.alert_body: Yerba mate can influence blood pressure. If you have high blood pressure, talk to your clinician before adding yerba mate and monitor your readings.
condition_rules.informational_note: Yerba mate can affect blood pressure — relevant to people with high blood pressure.

condition_rules.mechanism: Yerba mate contains caffeine and theobromine (xanthine stimulants) which increase heart rate and myocardial oxygen demand. Although isolated heart models demonstrate cardioprotective ischemia-reperfusion effects via Akt/eNOS pathways, the stimulant content poses risk for arrhythmia or increased cardiac workload in patients with existing heart disease (heart failure, arrhythmia, coronary artery disease).
condition_rules.action: Caution in patients with established heart disease, arrhythmias, or heart failure. Avoid high-dose yerba mate. Seek cardiologist guidance before regular use.
condition_rules.alert_body: Yerba mate can affect heart rate or rhythm in some people. If you have heart disease, talk to your cardiologist before adding yerba mate.
condition_rules.informational_note: Yerba mate can affect cardiovascular pathways — relevant to people with heart conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Yerba mate contains vitamin K (phylloquinone) which may oppose the anticoagulant effect of vitamin K antagonists such as warfarin. Consistent intake of vitamin K-containing foods/supplements reduces INR. Additionally, caffeine may affect platelet aggregation. Clinically significant INR reduction has been reported with dietary vitamin K in warfarin patients.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor INR closely in patients on warfarin or other vitamin K antagonists who consume yerba mate regularly. Advise consistent intake rather than irregular use to minimize INR fluctuation.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Yerba mate has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding yerba mate and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Yerba mate can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Caffeine in yerba mate exerts sympathomimetic BP elevation that directly opposes the mechanism of antihypertensive medications. Regular yerba mate consumption may blunt the therapeutic effect of antihypertensive drugs.
drug_class_rules.action: Advise patients on antihypertensive therapy to limit or eliminate yerba mate consumption. Monitor blood pressure response. Avoid high-caffeine doses.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Yerba mate can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding yerba mate.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Yerba mate can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) is essential for DNA synthesis, cell division, and one-carbon metabolism. B12 deficiency elevates homocysteine via impaired methionine synthase activity, and hyperhomocysteinemia is associated with recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL), implantation failure, and embryotoxicity. Case-control studies document significantly lower serum B12 in women with recurrent miscarriage vs. controls (mean 197 vs 300 pg/mL, p=0.004 in one Syrian cohort; 178 vs 269 pg/mL, p<0.001 in a Polish cohort). B12 is a standard component of preconception care and prenatal vitamins. Optimizing B12 status before conception is associated with improved fertility outcomes and reduced RPL risk via homocysteine normalization.
condition_rules.action: Ensure adequate vitamin B12 status (serum B12 >400 pg/mL is optimal preconceptionally) when trying to conceive. Standard preconception supplementation: 2.4–6 mcg/day (adequate intake) up to higher doses if deficient. B12 supplementation is recommended as part of a comprehensive preconception vitamin regimen for all women TTC, and is particularly important for vegans, vegetarians, those with gastric conditions, or those on metformin (which reduces B12 absorption).
condition_rules.alert_body: Vitamin B12 used while trying to conceive is often continued through pregnancy as part of prenatal care. If you are planning pregnancy, discuss dosing of Vitamin B12 with your clinician.
condition_rules.informational_note: Vitamin B12 is continued under clinician guidance — relevant to people planning pregnancy.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: B12 is an essential nutrient throughout pregnancy and lactation. Deficiency is associated with neural tube defects, developmental delay, and recurrent pregnancy loss via hyperhomocysteinemia. Standard prenatal vitamins contain B12; supplementation should continue throughout pregnancy and during breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: B12 supplementation during pregnancy is part of standard prenatal care. Vegans and those with malabsorption (pernicious anemia, gastric surgery, metformin use) need higher supplemental doses.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: B12 is essential throughout pregnancy and breastfeeding, and supplementation started pre-conception should continue. If you are pregnant or nursing, discuss dosing with your obstetrician.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: B12 is part of standard prenatal support — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

condition_rules.mechanism: Glycyrrhizin inhibits 11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 2 (11β-HSD2), preventing cortisol inactivation in mineralocorticoid target tissues. This produces pseudoaldosteronism: sodium retention, potassium wasting, and dose-dependent blood pressure elevation. Even doses of 100 mg glycyrrhizin/day (roughly 3–5 g licorice root extract/day) have been documented to raise systolic BP significantly.
condition_rules.action: Avoid licorice root supplements in hypertension. DGL (deglycyrrhizinated licorice) is not subject to this interaction. Disclose all licorice supplement use to prescriber.
condition_rules.alert_body: Whole licorice (with glycyrrhizin) can drive sodium retention and raise blood pressure. If you have hypertension, talk to your clinician; DGL licorice does not carry this concern.
condition_rules.informational_note: Licorice with glycyrrhizin raises blood pressure — relevant to people with hypertension.

condition_rules.mechanism: Glycyrrhizin-induced hypokalemia via mineralocorticoid excess lowers the threshold for cardiac arrhythmias and QT prolongation. Case reports document ventricular tachycardia and torsades de pointes associated with licorice-induced hypokalemia. Patients with pre-existing heart disease, LV dysfunction, or on QT-prolonging medications face amplified arrhythmia risk.
condition_rules.action: Avoid regular licorice root use in heart disease, especially if combined with digoxin, loop diuretics, or QT-prolonging drugs. Monitor potassium and cardiac rhythm if use cannot be avoided.
condition_rules.alert_headline: May affect blood pressure and heart rhythm
condition_rules.alert_body: Licorice root can affect heart rate or rhythm in some people. If you have heart disease, talk to your cardiologist before adding licorice root.
condition_rules.informational_note: Licorice root can affect cardiovascular pathways — relevant to people with heart conditions.

condition_rules.mechanism: Glycyrrhizin-induced sodium and water retention can worsen fluid overload in patients with impaired renal function, potentially accelerating decline in glomerular filtration rate and exacerbating edema. Patients with CKD are also at greater risk for hypokalemia-related complications given baseline electrolyte dysregulation.
condition_rules.action: Avoid licorice root supplements in chronic kidney disease. If use is unavoidable, monitor serum electrolytes (potassium, sodium) and blood pressure closely.
condition_rules.alert_body: Licorice root is cleared through the kidneys and may accumulate when kidney function is reduced. If you have kidney disease, talk to your nephrologist about safe intake of licorice root.
condition_rules.informational_note: Licorice root depends on kidney clearance — relevant to people with kidney conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Glycyrrhizin causes sodium retention and blood pressure elevation via pseudoaldosteronism, directly opposing the pharmacodynamic effect of all antihypertensive drug classes (ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, diuretics, beta-blockers). Clinical case series have documented failed BP control in patients consuming licorice while on antihypertensive therapy.
drug_class_rules.action: Discontinue licorice root supplements in patients on antihypertensive therapy. DGL (deglycyrrhizinated licorice) does not carry this interaction. Inform prescriber of any licorice supplement use.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Licorice's sodium-retaining effect works against antihypertensive therapy. If you take a blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber; DGL licorice is unaffected.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Licorice with glycyrrhizin raises blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

dose_thresholds.note: Corresponds to approximately 3g+ licorice root extract/day. DGL products are exempt.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Glycyrrhizin inhibits placental 11β-HSD2, allowing excess maternal cortisol to cross to the fetus. Finnish cohort data document >2-fold increased preterm delivery and neurodevelopmental harm (reduced IQ, ADHD risk) with sustained glycyrrhizin exposure in pregnancy.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid during pregnancy. DGL form may be used under clinical supervision. Lactation evidence is limited.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Licorice root's glycyrrhizin can allow excess maternal cortisol to reach the fetus. If you are pregnant, talk to your obstetrician; DGL licorice may be used under clinical supervision.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Licorice root with glycyrrhizin affects fetal cortisol — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

condition_rules.mechanism: In severely immunocompromised patients (solid organ transplant recipients, hematologic malignancy, high-dose corticosteroid therapy), orally administered probiotic organisms — particularly Lactobacillus spp. and Saccharomyces boulardii — have been documented in case reports to translocate across disrupted mucosal barriers, causing bacteremia or fungemia. Risk is low in healthy individuals but clinically meaningful in profound immunosuppression.
condition_rules.action: Use caution in severely immunocompromised patients (transplant recipients, patients on high-dose immunosuppression). Discuss risk-benefit with transplant team or treating physician before initiating probiotic supplements. Risk does not apply to mild immunosuppression (e.g., low-dose prednisone).
condition_rules.alert_body: Probiotics has mild immune-modulating activity. If you have an autoimmune condition, talk to your specialist before adding probiotics.
condition_rules.informational_note: Probiotics affects immune function — relevant to people with autoimmune conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Immunosuppressant drugs (calcineurin inhibitors, mTOR inhibitors, high-dose corticosteroids, biologic agents) impair mucosal and systemic immune defenses, increasing the risk that probiotic microorganisms may cause opportunistic infection. Lactobacillus bacteremia and Saccharomyces fungemia have been documented in patients on immunosuppressive regimens, particularly in transplant and oncology settings.
drug_class_rules.action: In patients on immunosuppressive therapy for transplant or oncologic indications, consult with the prescribing team before adding probiotic supplements. For patients on mild immunosuppression (e.g., methotrexate for RA at low doses), risk is substantially lower and probiotics may be appropriate.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Probiotics has mild immune-modulating activity. If you take immunosuppressive medication, talk to your specialist before adding probiotics.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Probiotics affects immune function — relevant to people on immunosuppressants.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Andrographolide, the primary bioactive diterpene of Andrographis paniculata, inhibits platelet-activating factor (PAF)-induced platelet aggregation and suppresses NF-κB-dependent expression of thromboxane A2 synthase. Multiple in vitro and animal studies confirm dose-dependent antiplatelet activity. In patients with pre-existing bleeding disorders, additive platelet inhibition may increase spontaneous bleeding risk.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in patients with bleeding disorders or thrombocytopenia. Avoid at high doses (>100 mg andrographolide/day). Discontinue at least 2 weeks before elective surgery.
condition_rules.alert_body: Andrographis has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding andrographis.
condition_rules.informational_note: Andrographis can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

condition_rules.mechanism: Andrographis paniculata exhibits immunostimulatory activity, upregulating T-cell proliferation, NK cell activity, and cytokine production (IL-2, IFN-γ). In patients with autoimmune conditions relying on immune suppression for disease control, stimulation of autoreactive immune pathways could theoretically exacerbate disease activity.
condition_rules.action: Use caution in patients with autoimmune conditions (lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis, IBD). Consult treating physician before use. Monitor for symptom flares.
condition_rules.alert_body: Andrographis has mild immune-modulating activity. If you have an autoimmune condition, talk to your specialist before adding andrographis.
condition_rules.informational_note: Andrographis affects immune function — relevant to people with autoimmune conditions.

condition_rules.mechanism: Despite hepatoprotective activity documented in some animal models, clinical case reports and DILI network databases document rare but real cases of drug-induced liver injury (DILI) associated with Andrographis paniculata supplementation, presenting as cholestatic or mixed hepatocellular injury. Patients with pre-existing liver disease have reduced hepatic reserve and may be at amplified risk.
condition_rules.action: Avoid Andrographis supplementation in patients with active liver disease or significantly elevated transaminases. If used, monitor liver enzymes (ALT, AST) at baseline and after 4-8 weeks of use. Discontinue immediately if hepatic symptoms develop.
condition_rules.alert_body: Andrographis is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of andrographis.
condition_rules.informational_note: Andrographis depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Andrographolide's PAF antagonism and inhibition of thromboxane A2 synthesis may produce additive anticoagulant or antiplatelet effects when combined with warfarin, heparin, or direct oral anticoagulants. No human pharmacokinetic studies of direct andrographis-warfarin interaction are available, but mechanistic evidence supports caution.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for signs of excessive anticoagulation (bruising, prolonged bleeding) if combining andrographis with anticoagulants. INR monitoring is advisable in warfarin users.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Andrographis has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding andrographis and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Andrographis can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive platelet inhibition via PAF antagonism and suppression of TXA2 synthesis. Combined use with aspirin, clopidogrel, or other antiplatelets may increase bleeding risk.
drug_class_rules.action: Use with caution when combining with antiplatelet agents. Avoid high-dose andrographis (>100 mg andrographolide/day) with dual antiplatelet therapy.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Andrographis has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding andrographis.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Andrographis can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Andrographis's immunostimulatory effects may pharmacodynamically oppose immunosuppressant medications (calcineurin inhibitors, corticosteroids, biologics), potentially reducing their therapeutic efficacy or triggering rejection episodes in transplant recipients.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid use in transplant recipients or patients on immunosuppressive therapy for serious autoimmune conditions without specialist guidance.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Andrographis has mild immune-modulating activity. If you take immunosuppressive medication, talk to your specialist before adding andrographis.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Andrographis affects immune function — relevant to people on immunosuppressants.

dose_thresholds.note: Threshold expressed in andrographolide-equivalent dose (the rule's canonical active), NOT whole-herb extract weight, so low-dose whole-herb products do not overfire. Clinical-team signoff 2026-04-24: this threshold is supported by mechanism (dose-dependent PAF antagonism and TXA2 suppression) but has no strong clinical-trial anchor — keep as alerting cutoff only, not a universally validated number.
