Clinical-team signoff 2026-04-24: this threshold is supported by mechanism (dose-dependent PAF antagonism and TXA2 suppression) but has no strong clinical-trial anchor — keep as alerting cutoff only, not a universally validated number.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Red clover (Trifolium pratense) contains coumestrol and coumarin-like compounds alongside isoflavones (biochanin A, formononetin, genistein, daidzein). Coumarins share structural homology with warfarin. Additionally, isoflavone metabolites may mildly inhibit platelet aggregation. While no direct clinical evidence of red clover-induced bleeding has been documented, the structural pharmacology of its constituents supports a caution-level concern in patients with pre-existing bleeding disorders.
condition_rules.action: Use caution in patients with bleeding disorders. Avoid high-dose red clover isoflavone extracts (>160 mg/day) in active bleeding. Disclose use to clinician.
condition_rules.alert_body: Red clover has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding red clover.
condition_rules.informational_note: Red clover can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

condition_rules.mechanism: The soy isoflavones genistein and daidzein — present in red clover at significant concentrations — are established inhibitors of thyroid peroxidase (TPO), the enzyme responsible for thyroid hormone synthesis. In vitro data demonstrate that genistein and daidzein competitively inhibit TPO at nutritional concentrations. In iodine-deficient individuals or those on thyroid replacement therapy, this inhibition can impair thyroid hormone synthesis and reduce circulating T3/T4.
condition_rules.action: Use caution in patients with hypothyroidism or those on levothyroxine. Separate red clover isoflavone supplements from thyroid medications by at least 4 hours. Monitor thyroid function (TSH) if initiating high-dose red clover supplements.
condition_rules.alert_body: Red clover can influence thyroid hormone levels. If you have a thyroid condition, talk to your endocrinologist before adding red clover and monitor your labs.
condition_rules.informational_note: Red clover affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people with thyroid conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Red clover's naturally occurring coumarin constituents (coumestrol) share structural similarity with vitamin K antagonists. While clinical evidence of warfarin potentiation is limited to case-level data, the herb is listed in systematic reviews of plants with anticoagulant potential. Additive effect with warfarin, heparins, or direct oral anticoagulants cannot be excluded.
drug_class_rules.action: Inform prescriber of red clover supplement use while on anticoagulant therapy. Consider periodic INR monitoring in warfarin users taking red clover supplements.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Red clover has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding red clover and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Red clover can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Red clover isoflavones are phytoestrogens with ERα/ERβ agonist activity. Estrogenic stimulation during organogenesis and fetal development is a recognized safety concern. Animal studies demonstrate adverse reproductive outcomes with high phytoestrogen exposure. Clinical safety data in human pregnancy are absent.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Avoid red clover isoflavone supplements during pregnancy and lactation. Occasional culinary intake (e.g., sprouts) is not subject to this restriction.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_headline: Not recommended in pregnancy or breastfeeding
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Red clover contains phytoestrogens with estrogen-receptor activity. If you are pregnant or breastfeeding, do not use red clover isoflavone supplements — culinary sprouts are not subject to this.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Red clover contains phytoestrogens — relevant to anyone pregnant, planning pregnancy, or breastfeeding.

condition_rules.mechanism: Chondroitin sulfate is a glycosaminoglycan structurally similar to heparin. In vitro data suggest weak anticoagulant activity at high concentrations. This property is pharmacologically plausible but has not produced clinically documented bleeding events as a standalone effect. Patients with baseline bleeding disorders represent a population where even weak anticoagulant activity warrants monitoring.
condition_rules.action: Monitor for signs of increased bleeding in patients with bleeding disorders taking chondroitin supplements. No dose adjustment required in the absence of anticoagulant co-medication.
condition_rules.alert_body: Chondroitin has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding chondroitin.
condition_rules.informational_note: Chondroitin can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

condition_rules.mechanism: Chondroitin sulfate's structural heparin-like properties and documented case reports of INR elevation with warfarin raise concern for perioperative bleeding risk. Standard pre-surgical supplement cessation guidance recommends discontinuing chondroitin approximately 2 weeks before elective surgery, consistent with practice for other supplements with anticoagulant potential.
condition_rules.action: Discontinue chondroitin supplements at least 2 weeks before elective surgery. Inform surgeon and anesthesiologist of recent supplement use.
condition_rules.alert_body: Chondroitin can affect bleeding around procedures. If you have surgery scheduled, talk to your surgeon about pausing chondroitin about two weeks before the procedure.
condition_rules.informational_note: Chondroitin can affect perioperative bleeding — relevant to anyone preparing for surgery.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Chondroitin sulfate shares structural similarities with heparin (both are sulfated glycosaminoglycans), and case reports have documented clinically significant INR elevation in warfarin-treated patients taking chondroitin-glucosamine combination products. The exact mechanism may involve inhibition of vitamin K-dependent clotting factors or direct potentiation of antithrombin III activity. One case report documented INR increase from 2.6 to 4.1 associated with glucosamine-chondroitin supplementation.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor INR in warfarin-treated patients who initiate chondroitin supplements. Alert prescriber to supplement use. Consider avoiding chondroitin if INR is already at the upper therapeutic range.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Chondroitin has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding chondroitin and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Chondroitin can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Guarana (Paullinia cupana) seeds contain 2-4.5% caffeine by dry weight — substantially higher than coffee beans (~1-2%). Caffeine causes acute blood pressure elevation (3-15 mmHg systolic) via adenosine receptor antagonism and sympathoadrenal activation. Guarana additionally contains theobromine and theophylline, which contribute to cardiovascular stimulation. Regular high-dose guarana supplementation can impair blood pressure control in hypertensive patients.
condition_rules.action: Limit guarana-containing supplements in hypertension. Monitor BP when initiating guarana supplementation. Account for total daily caffeine load from all sources.
condition_rules.alert_body: Guarana can influence blood pressure. If you have high blood pressure, talk to your clinician before adding guarana and monitor your readings.
condition_rules.informational_note: Guarana can affect blood pressure — relevant to people with high blood pressure.

condition_rules.mechanism: Guarana's high caffeine content (plus theobromine and theophylline) produces sympathomimetic cardiac stimulation: increased heart rate, elevated cardiac output, and potential for supraventricular and ventricular arrhythmias. In patients with pre-existing structural heart disease, heart failure, or arrhythmia history, these effects may destabilize cardiac rhythm or precipitate ischemia.
condition_rules.action: Avoid high-dose guarana supplements in patients with arrhythmia, ischemic heart disease, or heart failure. If caffeine-containing products are used, limit total daily caffeine to <200 mg/day.
condition_rules.alert_body: Guarana can affect heart rate or rhythm in some people. If you have heart disease, talk to your cardiologist before adding guarana.
condition_rules.informational_note: Guarana can affect cardiovascular pathways — relevant to people with heart conditions.

condition_rules.mechanism: High-dose caffeine (the primary active compound in guarana) lowers the seizure threshold by antagonizing adenosine receptors, which normally exert inhibitory control over neuronal excitability. Case reports document caffeine-triggered seizures in susceptible individuals, and animal studies confirm proconvulsant effects at high doses. Standard-dose guarana supplements provide caffeine equivalent to 1-4 cups of coffee.
condition_rules.action: Use caution with guarana in patients with seizure disorders or epilepsy. Limit total daily caffeine from all sources. Inform neurologist of guarana supplement use. Avoid energy drink and pre-workout products containing guarana.
condition_rules.alert_body: Guarana may affect seizure threshold in susceptible people. If you have a seizure disorder, talk to your neurologist before adding guarana.
condition_rules.informational_note: Guarana may affect seizure threshold — relevant to people with a seizure disorder.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Guarana's high caffeine content opposes blood pressure reduction achieved by antihypertensive medications through persistent sympathoadrenal stimulation and adenosine receptor antagonism. Chronic high-dose guarana use may make BP targets harder to achieve.
drug_class_rules.action: Limit guarana supplement use in patients on antihypertensive medications. Monitor blood pressure trends. Account for total caffeine burden including dietary sources.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Guarana can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding guarana.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Guarana can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Guarana's CNS stimulant effects (primarily via caffeine) pharmacodynamically oppose the action of sedative medications (benzodiazepines, barbiturates, non-benzodiazepine sleep aids, antihistamines with sedative properties). Combined use may impair the therapeutic efficacy of sedatives or, in opposite sequence, cause rebound stimulation that disrupts sleep architecture.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid combining guarana supplements with sedative medications. If sedatives are prescribed for a medical reason, guarana and other high-caffeine supplements should be discontinued.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Guarana has mild sedative effects. If you take a sedative or sleep medication, talk to your prescriber before adding guarana — the combination can increase drowsiness.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Guarana has sedative activity — relevant to people on sedatives or sleep medications.

dose_thresholds.note: Total caffeine from all sources (guarana, coffee, tea, other supplements) should be summed. 1g guarana seed extract ≈ 40-80 mg caffeine.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Guarana's caffeine content carries the same pregnancy risk as dietary caffeine. Caffeine crosses the placenta; fetal caffeine clearance is slow due to immature cytochrome P450 1A2. Total caffeine should be kept below 200 mg/day in pregnancy.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Total caffeine from all sources (including guarana) must be summed against the 200 mg/day pregnancy limit.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_headline: Guarana caffeine counts toward daily total
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Guarana contains caffeine that adds to your total daily intake. If you are pregnant or nursing, sum caffeine from all sources (including guarana) against a 200 mg/day ceiling.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Guarana caffeine adds to the daily total — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

condition_rules.mechanism: Copper homeostasis depends on hepatic biliary excretion as the primary elimination route. In Wilson's disease, ATP7B mutations block copper export into bile, causing pathological hepatic accumulation leading to cirrhosis. In non-Wilson's liver disease, impaired biliary function reduces copper clearance, and supplemental copper can accumulate to hepatotoxic levels even at low doses (>2 mg/day).
condition_rules.action: Avoid copper supplementation above 2 mg/day in liver disease. In Wilson's disease, supplemental copper is contraindicated. Consult hepatologist before copper supplementation in chronic liver disease.
condition_rules.alert_body: Copper is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of copper.
condition_rules.informational_note: Copper depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: No severe pregnancy-specific evidence at RDA-range doses; talk to your clinician before use, especially at higher doses.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: At RDA-range doses, this nutrient has no specific pregnancy or lactation concerns. Higher therapeutic doses may have a different safety profile — talk to your clinician.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: RDA-range standard nutrient — generally compatible with pregnancy and lactation; high-dose forms have separate evidence bases.

condition_rules.mechanism: Manganese is excreted almost exclusively via bile. Liver disease impairs biliary excretion, leading to systemic accumulation. Excess manganese deposits in the basal ganglia (globus pallidus), producing manganism — a Parkinson-like neurotoxic syndrome with rigidity, bradykinesia, and psychiatric symptoms. MRI T1 hyperintensity in liver disease patients with elevated manganese is well-documented.
condition_rules.action: Avoid manganese supplementation above 5 mg/day in liver disease. In advanced cirrhosis or liver failure, avoid supplemental manganese entirely. Evaluate manganese status if neurological symptoms develop.
condition_rules.alert_body: Manganese is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of manganese.
condition_rules.informational_note: Manganese depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: No severe pregnancy-specific evidence at RDA-range doses; talk to your clinician before use, especially at higher doses.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: At RDA-range doses, this nutrient has no specific pregnancy or lactation concerns. Higher therapeutic doses may have a different safety profile — talk to your clinician.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: RDA-range standard nutrient — generally compatible with pregnancy and lactation; high-dose forms have separate evidence bases.

condition_rules.mechanism: 5-HTP is a direct precursor to serotonin and has serotonergic pharmacology. Human pregnancy safety data are absent, so use is precautionary rather than evidence-driven; conservative practice is to avoid until safety is established.
condition_rules.action: Avoid 5-HTP during pregnancy unless your obstetric clinician specifically recommends it. Use is precautionary because human safety data are lacking.
condition_rules.alert_body: 5-HTP raises serotonin and has no human pregnancy safety data. If you are pregnant, talk to your obstetrician before any 5-HTP use.
condition_rules.informational_note: 5-HTP raises serotonin — relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: 5-HTP increases presynaptic serotonin synthesis while SSRIs/SNRIs block serotonin reuptake at SERT. The combination raises synaptic serotonin to levels associated with serotonin syndrome (hyperthermia, clonus, agitation, autonomic instability). Case reports of serotonin syndrome with this combination exist.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not combine with SSRIs or SNRIs. If transitioning off an antidepressant, observe a washout period before initiating 5-HTP. Seek prescriber guidance.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: Do not combine with SSRIs or SNRIs
drug_class_rules.alert_body: 5-HTP raises serotonin directly. If you take an SSRI or SNRI antidepressant, do not combine — the pairing can cause serotonin syndrome.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: 5-HTP raises serotonin — relevant to people on SSRI or SNRI antidepressants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Serotonin precursor combined with MAOI inhibition → serotonin syndrome.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not combine 5-HTP with MAOIs. Allow appropriate washout between MAOI discontinuation and 5-HTP use.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: If you take MAO inhibitors, do not combine with this product. 5-HTP raises serotonin levels and can cause serotonin syndrome when combined with MAO-inhibitor antidepressants.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: 5-HTP increases serotonin — relevant to anyone on MAOIs, SSRIs, SNRIs, or other serotonergic drugs.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Sennosides stimulate myenteric plexus nerve endings and increase colonic peristalsis via prostaglandin release. Chronic or high-dose use may trigger uterine smooth muscle contractions; limited human data show occasional association with preterm labor at high doses.
condition_rules.action: Avoid prolonged or high-dose use during pregnancy. Osmotic laxatives are preferred. Consult an obstetrician for any laxative use beyond occasional short-term relief.
condition_rules.alert_body: Senna has limited pregnancy safety data. If you are pregnant, talk to your obstetrician before adding senna to your supplement routine.
condition_rules.informational_note: Senna has limited pregnancy data — relevant to people who are pregnant or planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Chronic anthraquinone laxative use can cause electrolyte depletion (hypokalemia, hyponatremia) which is poorly tolerated in chronic kidney disease. Potassium wasting compounds the risk of dangerous hyperkalemia rebound and cardiac arrhythmia in CKD patients on renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system blockers.
condition_rules.action: Use senna only short-term in kidney disease. Monitor serum electrolytes with any repeated use; avoid long-term use without nephrologist oversight.
condition_rules.alert_body: Senna is cleared through the kidneys and may accumulate when kidney function is reduced. If you have kidney disease, talk to your nephrologist about safe intake of senna.
condition_rules.informational_note: Senna depends on kidney clearance — relevant to people with kidney conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Senna-induced hypokalemia potentiates digoxin toxicity by increasing cardiac glycoside binding affinity at the Na+/K+-ATPase pump. Even modest decreases in serum potassium (e.g., from 3.5 to 3.0 mEq/L) can shift digoxin into the toxic range.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid senna with digoxin or other cardiac glycosides. If a laxative is required, use an osmotic agent and monitor serum potassium and digoxin levels.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Senna-induced potassium loss makes digoxin more toxic. If you take digoxin or another cardiac glycoside, talk to your prescriber and discuss a gentler laxative instead.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Senna lowers potassium — relevant to people on digoxin or other cardiac glycosides.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Chasteberry (Vitex agnus-castus) acts on pituitary dopamine D2 receptors to suppress prolactin and modulate LH/FSH secretion. These hormonal effects on the hypothalamic-pituitary axis pose a theoretical risk of disrupting the hormonal environment required for embryo implantation and maintenance of early pregnancy.
condition_rules.action: Discontinue chasteberry upon confirmed pregnancy. Do not use during the first trimester. Inform obstetrician of prior use.
condition_rules.alert_body: Chasteberry influences pituitary hormones and is not recommended in pregnancy. If pregnancy is confirmed, talk to your obstetrician about discontinuing.
condition_rules.informational_note: Chasteberry affects pituitary hormones — relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Chasteberry has been studied for luteal phase defect and hyperprolactinemia-related infertility, showing possible benefit in some trials. However, it alters LH pulsatility and progesterone levels, which could be counterproductive if the underlying cause of infertility differs. Timing discontinuation at confirmed conception is critical as the hormonal window for implantation is sensitive.
condition_rules.action: If using chasteberry while trying to conceive, plan to discontinue immediately upon positive pregnancy test. Use should be under the guidance of a fertility specialist.
condition_rules.alert_body: Chasteberry has hormonal activity and no established pregnancy safety data. If you are trying to conceive, discuss dosing with your clinician and plan to stop chasteberry at confirmed pregnancy.
condition_rules.informational_note: Chasteberry is typically discontinued at confirmed pregnancy — relevant to people trying to conceive.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: N-acetylcysteine (NAC) inhibits platelet aggregation by reducing disulfide bonds on platelet surface glycoproteins and by increasing nitric oxide bioavailability. At high doses (>1200 mg/day) this antiplatelet effect may be clinically meaningful in patients with underlying coagulopathy.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in bleeding disorders. Avoid high doses (>1200 mg/day) without haematology oversight. Inform prescribers before surgical procedures.
condition_rules.alert_body: NAC has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding NAC.
condition_rules.informational_note: NAC can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

condition_rules.mechanism: NAC is used clinically, but high-dose chronic supplement use in pregnancy should still be supervised.
condition_rules.action: Monitor and keep use indication-driven.
condition_rules.alert_body: NAC is used clinically, but long-term high-dose supplement use in pregnancy should still be supervised. If you are pregnant, keep use indication-driven and discuss it with your clinician.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: NAC reduces disulfide bridges on coagulation factors and on platelet membrane glycoproteins, and it scavenges reactive oxygen species that normally promote pro-coagulant states. Combined with warfarin, case reports document enhanced anticoagulant effect and elevated INR.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor INR more frequently if starting or stopping NAC during anticoagulant therapy. Alert prescriber to any INR changes.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: NAC has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding NAC and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: NAC can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

dose_thresholds.note: Disulfide-bond reduction on platelet glycoproteins becomes clinically meaningful above 1200 mg/day per authored action.
High-dose NAC (>1.2g/day) may influence platelet aggregation. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22467323/

pregnancy_lactation.notes: No severe pregnancy-specific evidence at RDA-range doses; talk to your clinician before use, especially at higher doses.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: At RDA-range doses, this nutrient has no specific pregnancy or lactation concerns. Higher therapeutic doses may have a different safety profile — talk to your clinician.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: RDA-range standard nutrient — generally compatible with pregnancy and lactation; high-dose forms have separate evidence bases.

condition_rules.mechanism: Raw or non-PA-free butterbur (Petasites hybridus) contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids (senkirkine, senecionine) that are hepatotoxic and genotoxic — fetal exposure is unacceptable. Most commercial butterbur products are not certified PA-free, so the defensive default is contraindication. Even certified PA-free extracts lack human pregnancy safety data and remain not recommended.
condition_rules.action: Do not use any butterbur product during pregnancy unless it is certified PA-free AND your obstetric clinician specifically approves. The default assumption for unverified products is contraindication.
condition_rules.alert_body: If you are pregnant, do not use butterbur. Most products are not certified PA-free and contain liver-toxic alkaloids; PA-free extracts also lack pregnancy data. Talk to your obstetrician.
condition_rules.informational_note: Butterbur PA content makes it unsafe in pregnancy — relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Pyrrolizidine alkaloids in non-PA-free butterbur are direct hepatotoxins that cause sinusoidal obstruction syndrome (veno-occlusive disease). Even PA-certified-free products carry a hepatotoxicity risk signal; multiple case reports of liver injury from butterbur supplements exist.
condition_rules.action: Avoid butterbur in any pre-existing liver disease. If considering butterbur for migraine, use only a certified PA-free product and only under hepatologist guidance. Monitor LFTs.
condition_rules.alert_body: Butterbur alkaloids are direct liver toxins. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician; even PA-free products carry some liver-injury risk.
condition_rules.informational_note: Butterbur has liver safety signals — relevant to people with liver conditions.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Cascara sagrada contains anthraquinone glycosides (cascarosides A-D) that stimulate colonic smooth muscle via prostaglandin and nitric oxide pathways. Anthraquinones pass into breast milk and are detectable in maternal plasma; their stimulant effects on smooth muscle may extend to uterine tissue.
condition_rules.action: Avoid during pregnancy. Select a safer laxative such as polyethylene glycol or psyllium and consult an obstetrician.
condition_rules.alert_body: Cascara sagrada stimulates colonic muscle and passes into breast milk. If you are pregnant, talk to your obstetrician about a gentler laxative instead.
condition_rules.informational_note: Cascara sagrada is a stimulant laxative — relevant to anyone pregnant.

condition_rules.mechanism: Chronic use of anthraquinone laxatives including cascara sagrada has been associated with hepatotoxicity; the FDA withdrew OTC cascara sagrada products in 2002 partly on safety grounds. Emodins and aloe-emodins in cascara have demonstrated hepatotoxic effects in rodent models and isolated human hepatocytes.
condition_rules.action: Do not use cascara sagrada in liver disease. Use an osmotic laxative instead.
condition_rules.alert_body: Long-term cascara use has been linked to liver injury, and FDA withdrew it from OTC monographs in 2002. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about alternatives.
condition_rules.informational_note: Cascara sagrada has liver safety signals relevant to people with liver conditions.

condition_rules.mechanism: Anthraquinone-induced electrolyte depletion (hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia) is amplified in CKD where electrolyte homeostasis is already impaired. Hypokalaemia exacerbates the cardiovascular risk profile in dialysis patients and in those on renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system inhibitors.
condition_rules.action: Avoid prolonged use in kidney disease. If a laxative is necessary, choose an osmotic agent. Monitor electrolytes with any use.
condition_rules.alert_body: Cascara sagrada is cleared through the kidneys and may accumulate when kidney function is reduced. If you have kidney disease, talk to your nephrologist about safe intake of cascara sagrada.
condition_rules.informational_note: Cascara sagrada depends on kidney clearance — relevant to people with kidney conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Anthraquinone-induced hypokalemia increases myocardial sensitivity to digoxin and other cardiac glycosides by enhancing Na+/K+-ATPase inhibition. Potassium depletion lowers the toxic threshold for digoxin significantly.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid cascara sagrada with cardiac glycosides. If constipation management is required, select a potassium-sparing approach and monitor serum potassium and digoxin levels.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Cascara-induced potassium loss makes digoxin more toxic. If you take digoxin or another cardiac glycoside, talk to your prescriber about a gentler laxative.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Cascara lowers potassium — relevant to people on digoxin or other cardiac glycosides.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Chinese skullcap (Scutellaria baicalensis) contains baicalin and baicalein which inhibit CYP1A2 and CYP2C9. Animal data suggest possible teratogenic risk at high doses; human safety data in pregnancy are absent.
condition_rules.action: Avoid during pregnancy due to absent safety data. Inform obstetrician of any prior use.
condition_rules.alert_body: Chinese skullcap lacks human pregnancy safety data and animal studies suggest teratogenic signals. If you are pregnant, talk to your obstetrician before any use.
condition_rules.informational_note: Chinese skullcap lacks pregnancy safety data — relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Scutellaria species have been linked to herb-induced liver injury (HILI) in published case series. Proposed mechanisms include flavone-mediated mitochondrial dysfunction and idiosyncratic immune-mediated hepatocyte damage. Chinese skullcap preparations adulterated with Teucrium species carry additional germander-related hepatotoxicity risk.
condition_rules.action: Avoid in active liver disease. If use is considered, verify product is authenticated Scutellaria baicalensis (not Teucrium), monitor LFTs monthly, and discontinue immediately upon any elevation.
condition_rules.alert_body: Chinese skullcap is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of chinese skullcap.
condition_rules.informational_note: Chinese skullcap depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Thymoquinone (the primary active constituent of black seed oil/Nigella sativa) stimulates insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells and increases peripheral glucose uptake via PPAR-gamma activation. Multiple small RCTs demonstrate fasting glucose reductions of 15-20 mg/dL, creating additive hypoglycemia risk with antidiabetic agents.
condition_rules.action: Monitor blood glucose closely when starting or stopping black seed oil in patients on diabetes medications. Warn of additive hypoglycemia risk.
condition_rules.alert_body: Black seed oil can lower blood sugar. If you have diabetes, talk to your clinician before adding black seed oil and monitor your glucose, especially in the first weeks.
condition_rules.informational_note: Black seed oil affects blood sugar — relevant to people with diabetes.

condition_rules.mechanism: Thymoquinone inhibits voltage-gated calcium channels and increases endothelial nitric oxide synthase (eNOS) activity, reducing peripheral vascular resistance. Reductions of 5-10 mmHg systolic have been reported in small trials, creating additive hypotension risk with antihypertensive therapy.
condition_rules.action: Monitor blood pressure when combining with antihypertensive medications. Alert prescriber to use.
condition_rules.alert_body: Black seed oil can influence blood pressure. If you have high blood pressure, talk to your clinician before adding black seed oil and monitor your readings.
condition_rules.informational_note: Black seed oil can affect blood pressure — relevant to people with high blood pressure.

condition_rules.mechanism: Black seed oil (Nigella sativa) has limited human pregnancy safety data, and preclinical (animal) studies have signaled uterine-effect activity at high doses. Human pregnancy effects are not established; conservative practice is to avoid until data exist.
condition_rules.action: Avoid black seed oil during pregnancy unless your obstetric clinician specifically recommends it. Use is precautionary because human pregnancy data are lacking.
condition_rules.alert_body: Black seed oil has limited pregnancy safety data and animal studies have flagged uterine-effect signals. If you are pregnant, talk to your obstetrician before use.
condition_rules.informational_note: Black seed oil pregnancy data are limited — relevant to anyone pregnant.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Thymoquinone inhibits thromboxane B2 synthesis and platelet aggregation, and in vitro data suggest weak inhibition of CYP2C9 which is the primary metabolic pathway for warfarin S-enantiomer. A combined antiplatelet and potential pharmacokinetic interaction may increase bleeding risk.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor INR if combining black seed oil with warfarin or other anticoagulants. Alert prescriber to use.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Black seed oil has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding black seed oil and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Black seed oil can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive glucose-lowering via PPAR-gamma activation and beta-cell stimulation. Risk of symptomatic hypoglycemia particularly with sulfonylureas and insulin.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor blood glucose more frequently. Avoid unsupervised combinations with insulin or sulfonylureas.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Black seed oil can lower blood sugar. If you take a diabetes medication, talk to your prescriber before adding black seed oil and monitor your glucose more closely at first.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Black seed oil affects blood sugar — relevant to people on diabetes medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive blood pressure-lowering via calcium channel inhibition and increased nitric oxide production may cause symptomatic hypotension, particularly with calcium channel blockers and ACE inhibitors.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor blood pressure. Alert prescriber to black seed oil use if on antihypertensive therapy.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Black seed oil can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding black seed oil.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Black seed oil can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: DHA (docosahexaenoic acid) is incorporated into platelet phospholipid membranes, displacing arachidonic acid and reducing thromboxane A2 (TXA2) synthesis. TXA2 is the primary platelet aggregation amplifier; reduced TXA2 production measurably prolongs bleeding time at doses above 2 g/day.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in bleeding disorders. Limit DHA to doses under 2 g/day unless under physician oversight. Inform surgeon of use before any procedure.
condition_rules.alert_body: DHA has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding DHA.
condition_rules.informational_note: DHA can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: DHA reduces platelet TXA2 synthesis and may weakly inhibit vitamin K-dependent coagulation factor synthesis. Combined with warfarin, a small but clinically relevant INR elevation has been observed in controlled studies.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor INR when adding or removing high-dose DHA (>2 g/day) in patients on warfarin. Alert prescriber to use.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: DHA has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding DHA and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: DHA can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive antiplatelet effect via independent mechanisms: clopidogrel blocks P2Y12 receptor while DHA reduces TXA2 production. The combination further impairs platelet aggregation beyond either agent alone.
drug_class_rules.action: Advise prescriber of DHA use when on antiplatelet therapy. Avoid high-dose supplements (>3 g/day) without haematology guidance.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: DHA has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding DHA.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: DHA can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

dose_thresholds.note: TXA2 suppression measurably prolongs bleeding time above 2 g/day per authored mechanism.
INR monitoring warranted above 2 g/day per authored action.
Authored avoid-threshold for combined antiplatelet therapy without haematology guidance.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: EPA (eicosapentaenoic acid) competitively inhibits arachidonic acid conversion to thromboxane A2 via cyclooxygenase, and generates thromboxane A3 (a much weaker platelet aggregator) instead. This antiplatelet mechanism prolongs bleeding time, particularly at doses above 2 g/day.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in bleeding disorders. Limit EPA intake to under 2 g/day without physician oversight. Disclose use to any treating surgeon.
condition_rules.alert_body: EPA has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding EPA.
condition_rules.informational_note: EPA can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: EPA reduces platelet TXA2 synthesis and may affect the vitamin K-dependent coagulation cascade. Clinical studies with high-dose fish oil (containing EPA) and warfarin show small but consistent INR elevations.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor INR when starting or stopping high-dose EPA (>2 g/day) in patients on warfarin or other anticoagulants.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: EPA has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding EPA and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: EPA can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive antiplatelet effect: EPA reduces TXA2 while clopidogrel blocks P2Y12 receptor, producing complementary inhibition of platelet aggregation pathways. Combined bleeding risk is greater than with either agent alone.
drug_class_rules.action: Inform prescriber of EPA supplement use when on antiplatelet therapy. Avoid doses >3 g/day without haematological oversight.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: EPA has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding EPA.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: EPA can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

dose_thresholds.note: TXA2→TXA3 substrate shift produces measurable bleeding-time prolongation above 2 g/day per authored mechanism.
INR monitoring warranted above 2 g/day per authored action.
Authored avoid-threshold without haematological oversight.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: L-theanine stimulates alpha-wave brain activity and attenuates sympathetic nervous system activation, which has been associated with modest blood pressure reductions (approximately 5-8 mmHg systolic) in small RCTs. Additive hypotensive effects are possible, particularly with alpha-blocker and calcium channel blocker antihypertensives.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor blood pressure when combining L-theanine with antihypertensive medications, particularly in patients with borderline-low blood pressure.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: L-theanine can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding L-theanine.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: L-theanine can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: L-theanine increases GABA, glycine, and serotonin levels in the brain and modulates AMPA receptor activity, producing anxiolytic and sedative-like effects. These CNS depressant effects may be additive with benzodiazepines, non-benzodiazepine hypnotics (z-drugs), and barbiturates.
drug_class_rules.action: Advise patients on sedative-hypnotic medications of potential additive sedation. Avoid high doses of L-theanine if operating machinery or driving.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: L-theanine has mild sedative effects. If you take a sedative or sleep medication, talk to your prescriber before adding L-theanine — the combination can increase drowsiness.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: L-theanine has sedative activity — relevant to people on sedatives or sleep medications.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Bacopa monnieri has been shown in animal studies to increase thyroid hormone levels (T4 specifically) via stimulation of thyroid iodide uptake and thyroglobulin synthesis. The clinical relevance in humans is uncertain but persons with hyperthyroidism or Graves disease may be at greater risk.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in thyroid disorders, particularly hyperthyroidism. Monitor thyroid function tests (TSH, free T4) periodically during use.
condition_rules.alert_body: Bacopa can influence thyroid hormone levels. If you have a thyroid condition, talk to your endocrinologist before adding bacopa and monitor your labs.
condition_rules.informational_note: Bacopa affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people with thyroid conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Bacopa may increase endogenous thyroid hormone levels, potentially altering the equilibrium maintained by levothyroxine replacement therapy and requiring dose adjustment.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor TSH levels when initiating or discontinuing Bacopa in patients on thyroid hormone replacement. Alert prescriber to supplement use.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Bacopa can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing bacopa away from your dose.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Bacopa affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Bacopa has adaptogenic effects and modulates serotonergic and GABA-ergic neurotransmission. Animal studies show additive sedative effects when combined with CNS depressants.
drug_class_rules.action: Advise caution with concomitant use of sedative-hypnotic medications. Avoid if operating heavy machinery.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Bacopa has mild sedative effects. If you take a sedative or sleep medication, talk to your prescriber before adding bacopa — the combination can increase drowsiness.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Bacopa has sedative activity — relevant to people on sedatives or sleep medications.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Creatine is metabolised to creatinine and excreted renally. Supplemental creatine reliably elevates serum creatinine, which can spuriously worsen estimated GFR calculations (eGFR via CKD-EPI or MDRD) and complicate monitoring of true kidney function in CKD patients. At high doses, creatine may also increase glomerular filtration pressure.
condition_rules.action: In CKD patients, consult a nephrologist before starting creatine. Clarify with the treating team that serum creatinine elevations may be non-pathological (supplement-related). Avoid creatine loading protocols (>10 g/day) in kidney disease.
condition_rules.alert_body: Creatine is cleared through the kidneys and may accumulate when kidney function is reduced. If you have kidney disease, talk to your nephrologist about safe intake of creatine.
condition_rules.informational_note: Creatine depends on kidney clearance — relevant to people with kidney conditions.

dose_thresholds.note: Loading protocols above 10 g/day avoided in CKD per authored action; below threshold, use still requires nephrology consult.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Dandelion (Taraxacum officinale) acts as a natural diuretic, increasing urine output via inhibition of renal tubular sodium reabsorption. In CKD, fluid management is critical; uncontrolled diuresis can precipitate dehydration, acute kidney injury, and electrolyte derangements (hypokalemia or hyperkalemia depending on residual kidney function).
condition_rules.action: Avoid unsupervised dandelion supplementation in kidney disease. Any diuretic effect should be managed in consultation with a nephrologist.
condition_rules.alert_body: Dandelion is cleared through the kidneys and may accumulate when kidney function is reduced. If you have kidney disease, talk to your nephrologist about safe intake of dandelion.
condition_rules.informational_note: Dandelion depends on kidney clearance — relevant to people with kidney conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Dandelion's diuretic effect is additive with thiazide and loop diuretics used for blood pressure control, potentially causing excessive volume depletion and symptomatic hypotension.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor blood pressure and fluid status when using dandelion with diuretic antihypertensives. Alert prescriber.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Dandelion can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding dandelion.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Dandelion can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Dandelion root extract has demonstrated alpha-glucosidase inhibitory activity and modest insulin-sensitising effects in animal models, producing additive glucose-lowering when combined with antidiabetic medications.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor blood glucose when using dandelion with antidiabetic drugs. Warn of possible additive hypoglycemia, particularly with sulfonylureas.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Dandelion can lower blood sugar. If you take a diabetes medication, talk to your prescriber before adding dandelion and monitor your glucose more closely at first.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Dandelion affects blood sugar — relevant to people on diabetes medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Diuretic effect → potential lithium concentration via fluid/sodium depletion. Same mechanism family as the established lithium-thiazide warning. Common in 'detox' and 'liver support' supplements.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take lithium, talk to your prescriber before using dandelion extract. Watch for signs of lithium toxicity (tremor, confusion, GI upset).
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Dandelion is a mild diuretic. Like prescription diuretics, it can raise lithium levels. Common in 'detox' and 'liver support' supplements.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Dandelion's diuretic effect can raise lithium — relevant to anyone on lithium therapy.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: L-carnitine (including the acetylated form ALCAR) has been shown in a placebo-controlled trial to act as a peripheral antagonist of thyroid hormone action by preventing thyroid hormone nuclear translocation. It may thus reduce the effectiveness of exogenous thyroid hormone therapy and potentially worsen hypothyroidism at high doses.
condition_rules.action: Monitor TSH in patients with hypothyroidism taking ALCAR. Alert endocrinologist to supplement use; dose of levothyroxine may need adjustment.
condition_rules.alert_body: Acetyl-L-carnitine can influence thyroid hormone levels. If you have a thyroid condition, talk to your endocrinologist before adding Acetyl-L-carnitine and monitor your labs.
condition_rules.informational_note: Acetyl-L-carnitine affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people with thyroid conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: ALCAR antagonises thyroid hormone nuclear receptor entry, potentially reducing the efficacy of levothyroxine replacement. TSH may rise despite adequate levothyroxine dosing.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor TSH levels when starting or stopping ALCAR in patients on levothyroxine or other thyroid hormone replacement therapies.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Acetyl-L-carnitine can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing Acetyl-L-carnitine away from your dose.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Acetyl-L-carnitine affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Case reports and mechanistic data suggest that L-carnitine may enhance the anticoagulant effect of acenocoumarol and potentially warfarin, possibly through altered vitamin K metabolism or carnitine-mediated changes in fatty acid oxidation that affect coagulation factor synthesis.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor INR when starting or stopping ALCAR in patients on anticoagulants. Alert prescriber to use.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Acetyl-L-carnitine has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding Acetyl-L-carnitine and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Acetyl-L-carnitine can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Limited data — discuss with your healthcare provider before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: White willow bark contains salicin, which is metabolised in the gut and liver to salicylate and salicylic acid — pharmacologically similar to aspirin. Salicylates irreversibly acetylate cyclooxygenase-1 in platelets, blocking thromboxane A2 synthesis and impairing platelet aggregation for the lifespan of the platelet (8-10 days).
condition_rules.action: Avoid in bleeding disorders. Treat similarly to aspirin from a bleeding risk perspective.
condition_rules.alert_body: White willow bark acts like aspirin on platelets. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your clinician before use — it carries the same bleeding risk as aspirin.
condition_rules.informational_note: White willow bark is aspirin-like — relevant to people with bleeding disorders.

condition_rules.mechanism: Salicylates from white willow bark irreversibly inhibit platelet cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1). Because platelet COX-1 inhibition lasts the platelet lifespan (~10 days), stopping willow bark 7-10 days pre-operatively is necessary to restore normal platelet function.
condition_rules.action: Discontinue white willow bark at least 7-10 days before any elective surgical procedure. Inform the surgical and anaesthesia team.
condition_rules.alert_body: White willow bark inhibits platelets for the life of the platelet. If you have surgery scheduled, talk to your surgeon about pausing it 7-10 days beforehand.
condition_rules.informational_note: White willow bark is aspirin-like — relevant to anyone preparing for surgery.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Salicylate from white willow bark inhibits platelet aggregation (antiplatelet) AND displaces warfarin from albumin binding sites, potentially elevating free warfarin plasma concentrations and raising INR. The combination significantly increases haemorrhagic risk.
drug_class_rules.action: Avoid white willow bark with anticoagulants. If analgesic is needed, consult prescriber about alternatives.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: White willow bark adds to warfarin's effect and raises bleeding risk. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber about a different pain option.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: White willow bark is aspirin-like — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive irreversible COX-1 inhibition. White willow bark combined with aspirin or clopidogrel produces dual antiplatelet inhibition beyond standard cardioprotective dosing.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not combine with antiplatelet therapy. Consult prescriber if analgesic is needed.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: Do not combine with antiplatelet medications
drug_class_rules.alert_body: White willow bark adds to aspirin and clopidogrel platelet effects. If you take antiplatelet therapy, do not combine — talk to your prescriber for alternatives.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: White willow bark is aspirin-like — relevant to people on antiplatelet medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Concurrent salicylate from white willow bark and NSAID use creates additive COX-1/COX-2 inhibition, increasing gastrointestinal bleeding risk and nephrotoxicity. Comparable to aspirin-NSAID co-administration.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not combine white willow bark with NSAIDs. Select an alternative analgesic in consultation with prescriber.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: Can add to NSAID side-effect risk
drug_class_rules.alert_body: White willow bark combined with NSAIDs increases GI bleeding and kidney strain. If you take NSAIDs regularly, talk to your prescriber about a safer pain option.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: White willow bark is aspirin-like — relevant to people on NSAIDs.

condition_rules.mechanism: Huperzine A inhibits acetylcholinesterase (AChE), increasing synaptic acetylcholine. Elevated cholinergic tone in the CNS can lower seizure threshold and has been associated with seizure-like activity in overdose scenarios. The clinical significance at standard supplement doses is unclear but warrants caution.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in seizure disorders. Alert neurologist to huperzine A use. Report any changes in seizure frequency.
condition_rules.alert_body: Huperzine A may affect seizure threshold in susceptible people. If you have a seizure disorder, talk to your neurologist before adding Huperzine A.
condition_rules.informational_note: Huperzine A may affect seizure threshold — relevant to people with a seizure disorder.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Huperzine A inhibits acetylcholinesterase, dramatically increasing synaptic acetylcholine. Anticholinergic drugs (oxybutynin, benztropine, scopolamine, tricyclic antidepressants) act by blocking muscarinic acetylcholine receptors. Combining an AChE inhibitor with an anticholinergic creates pharmacological antagonism that can result in cholinergic toxidrome (bronchospasm, bradycardia, excessive secretions, miosis) if the cholinergic effect predominates, or unpredictable effects on both systems.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not combine huperzine A with anticholinergic medications. Alert prescriber to supplement use.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Huperzine A boosts acetylcholine while anticholinergics block it — these effects cancel. If you take an anticholinergic medication, talk to your prescriber before use.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Huperzine A boosts acetylcholine — relevant to people on anticholinergic medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Alpha-GPC (L-alpha glycerylphosphorylcholine) is a choline donor that directly increases brain acetylcholine synthesis and release by providing substrate for choline acetyltransferase. Anticholinergic drugs block muscarinic receptors to reduce cholinergic transmission. Alpha-GPC antagonises this pharmacological effect, potentially reducing the therapeutic efficacy of anticholinergics (e.g., overactive bladder treatment with oxybutynin) or creating unpredictable CNS effects.
drug_class_rules.action: Inform prescriber of alpha-GPC use if taking anticholinergic medications. The therapeutic effect of anticholinergics may be reduced. Monitor for loss of efficacy.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Alpha-GPC can influence brain-signaling balance. If you take an anticholinergic medication, talk to your prescriber before adding Alpha-GPC.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Alpha-GPC affects brain-signaling balance — relevant to people on anticholinergic medications.

condition_rules.mechanism: L-tyrosine is the direct biosynthetic precursor to thyroid hormones (T3 and T4). Thyroglobulin contains tyrosine residues that are iodinated to form monoiodotyrosine and diiodotyrosine, which couple to form T3 and T4. Supplemental tyrosine loading may theoretically increase thyroid hormone synthesis, particularly concerning in hyperthyroidism or Graves disease.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in hyperthyroidism. Monitor thyroid function tests (TSH, free T4) when initiating L-tyrosine supplementation. Alert endocrinologist to use.
condition_rules.alert_body: L-tyrosine can influence thyroid hormone levels. If you have a thyroid condition, talk to your endocrinologist before adding L-tyrosine and monitor your labs.
condition_rules.informational_note: L-tyrosine affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people with thyroid conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: L-tyrosine is converted to tyramine in the gut. Tyramine is normally metabolised by MAO-A in the gut wall and liver (first-pass). MAO inhibitors block this pathway, leading to tyramine accumulation and triggering norepinephrine release from sympathetic nerve terminals — the classic 'cheese reaction' causing hypertensive crisis (severe headache, diaphoresis, hypertension to malignant levels, stroke risk).
drug_class_rules.action: Absolutely contraindicated with MAOIs. A tyramine-restricted diet AND avoidance of tyrosine supplements is mandatory during MAOI therapy.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: L-tyrosine converts to tyramine, which MAOIs prevent the body from breaking down. If you take an MAOI, do not combine — this pairing risks a hypertensive crisis.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: L-tyrosine converts to tyramine — relevant to people on MAOI antidepressants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: L-tyrosine provides substrate for thyroid hormone synthesis. Supplemental loading could theoretically alter thyroid hormone production, affecting the dose balance required for stable levothyroxine replacement.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor TSH levels periodically if taking L-tyrosine with levothyroxine. Alert endocrinologist to supplement use.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: L-tyrosine can interact with thyroid hormone absorption or metabolism. If you take thyroid medication, talk to your endocrinologist about timing L-tyrosine away from your dose.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: L-tyrosine affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people on thyroid medication.

condition_rules.mechanism: Silymarin (milk thistle extract) has hepatoprotective effects via antioxidant activity and stimulation of ribosomal RNA polymerase I. However, silymarin is a potent inhibitor of CYP3A4, CYP2C9, UGT1A1, and P-glycoprotein. In patients with liver disease on polypharmacy, this CYP inhibition can increase plasma concentrations of co-administered hepatically metabolised drugs, paradoxically worsening hepatotoxic drug burden.
condition_rules.action: In liver disease patients on multiple medications, review all concurrent drugs for CYP3A4/2C9 interactions before starting silymarin. Clinically meaningful interactions exist with immunosuppressants (tacrolimus, cyclosporine), statins, and antivirals. Benefit-risk assessment required.
condition_rules.alert_body: Silymarin is processed through the liver and may accumulate when clearance is reduced. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician about safe intake of silymarin.
condition_rules.informational_note: Silymarin depends on liver clearance — relevant to people with liver conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Silymarin inhibits CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein, which are the primary metabolic and transport pathways for tacrolimus and cyclosporine. Inhibition can increase plasma immunosuppressant concentrations by 20-40%, raising toxicity risk (nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity).
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor immunosuppressant trough levels (tacrolimus, cyclosporine) when starting or stopping silymarin. Dose adjustment by transplant team may be required.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Silymarin has mild immune-modulating activity. If you take immunosuppressive medication, talk to your specialist before adding silymarin.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Silymarin affects immune function — relevant to people on immunosuppressants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Silymarin inhibits CYP3A4 and OATP1B1 transport, which are primary pathways for simvastatin, lovastatin, and atorvastatin metabolism. Elevated statin plasma levels increase myopathy and rhabdomyolysis risk.
drug_class_rules.action: Use the lowest effective statin dose when combining with silymarin. Monitor for myalgia, weakness, or elevated CK. Avoid high-dose simvastatin or lovastatin.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Silymarin shares muscle or liver pathways with statins. If you take a statin, talk to your prescriber before adding silymarin and watch for new muscle aches.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Silymarin affects statin pathways — relevant to people on statin therapy.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Forskolin (Coleus forskohlii) directly activates adenylate cyclase, raising intracellular cAMP. In vascular smooth muscle, increased cAMP activates protein kinase A, which phosphorylates myosin light chain kinase and causes vasodilation. This additive blood pressure reduction can cause symptomatic hypotension when combined with antihypertensive agents.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor blood pressure closely when combining forskolin with antihypertensive medications. Alert prescriber to supplement use.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Forskolin can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding forskolin.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Forskolin can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Forskolin has demonstrated platelet aggregation inhibitory effects via cAMP-dependent suppression of platelet activation. This antiplatelet property may add to anticoagulant bleeding risk.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor coagulation parameters when using forskolin with anticoagulants. Inform prescriber of supplement use.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Forskolin has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding forskolin and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Forskolin can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

condition_rules.mechanism: Icariin (the primary active flavonoid in Epimedium/horny goat weed) inhibits phosphodiesterase-5 (PDE5) and to a lesser extent PDE4. PDE5 inhibition causes vasodilation; in patients with cardiovascular disease, particularly those on nitrates, this can precipitate severe hypotension. Icariin also has a weak estrogen receptor agonist activity.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in established heart disease. Do not combine with nitrates (extreme hypotension risk). Alert cardiologist to supplement use.
condition_rules.alert_headline: PDE5-like effect — caution with heart meds
condition_rules.alert_body: Icariin can affect heart rate or rhythm in some people. If you have heart disease, talk to your cardiologist before adding icariin.
condition_rules.informational_note: Icariin can affect cardiovascular pathways — relevant to people with heart conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: PDE5 inhibition by icariin causes smooth muscle relaxation and vasodilation, producing additive hypotension with antihypertensive agents, especially alpha-blockers and nitrates.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor blood pressure if combining horny goat weed with antihypertensives. Do not combine with alpha-blockers or nitrates.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Icariin can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding icariin.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Icariin can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

condition_rules.mechanism: Stinging nettle (Urtica dioica) leaf extract has demonstrated alpha-glucosidase inhibitory activity and increases insulin secretion in animal and small human studies. These mechanisms produce additive glucose-lowering when combined with antidiabetic medications.
condition_rules.action: Monitor blood glucose when using stinging nettle with diabetes medications. Risk of additive hypoglycemia, particularly with insulin and sulfonylureas.
condition_rules.alert_body: Stinging nettle can lower blood sugar. If you have diabetes, talk to your clinician before adding stinging nettle and monitor your glucose, especially in the first weeks.
condition_rules.informational_note: Stinging nettle affects blood sugar — relevant to people with diabetes.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Stinging nettle has diuretic and mild vasodilatory properties in animal models. Combined with antihypertensive medications, additive blood pressure lowering and risk of volume depletion are possible.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor blood pressure and fluid status when combining stinging nettle with antihypertensive or diuretic medications.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Stinging nettle can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding stinging nettle.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Stinging nettle can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Alpha-glucosidase inhibitory and insulin secretagogue properties of nettle extract create additive hypoglycemic risk with antidiabetic drugs, particularly sulfonylureas and insulin.
drug_class_rules.action: Advise patients to monitor blood glucose more frequently. Warn of hypoglycemia symptoms if using nettle alongside antidiabetic medications.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Stinging nettle can lower blood sugar. If you take a diabetes medication, talk to your prescriber before adding stinging nettle and monitor your glucose more closely at first.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Stinging nettle affects blood sugar — relevant to people on diabetes medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Pygeum africanum bark extract contains phytosterols (beta-sitosterol), pentacyclic triterpenes, and ferulic acid esters that inhibit prostaglandin biosynthesis by reducing arachidonic acid release from membrane phospholipids and by directly inhibiting 5-lipoxygenase and COX pathways. Combined with NSAID COX inhibition, there is theoretical additive anti-inflammatory activity and a possible additive risk of gastrointestinal mucosal injury.
drug_class_rules.action: If combining pygeum with NSAIDs, use the lowest NSAID dose for the shortest duration. Take both with food. Monitor for GI symptoms.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: May add to NSAID bleeding risk
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Pygeum has mild antiplatelet activity that can add to NSAID bleeding risk. If you take NSAIDs regularly, talk to your prescriber before adding pygeum.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Pygeum can affect platelet function — relevant to people on NSAIDs.

condition_rules.mechanism: Oleuropein and hydroxytyrosol in olive leaf extract stimulate glucose uptake in muscle cells via GLUT4 translocation (independent of insulin) and inhibit intestinal alpha-glucosidase. RCTs show fasting glucose reductions of 15-25 mg/dL, creating clinically relevant additive hypoglycemia risk with antidiabetic medications.
condition_rules.action: Monitor blood glucose when starting or stopping olive leaf extract in diabetic patients on medication. Warn of additive hypoglycemia risk with insulin and sulfonylureas.
condition_rules.alert_body: Olive leaf can lower blood sugar. If you have diabetes, talk to your clinician before adding olive leaf and monitor your glucose, especially in the first weeks.
condition_rules.informational_note: Olive leaf affects blood sugar — relevant to people with diabetes.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Oleuropein is a calcium channel blocker with endothelium-independent vasodilatory effects and mild ACE-inhibitory activity. A 500 mg/day olive leaf extract RCT showed blood pressure reduction comparable to 25 mg/day captopril. Additive hypotension with antihypertensive drugs is a real risk.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor blood pressure when combining olive leaf extract with antihypertensive medications. Alert prescriber; dose adjustment of antihypertensives may be needed.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Olive leaf can influence blood pressure and may add to or counter antihypertensive therapy. If you take blood-pressure medication, talk to your prescriber before adding olive leaf.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Olive leaf can affect blood pressure — relevant to people on antihypertensive medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: GLUT4 translocation stimulation and alpha-glucosidase inhibition produce additive blood glucose lowering with antidiabetic agents. Risk of hypoglycemia is greatest with insulin, sulfonylureas, and meglitinides.
drug_class_rules.action: Increase blood glucose monitoring frequency. Advise patients to recognise hypoglycemia symptoms.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Olive leaf can lower blood sugar. If you take a diabetes medication, talk to your prescriber before adding olive leaf and monitor your glucose more closely at first.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Olive leaf affects blood sugar — relevant to people on diabetes medications.

dose_thresholds.note: 500 mg/day olive leaf extract is the clinically-active RCT anchor for BP reduction comparable to 25 mg/day captopril and the fasting glucose reductions of 15–25 mg/dL. Below 500 mg, additive effects on BP/glucose are not consistently documented. Clinical-team signoff 2026-04-24: use >= to capture the validated trial dose cleanly.
