condition_rules.mechanism: Cordyceps (Cordyceps sinensis/militaris) contains cordycepin (3-deoxyadenosine) and beta-glucans that stimulate NK cell activity, macrophage activation, and T-helper cell proliferation via Toll-like receptor and NFkappaB pathways. This immune potentiation may exacerbate autoimmune conditions (e.g., rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, multiple sclerosis, inflammatory bowel disease).
condition_rules.action: Avoid unsupervised use in autoimmune conditions. Consult a rheumatologist or immunologist before starting cordyceps.
condition_rules.alert_body: Cordyceps has mild immune-modulating activity. If you have an autoimmune condition, talk to your specialist before adding cordyceps.
condition_rules.informational_note: Cordyceps affects immune function — relevant to people with autoimmune conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Cordyceps immune-stimulating effects (NK cell and T-cell activation via NFkappaB pathway) directly oppose the mechanism of immunosuppressants such as tacrolimus, cyclosporine, mycophenolate, and corticosteroids, potentially precipitating organ rejection in transplant patients.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not use cordyceps if on immunosuppressant therapy. Alert transplant team or rheumatologist to any supplement use.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Cordyceps stimulates NK and T-cell activity, working against immunosuppressants. If you take transplant or autoimmune medications, do not combine.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Cordyceps is immune-stimulating — relevant to people on immunosuppressant medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Cordyceps has demonstrated in vitro platelet aggregation inhibitory activity and adenosine-mediated vasodilation. Cordycepin (adenosine analogue) may contribute to antiplatelet effects additive with anticoagulant therapy.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor coagulation parameters when combining cordyceps with anticoagulants. Alert prescriber to supplement use.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Cordyceps has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding cordyceps and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Cordyceps can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Lion's mane (Hericium erinaceus) contains hericenones and erinacines that have demonstrated inhibition of ADP-induced and collagen-induced platelet aggregation in in vitro and animal studies, attributed to reduced thromboxane B2 synthesis. Combined with anticoagulant therapy, additive antiplatelet/anticoagulant effects may increase bleeding risk.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor coagulation parameters when combining lion's mane with anticoagulants or antiplatelets. Report any unusual bruising or bleeding. Alert prescriber to supplement use.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Lion's mane has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding lion's mane and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Lion's mane can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Quercetin inhibits thyroid peroxidase (TPO), the enzyme responsible for iodinating tyrosine residues in thyroglobulin to form T3 and T4. At high supplemental doses (>500 mg/day), this TPO inhibition may impair thyroid hormone synthesis, contributing to goitre formation or worsening hypothyroidism, particularly in individuals with borderline iodine intake.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in thyroid disorders, especially hypothyroidism. Limit dose to under 500 mg/day. Monitor TSH periodically and inform endocrinologist of use.
condition_rules.alert_body: Quercetin can influence thyroid hormone levels. If you have a thyroid condition, talk to your endocrinologist before adding quercetin and monitor your labs.
condition_rules.informational_note: Quercetin affects thyroid pathways — relevant to people with thyroid conditions.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Quercetin inhibits CYP2C9 (the primary metabolic enzyme for S-warfarin) and inhibits P-glycoprotein. Both mechanisms reduce warfarin clearance, potentially increasing plasma warfarin concentration and INR. Quercetin also inhibits platelet aggregation independently.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor INR frequently when starting or stopping quercetin supplementation in patients on warfarin. Alert prescriber to dose and any INR changes.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Quercetin has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding quercetin and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Quercetin can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

dose_thresholds.note: Thyroid peroxidase inhibition becomes clinically relevant above 500 mg/day per authored action.

pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: There isn't enough specific safety data to give a confident recommendation for pregnancy or breastfeeding. Talk to your obstetrician or pediatrician before using this supplement.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Pregnancy/lactation safety data is limited — clinician guidance recommended.

condition_rules.mechanism: Resveratrol inhibits thromboxane A2 synthesis by suppressing COX-1/COX-2 activity in platelets and inhibits collagen-induced platelet aggregation via the GPVI signalling pathway. At doses above 150 mg/day, clinically meaningful antiplatelet effects have been documented.
condition_rules.action: Use with caution in bleeding disorders. Limit dose and inform haematologist or treating physician of use, particularly before invasive procedures.
condition_rules.alert_body: Resveratrol has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding resveratrol.
condition_rules.informational_note: Resveratrol can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

condition_rules.mechanism: Limited human pregnancy data; animal work suggests developmental effects at some exposures.
condition_rules.action: Avoid high-dose self-supplementation.
condition_rules.alert_body: Resveratrol has limited human pregnancy data, and higher exposures raise developmental questions in animal studies. If you are pregnant, avoid routine high-dose self-supplementation.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Resveratrol inhibits CYP2C9 (primary metabolism pathway for S-warfarin) and CYP3A4. Inhibition of CYP2C9 reduces warfarin clearance, raising plasma concentrations and INR. Concomitant antiplatelet activity adds further haemostatic impairment.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor INR closely when starting, adjusting, or stopping resveratrol in patients on warfarin. Alert prescriber; warfarin dose may need reduction.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Resveratrol has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding resveratrol and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Resveratrol can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive antiplatelet effect via complementary mechanisms: resveratrol inhibits COX-1/COX-2 and GPVI signalling while aspirin/clopidogrel block COX-1 or P2Y12 pathways, producing greater aggregate inhibition of platelet function than either alone.
drug_class_rules.action: Inform prescriber of resveratrol use when on antiplatelet therapy. Avoid high doses (>250 mg/day) without physician oversight.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Resveratrol has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding resveratrol.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Resveratrol can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

dose_thresholds.note: COX-1/COX-2 and GPVI inhibition produces clinically meaningful antiplatelet effect above 150 mg/day per authored mechanism.
Authored avoid-threshold without physician oversight.

Resveratrol-warfarin interaction evidence is animal-only (mouse model at 0.5% of diet potentiated warfarin; 10x lower dose had no effect). Typical supplement doses are 100-250 mg/day. Threshold raised to >500 mg/day to avoid over-alerting on standard supplements. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27040449/

condition_rules.mechanism: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) prolong bleeding time by inhibiting thromboxane A2-mediated platelet aggregation and reducing platelet-activating factor. FDA drug labels for icosapent ethyl (Vascepa) and omega-3-acid ethyl esters (Lovaza) explicitly warn about increased bleeding risk. Doses >3g/day have potential antithrombotic effects per FDA labeling.
condition_rules.action: Monitor for signs of bleeding. Inform prescriber if taking anticoagulants or antiplatelets. Consider dose reduction if >3g EPA+DHA/day.
condition_rules.alert_body: Fish oil has mild antiplatelet activity. If you have a bleeding disorder, talk to your hematologist before adding fish oil.
condition_rules.informational_note: Fish oil can affect platelet function — relevant to people with a bleeding disorder.

condition_rules.mechanism: Omega-3 fatty acids may increase perioperative bleeding risk due to antiplatelet effects. Multiple FDA drug labels recommend caution with concurrent anticoagulant/antiplatelet therapy.
condition_rules.action: Consider discontinuing omega-3 supplements 7-10 days before planned surgery. Inform surgeon and anesthesiologist.
condition_rules.alert_body: Fish oil can affect bleeding around procedures. If you have surgery scheduled, talk to your surgeon about pausing fish oil about two weeks before the procedure.
condition_rules.informational_note: Fish oil can affect perioperative bleeding — relevant to anyone preparing for surgery.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Omega-3 fatty acids prolong bleeding time and may potentiate anticoagulant effects. FDA labels for icosapent ethyl and omega-3-acid ethyl esters explicitly state: 'omega-3 acids may prolong bleeding time. Monitor patients receiving anticoagulants.'
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor INR/bleeding time more frequently. Watch for signs of bruising or bleeding. Inform prescriber.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Fish oil has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding fish oil and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Fish oil can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Additive antiplatelet effects. Omega-3 fatty acids inhibit platelet aggregation via thromboxane A2 suppression, which may compound the effects of antiplatelet agents like aspirin or clopidogrel.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor for increased bruising or bleeding. Inform prescriber of omega-3 supplementation.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Fish oil has mild antiplatelet activity. If you take an antiplatelet medication like aspirin or clopidogrel, talk to your prescriber before adding fish oil.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Fish oil can affect platelet function — relevant to people on antiplatelets.

dose_thresholds.note: Harmonized with individual DHA/EPA rules per clinical-team signoff 2026-04-24. 2 g/day EPA+DHA measurably prolongs bleeding time via TXA2 suppression.
Harmonized with DHA/EPA individual anticoagulant thresholds per clinical-team signoff 2026-04-24. Previous >3000 mg threshold narrowed to >2000 mg to align with omega-3 bleeding-time data.
Preserves the stricter high-dose warning at 3 g/day for antiplatelet co-therapy per FDA labeling of Vascepa/Lovaza. Clinical-team signoff 2026-04-24.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Omega-3 (particularly DHA) is recommended during pregnancy for fetal brain development. FDA advises keeping total omega-3 intake from supplements and food below 3g/day.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Omega-3 (especially DHA) is recommended during pregnancy for fetal brain development. If you are pregnant or nursing, discuss dosing with your clinician and keep total omega-3 under 3 g per day.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Omega-3 is part of standard prenatal support — relevant to anyone pregnant or breastfeeding.

condition_rules.mechanism: CBD is extensively metabolized by CYP3A4 and CYP2C19 in the liver. FDA drug labels for multiple medications warn that concomitant CBD causes ALT/AST elevation. In patients with pre-existing hepatic impairment, CBD may worsen liver function. The FDA-approved CBD product (Epidiolex) carries a hepatotoxicity warning.
condition_rules.action: Avoid CBD supplements in liver disease. If already taking, monitor liver function tests regularly. Discontinue if ALT/AST exceed 3x upper limit of normal.
condition_rules.alert_body: CBD is heavily metabolized in the liver and can elevate liver enzymes. If you have liver disease, talk to your clinician and monitor liver function labs.
condition_rules.informational_note: CBD is liver-metabolized — relevant to people with liver conditions.

condition_rules.mechanism: CBD has anticonvulsant properties (FDA-approved for Lennox-Gastaut and Dravet syndromes). However, CBD supplements are not pharmaceutical-grade and have variable potency, contaminant profiles, and dosing. Interaction with existing anticonvulsant regimens (especially valproate) may cause hepatotoxicity or alter drug levels.
condition_rules.action: Use only pharmaceutical-grade CBD (Epidiolex) for seizure management. Supplement-grade CBD should not be substituted. Inform neurologist of any CBD use.
condition_rules.alert_body: CBD may affect seizure threshold in susceptible people. If you have a seizure disorder, talk to your neurologist before adding CBD.
condition_rules.informational_note: CBD may affect seizure threshold — relevant to people with a seizure disorder.

condition_rules.mechanism: FDA strongly advises against CBD use during pregnancy. Animal studies show potential risks to the developing fetus. No adequate human safety data exists for CBD during pregnancy.
condition_rules.action: Avoid CBD supplements during pregnancy. Consult healthcare provider.
condition_rules.alert_headline: FDA advises against use in pregnancy
condition_rules.alert_body: FDA strongly advises against CBD use in pregnancy based on animal studies. If you are pregnant, talk to your obstetrician before any CBD product.
condition_rules.informational_note: CBD lacks pregnancy safety data — relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: CBD inhibits CYP2C19 and CYP3A4, increasing plasma levels of clobazam, rufinamide, topiramate, and zonisamide. With valproate specifically, concomitant CBD causes ALT/AST elevation (hepatotoxicity risk). FDA drug labels for divalproex sodium explicitly warn about this interaction.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor liver function tests if CBD used with valproate. Monitor anticonvulsant drug levels. Dose adjustment may be needed.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: CBD can influence how seizure medications work. If you take an anticonvulsant, talk to your neurologist before adding CBD.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: CBD may affect anticonvulsant therapy — relevant to people on seizure medications.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: CBD inhibits CYP3A4, which metabolizes tacrolimus and sirolimus. FDA drug labels for both tacrolimus and sirolimus explicitly warn that CBD co-administration increases their plasma concentrations, risking nephrotoxicity and immunosuppressive toxicity.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor tacrolimus/sirolimus trough levels closely. Dose reduction for the immunosuppressant should be considered. Inform transplant team of CBD use.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: CBD has mild immune-modulating activity. If you take immunosuppressive medication, talk to your specialist before adding CBD.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: CBD affects immune function — relevant to people on immunosuppressants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: CBD inhibits CYP2C9, which metabolizes warfarin. Case reports document elevated INR when CBD is added to warfarin therapy. The interaction may increase bleeding risk.
drug_class_rules.action: Monitor INR more frequently when starting or stopping CBD. Inform prescriber.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: CBD has mild antiplatelet or anticoagulant activity. If you take an anticoagulant, talk to your prescriber before adding CBD and ask about INR or bleeding checks.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: CBD can affect clotting — relevant to people on anticoagulants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: CBD has CNS depressant effects and may potentiate sedation when combined with benzodiazepines, opioids, or other sedatives.
drug_class_rules.action: Use caution with concurrent sedative medications. Monitor for excessive drowsiness.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: CBD has mild sedative effects. If you take a sedative or sleep medication, talk to your prescriber before adding CBD — the combination can increase drowsiness.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: CBD has sedative activity — relevant to people on sedatives or sleep medications.

pregnancy_lactation.notes: FDA strongly advises against CBD during pregnancy and breastfeeding. Animal studies show developmental toxicity. CBD transfers to breast milk.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: FDA strongly advises against CBD use in pregnancy based on animal studies. If you are pregnant, talk to your obstetrician before any CBD product.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: CBD lacks pregnancy safety data — relevant to anyone pregnant or planning pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Pennyroyal contains pulegone and related hepatotoxic constituents; pregnancy use is unsafe because of toxicity and abortifacient concern.
condition_rules.action: Do not use in pregnancy.
condition_rules.alert_body: Pennyroyal contains toxic compounds that can harm the liver and has abortifacient concern. If you are pregnant, do not use it.
condition_rules.informational_note: Pennyroyal contains hepatotoxic constituents and is not safe in pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Synephrine is a sympathomimetic; pregnancy is not the place for stimulant-driven cardiovascular stress.
condition_rules.action: Do not use supplement-dose bitter orange in pregnancy.
condition_rules.alert_body: Supplement-dose bitter orange contains synephrine, a stimulant that can stress the cardiovascular system. If you are pregnant, do not use it.
condition_rules.informational_note: Bitter orange contains synephrine and is not appropriate in pregnancy.

condition_rules.mechanism: Antiplatelet effects make peripartum bleeding the main concern.
condition_rules.action: Avoid chronic use in pregnancy and stop before delivery if used.
condition_rules.alert_body: Ginkgo can have antiplatelet effects, which is a concern near delivery. If you are pregnant, avoid chronic use and stop before birth if your clinician has you taking it.
condition_rules.informational_note: Ginkgo's antiplatelet activity is a peripartum bleeding concern, especially near delivery.

condition_rules.action: Avoid concentrated extracts; culinary use is not the same as supplement use.
condition_rules.alert_body: Holy basil has limited pregnancy data, and animal studies raise endocrine and uterotonic uncertainty. If you are pregnant, avoid concentrated extracts.

condition_rules.action: Monitor if combined with acarbose-like therapy or insulin.
condition_rules.alert_body: White mulberry can slow carbohydrate absorption through alpha-glucosidase inhibition. If you use it with acarbose-like therapy or insulin, monitor for low blood sugar.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: PEA is a direct MAO substrate; combination with MAOIs causes hypertensive crisis.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not combine PEA-containing supplements with MAOIs. Discontinue MAOI for the appropriate washout period before any PEA-containing product.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: If you take MAO inhibitors, do not combine with this product. PEA combined with MAO-inhibitor antidepressants can cause a dangerous spike in blood pressure.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: PEA is an MAO substrate — relevant to anyone taking phenelzine, tranylcypromine, selegiline, or related drugs.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Serotonin precursor combined with MAOI inhibition → serotonin syndrome.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not combine L-tryptophan with MAOIs.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: If you take MAO inhibitors, do not combine with this product. L-tryptophan raises serotonin levels and can cause serotonin syndrome when combined with MAO-inhibitor antidepressants.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: L-tryptophan increases serotonin — relevant to anyone on MAOIs, SSRIs, SNRIs, or other serotonergic drugs.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Hordenine is a β-PEA analog and direct MAO substrate. Often combined with PEA in pre-workout / fat-burner stacks, compounding the risk.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not combine hordenine with MAOIs. Avoid pre-workout and fat-burner products listing hordenine if you take an MAOI.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: If you take MAO inhibitors, do not combine with this product. Hordenine works like PEA and can cause dangerous blood pressure changes with MAO-inhibitor antidepressants.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Hordenine is an MAO substrate — relevant to anyone on MAOIs.

drug_class_rules.action: If you take an MAOI, avoid SAMe unless cleared by your prescriber. Allow washout if switching.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: If you take MAO inhibitors, do not combine with this product. SAMe has antidepressant-like activity. Combined with MAO-inhibitor antidepressants, it can raise the risk of serotonin syndrome.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: SAMe is serotonergic — relevant to anyone on MAOIs or other antidepressants.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: High sodium intake increases lithium clearance. Low sodium increases lithium retention → toxicity risk. Like caffeine, the principle is consistency.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take lithium, keep sodium intake stable. Avoid sudden low-sodium diets or salt-tablet supplements without prescriber approval.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: Big swings in sodium intake change your lithium levels. Talk to your prescriber before starting a low-sodium diet or salt supplement.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Sodium intake affects lithium levels bidirectionally — relevant to anyone on lithium therapy.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Mild fibrinolytic / antiplatelet activity at high dose (≥500 mg/day). Bromelain enhances plasmin generation and modestly inhibits platelet aggregation. Clinical bleeding events with warfarin are rare but documented in case reports.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take warfarin or an antiplatelet, mention bromelain supplements ≥500 mg/day to your prescriber. Watch for unusual bruising or bleeding.
drug_class_rules.alert_headline: Mild bleeding risk at high dose
drug_class_rules.alert_body: High-dose bromelain (500 mg/day or more) has mild blood-thinning activity. Combined with warfarin or antiplatelet drugs, this can slightly raise bleeding risk.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Bromelain has fibrinolytic activity at clinical doses — relevant to anyone on blood thinners.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Tyramine is a sympathomimetic biogenic amine and MAO substrate. With MAO-inhibitor medication, ingested tyramine cannot be metabolized normally, producing massive norepinephrine release and severe hypertensive crisis ('cheese reaction'). Documented fatalities in the clinical literature.
drug_class_rules.action: Do not combine tyramine-rich extracts with MAOIs. Read supplement labels for aged-yeast, fermented bovine, or tyramine-containing protein hydrolysates if you take an MAOI.

drug_class_rules.informational_note: Tyramine is the classic MAOI dietary contraindication — relevant to anyone on phenelzine, tranylcypromine, isocarboxa...

pregnancy_lactation.notes: Concentrated tyramine extracts may elevate sympathetic tone — talk to your clinician before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Concentrated tyramine sources can elevate blood pressure and sympathetic tone. Discuss with your obstetrician or pediatrician before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Tyramine elevates sympathetic tone — pregnancy/lactation guidance recommended.

drug_class_rules.mechanism: Saikosaponins inhibit CYP2D6 in vitro and in animal models. Clinical evidence in humans is limited but consistent in mechanism. Bupleurum often appears in multi-herb TCM formulas (Xiao Yao San, Bupleurum & Dragon Bone) where users may not recognize it on the ingredient label.
drug_class_rules.action: If you take a CYP2D6-substrate prescription drug, consider discussing this supplement with your prescriber. Especially relevant for many SSRIs/SNRIs, tricyclics, codeine, tramadol, and tamoxifen.
drug_class_rules.alert_body: If you take a CYP2D6-substrate prescription drug, consider discussing bupleurum with your prescriber. Bupleurum may slow how some antidepressants and pain medications are processed.
drug_class_rules.informational_note: Bupleurum inhibits CYP2D6 in preclinical evidence — relevant to anyone on antidepressants, opioids, or tamoxifen.

pregnancy_lactation.mechanism: Emmenagogue activity in TCM literature; limited modern safety data in pregnancy. Lactation safety data absent.
pregnancy_lactation.notes: Use caution in pregnancy due to traditional emmenagogue activity. Lactation safety not established — clinician guidance recommended.
pregnancy_lactation.alert_body: Bupleurum has traditional emmenagogue activity and limited safety data in pregnancy. Talk to your obstetrician before use during pregnancy or breastfeeding.
pregnancy_lactation.informational_note: Bupleurum has limited pregnancy safety data — clinician guidance recommended.
